[此时：2026-04-25T21:26:12-07:00 | 上下文：12.8% (系统 25614 + 对话 0)]

You have just been launched into a fresh impersonate-meta network. The human has not provided any subject yet. Your first action is to greet them and gather the structured information you need before any harvesting can begin.

**Before greeting, load the methodology skill.** Call `library` and read `impersonate-meta/SKILL.md` (and the `primers/`, `templates/`, `failure-catalog.md` files alongside it). This skill defines the entire process: 5 patterns you must follow, 7 anti-patterns you must avoid, the VA claim schema, the four-piece profile structure, the close-out checklist. Every phase you run from here on is governed by it. Do not improvise the methodology — it is already written.

Send the human ONE email containing the following content. Adapt the wording to the network's language (wen) and your voice, but keep all the questions and the constraints — they are load-bearing. Do not start any tool work until the human replies.

---

Greet message to send (template):

> Welcome. I'll coordinate an agent network to build a deep, sourced persona skill of whoever you choose to model.
>
> The methodology I follow is `impersonate-meta` (already loaded in my library). It has 5 patterns I follow, 7 anti-patterns I guard against, and a close-out checklist I run before declaring anything done. If you want to read it yourself: `impersonate-meta/SKILL.md` plus the `primers/` (research workflow, online pitfalls, reading the web), `templates/` (VA, VM, profile skeletons), and `failure-catalog.md` (13 real failures from the Marco Velli case study).
>
> Before I dispatch any avatars, I need a few things from you. The more specific the seed, the less likely the harvester is to hallucinate or scrape an unrelated person.
>
> **Required:**
>
> 1. **Full name** — the canonical name they publish under (e.g. "Marco Velli", not "Dr. M. Velli").
> 2. **Domain** — one sentence on who they are and what they're known for.
> 3. **At least one authoritative source link** (in order of preference):
>    - Google Scholar profile URL — best
>    - ORCID
>    - Semantic Scholar profile
>    - University faculty page (last resort)
>
> **Strongly recommended:**
>
> 4. **Disambiguation hints** if the name is common — affiliation, field, career years.
> 5. **Lecture or interview videos** — without verbatim source material I can only describe voice abstractly, not capture it.
> 6. **3–5 core papers** you consider most representative. These anchor the VA argument framework.
>
> **Optional but useful:** Wikipedia page, personal/lab site, press profiles, specific debates or episodes you want captured.
>
> **What I will not do:**
>
> - No private information — only published, public sources.
> - No fabricated quotes or anecdotes — unsourceable claims get marked `[unverified]` or dropped.
> - No biography invention — dates, positions, awards are sourced or flagged.
> - No off-record register — the persona models public voice (lecture, interview, paper), not private conversation.
>
> If you can format like this, it speeds things up:
>
> ```
> Name: <full name>
> Domain: <one sentence>
> Scholar: <url or fallback>
> Faculty page: <url, optional>
> Disambiguation: <affiliation, field, years if needed>
> Videos: <urls, optional>
> Core papers: <3–5 titles or arxiv ids, optional>
> Special focus: <anything you specifically want, optional>
> ```
>
> Reply when ready and I'll start Phase 1 (intelligence gathering).

---

After sending the greet, return to idle. Do not start harvesting, codex submissions, or avatar spawning until the human's reply arrives in your inbox.

If the human replies with insufficient info (no name, no source link), reply once asking for the missing required field. Do not proceed without at least Name + Domain + one Scholar/ORCID/Semantic Scholar/faculty link — the harvester needs a real anchor or it scrapes noise.
Let me first load the impersonate-meta skill and then send the greeting email to the human.

content.content:
1	---
2	name: impersonate-meta
3	description: >-
4	  Reusable methodology for deeply impersonating any academic or public figure
5	  via an agent network. Covers intelligence gathering, avatar orchestration,
6	  skill production, integration, and persona assembly. v2 incorporates lessons
7	  from the full Marco Velli case study (105 VA arguments, 10 VM method cards,
8	  4-profile suit, 338-entry bib, ~90K tokens).
9	version: 2.0
10	---
11	
12	# Impersonation Meta-Skill: Building a Deep Persona via Agent Network
13	
14	> *This skill distills the methodology used to build the complete Marco Velli
15	> persona (solar physicist, UCLA) into a reusable procedure for any academic
16	> or public figure. The Velli case produced a ~90K-token persona with 105
17	> verifiable argument records (VA), 10 method cards (VM), and 19 files across
18	> 6 layers. This skill teaches you how to replicate that process for any
19	> subject.*
20	
21	---
22	
23	## Overview
24	
25	The goal: create a comprehensive, machine-readable persona that an AI agent
26	can inhabit authentically — not just facts, but voice, values, argumentation
27	patterns, and domain expertise. The method has **two phases**:
28	
29	1. **Phase 1: Intelligence Gathering** — collect everything publicly
30	   available and distill into structured knowledge
31	2. **Phase 2: Persona Production** — decompose expertise, build the
32	   four-piece profile, produce domain skills via avatar network, induct
33	   method cards, assemble and validate the complete persona
34	
35	The key insight from the Velli case: **the persona is not a single document.**
36	It is a layered directory with a navigation entry point, a four-piece profile
37	suit, a citation database, and a structured set of verifiable arguments.
38	
39	### The Velli Persona Anatomy (Reference Architecture)
40	
41	This is what a complete persona directory looks like. Your target output
42	follows this structure (substituting your subject):
43	
44	```
45	persona-<subject>/
46	├── SKILL.md                       ← Entry point, navigation, loading guide
47	├── <subject>.bib                  ← BibTeX citation database (pruned)
48	├── <subject>-bib-full.bib         ← Full bib (archived, not shipped)
49	├── scripts/
50	│   └── generate_bib.py            ← BibTeX generation tool
51	├── profile/
52	│   ├── SKILL.md                   ← Profile overview & loading guide
53	│   ├── biography.md               ← Timeline, positions, honors
54	│   ├── voice.md                   ← Speaking style, metaphors, humor
55	│   ├── values.md                  ← Science philosophy, cultural views
56	│   └── relationships.md           ← Mentors, co-authors, students
57	├── arguments/
58	│   ├── SKILL.md                   ← Argument index & search guide
59	│   ├── physics/                   ← Domain-specific arguments (85 VA)
60	│   ├── methodology/               ← Thinking methods (8 VA)
61	│   └── culture/                   ← Scientific culture (7 VA)
62	└── methods/
63	    └── SKILL.md                   ← Method cards (10 VM, cognative fingerprint)
64	```
65	
66	---
67	
68	## Phase 1: Intelligence Gathering
69	
70	### Step 1.1: Bibliography Mining
71	
72	**Target:** Complete publication list with citation data.
73	
74	**Procedure:**
75	1. Find the subject's Google Scholar / Semantic Scholar / ORCID profile
76	2. Scrape all pages (typical: 2-5 pages, 100-300 papers)
77	3. For each paper, extract: title, authors, year, journal, citation count,
78	   URL
79	4. Organize into research themes by co-author clusters and keyword patterns
80	5. Record in `codex` as a structured entry with ~9 research themes, key
81	   papers per theme with citation counts
82	
83	**Tools:** `web_search`, `web_read`, `codex(submit)`
84	
85	### Step 1.2: Biography & Career
86	
87	**Target:** Complete career trajectory, honors, institutional affiliations.
88	
89	**Sources to check:**
90	- University faculty page
91	- AGU/AMS/APS/etc. fellow listings
92	- Award citation text
93	- Wikipedia (if exists)
94	- Interviews and profiles
95	- Mission/team pages (for scientists)
96	
97	**Output:** Codex entry with timeline, positions, honors, key roles — raw
98	material for `profile/biography.md`.
99	
100	### Step 1.3: Voice & Communication Style
101	
102	**Target:** How they speak, write, argue, and frame ideas. This is NOT
103	surface-level — it must capture reasoning patterns.
104	
105	**Best sources:**
106	- YouTube lectures / conference talks (transcribe with `listen`)
107	- Podcasts and interviews
108	- Popular-science writing / press releases
109	- Textbook prefaces (if applicable)
110	
111	**What to capture:**
112	- Metaphor inventory (the specific images they use)
113	- Humor patterns (self-deprecation? irony? cultural references?)
114	- Argumentation structure (how they build a case)
115	- Conversational markers (fill words, rhetorical devices)
116	- Emotional register (what excites/frustrates them)
117	- Philosophy and values (what they say when they reflect)
118	
119	**Citation discipline during this step:** Every claim about the subject's
120	voice must be traceable to a specific source. Use YouTube timestamps.
121	Mark direct quotes `[source: URL @ timestamp]`. Mark unverifiable
122	assertions `⚠️ [unverified]`.
123	
124	**Output:** Raw notes that will become `profile/voice.md`.
125	
126	### Step 1.4: Collaborator Network
127	
128	**Target:** Map the social graph — who they work with, in what capacity.
129	
130	**Procedure:**
131	1. From bibliography, extract co-author frequency
132	2. Identify tiers: core (15+ papers), frequent (5-15), project colleagues
133	3. Note current group members (students, postdocs, research staff)
134	4. Note historical/foundational collaborators (mentors, deceased colleagues)
135	
136	**Output:** Raw notes that will become `profile/relationships.md`.
137	
138	### Source Discipline Rules (Applied Throughout Phase 1)
139	
140	All gathered intelligence must follow these rules:
141	
142	| Category | Rule | Enforcement |
143	|----------|------|-------------|
144	| **Verifiable claims** | Must have a citekey to a published source | Every VA entry checked |
145	| **Observational claims** (video/audio) | Must have `[source: URL @ timestamp]` | Every behavioral observation |
146	| **Unverifiable claims** | Must be marked `⚠️ [unverified]` | Tracked and accounted for |
147	| **Fabricated anecdotes** | NEVER allowed | Immediate rejection |
148	
149	**Footer on every intelligence output:**
150	> *"No fabricated anecdotes. If you cannot trace a claim to a source, mark
151	> it [unverified]. If you are unsure, do not include it."*
152	
153	---
154	
155	## Phase 2: Persona Production
156	
157	Phase 2 produces the complete persona directory. This phase uses an **avatar
158	network** (parallel specialized agents) to decompose expertise, but the final
159	assembly is done by the main agent.
160	
161	### Step 2.1: Decompose Expertise into Skill Domains
162	
163	From the research themes identified in Phase 1, group into **5-8 skill
164	domains**. Each domain becomes one avatar's responsibility.
165	
166	**Criteria for a good skill domain:**
167	- Coherent sub-field (can be explained independently)
168	- Has a distinct literature (5-15 key papers)
169	- Maps to a specific aspect of the persona's expertise
170	- Can be authored as a standalone knowledge base
171	
172	**Example decomposition (Velli case):**
173	
174	| Avatar | Domain | Skill Name |
175	|--------|--------|-----------|
176	| alfven-waves | Core theory thread | alfven-turbulence |
177	| psp-mission | Major project/mission | psp-observations |
178	| switchbacks | Hot discovery topic | switchbacks-theory |
179	| coronal-heating | Classical unsolved problem | coronal-heating |
180	| reconnection | Theoretical breakthrough | ideal-tearing |
181	| velli-voice | Communication style | academic-voice-velli |
182	
183	### Step 2.2: Spawn Avatars with Task Briefs
184	
185	For each skill domain, write a **task brief** (see Appendix B for template)
186	and dispatch via `avatar(spawn)`.
187	
188	**Avatar type:** `shallow` (default) — they don't need your full memory,
189	just the tools and the brief.
190	
191	**Dispatch pattern:** Spawn in batches of 3. Send briefs immediately via
192	email. Don't wait for one batch before spawning the next.
193	
194	### Step 2.3: Collect, Quality-Check, and Integrate Skills
195	
196	As avatars report completion:
197	
198	1. **Verify with explicit commands** — do NOT trust "dispatched" or
199	   "I sent it." Always check:
200	   ```bash
201	   ls -la .library/custom/<skill>/SKILL.md
202	   wc -l .library/custom/<skill>/SKILL.md
203	   grep '^## ' .library/custom/<skill>/SKILL.md
204	   ```
205	2. **Size check:** < 50 lines → incomplete; 50-300 → typical good range;
206	   300+ → thorough (verify not padded)
207	3. **Structure check:** headings must map to the brief's requirements
208	4. **Content spot-check:** read opening and one substantive section
209	5. **Copy** to your own `.library/custom/`:
210	   ```bash
211	   cp -r /path/to/avatar/.library/custom/<skill>/ .library/custom/
212	   ```
213	
214	### Step 2.4: Cross-Validation Review
215	
216	Use a daemon to systematically compare all skills:
217	
218	**Check for:**
219	1. **Overlap zones** — topics covered by multiple skills
220	2. **Factual contradictions** — different claims about the same fact
221	3. **Missing links** — topics that should reference each other but don't
222	4. **Coverage gaps** — important topics not covered by any skill
223	
224	**Output:** Integration review document (see Appendix C for template).
225	
226	### Step 2.5: Build the Four-Piece Profile Suit
227	
228	Each profile piece has its own rubric:
229	
230	#### `profile/biography.md`
231	- Complete timeline (year by year)
232	- Positions held (start date, end date, institution)
233	- Honors and awards (with citation text where available)
234	- Key institutional roles (department chair, mission scientist, etc.)
235	- **Source rule:** Every date must cite a verifiable source
236	
237	#### `profile/voice.md`
238	- Storyteller archetype documentation
239	- Metaphor inventory (with source timestamps)
240	- Humor patterns (with examples)
241	- Argumentation structure (generalized pattern, not one-off observations)
242	- Conversational markers
243	- **Source rule:** Every observation backed by `[source: URL @ timestamp]`
244	- **Footer:** *"No fabricated anecdotes. Sources from public lectures,
245	  interviews, and published writing only."*
246	
247	#### `profile/values.md`
248	- Science philosophy (how they think science should work)
249	- Teaching philosophy (how they teach and mentor)
250	- Attitude toward collaboration, competition, public engagement
251	- Views on specific controversies or debates they've weighed in on
252	- **Source rule:** Claims from interviews/talks preferred over inference
253	
254	#### `profile/relationships.md`
255	- Mentor tree (who mentored them, who they mentored)
256	- Co-author map (core/frequent/project tiers)
257	- Institutional network (where they've worked, with whom)
258	- Students and postdocs (current group, notable alumni)
259	- **Source rule:** Each relationship must cite a specific paper or source
260	
261	### Step 2.6: Build Method Cards as Cognative Fingerprint
262	
263	Method cards are **not** a generic checklist. They emerge from **reverse
264	induction** from the VA arguments. Each card answers: "What recurring method
265	does this person use across multiple domains?"
266	
267	**The 10-card framework (from Velli case):**
268	
269	| VM ID | Card Name | Minimum VA Backing |
270	|-------|-----------|-------------------|
271	| VM001 | Start from the Gradient | ≥3 VA |
272	| VM002 | Find the Critical Point | ≥3 VA |
273	| VM003 | Cross-Scale Connection | ≥3 VA |
274	| VM004 | The Expanding Box Model | ≥3 VA |
275	| VM005 | Theory-Driven, Data-Validated | ≥3 VA |
276	| VM006 | Parameterize Uncertainty | ≥3 VA |
277	| VM007 | Numerical Experiments | ≥3 VA |
278	| VM008 | Comparative Analysis | ≥3 VA |
279	| VM009 | Let Surprise Guide New Theory | ≥3 VA |
280	| VM010 | Multi-Point Synergy | ≥3 VA |
281	
282	**Each card must contain:**
283	
284	```
285	## VM00X: Card Name
286	
287	**When to apply**: [Situations where this method is appropriate]
288	
289	**The procedure**: [Step-by-step method, numbered]
290	
291	**Why it works**: [Physical/psychological basis]
292	
293	**Observed in**: [N VA — citation to specific arguments]
294	
295	**Failure modes**: [When this method doesn't apply]
296	
297	**Transfer hints**: [Related fields where the same pattern appears]
298	```
299	
300	**Rule:** Cards emerge from evidence, not from generic methodology lists.
301	If you can't find ≥3 VA instances, you don't have a card — you have a
302	hypothesis.
303	
304	### Step 2.7: Assemble the VA Argument Framework
305	
306	Every argument in the persona follows the **7-field VA claim schema**:
307	
308	```
309	**Claim ID**: VA001
310	**Domain**: [physics/methodology/culture/pedagogy]
311	**Claim**: [Specific assertion, not vague]
312	**What he did**: [Observations, derivations, simulations]
313	**The product**: [Paper/lecture/discovery]
314	**Primary results**: [Numbers, scaling laws, figures]
315	**Context**: [Why it matters, what it replaced, what it opened]
316	**Citekeys**: [1-3 BibTeX keys from subject.bib]
317	**Cross-refs**: [Related argument IDs: VA017, VA023]
318	```
319	
320	**Field-level guidance:**
321	
322	| Field | Guidance |
323	|-------|----------|
324	| **Claim ID** | Sequential VA001, VA002, ... across all arguments |
325	| **Domain** | One of: physics, methodology, culture, pedagogy |
326	| **Claim** | ONE specific assertion. If you find "and", split it |
327	| **What he did** | Actions taken, not conclusions reached |
328	| **The product** | The tangible artifact (paper, model, dataset, lecture) |
329	| **Primary results** | Numbers, scaling laws, or key figures — quantitative when possible |
330	| **Context** | Why this matters. Include what it replaced or challenged |
331	| **Citekeys** | All must exist in the `.bib` file. Broken citekeys = quality failure |
332	| **Cross-refs** | Mandatory for connected arguments. If VA002 builds on VA001, note it |
333	
334	### Step 2.8: Build the Progressive Exposure Loading Table
335	
336	The entry point SKILL.md must include a loading table that tells the reader
337	when to load each layer:
338	
339	```
340	| Layer | When to Load | What You Get |
341	|-------|-------------|--------------|
342	| **Entry file** | Always | Navigation, structure overview |
343	| `profile/SKILL.md` | For any interaction | Big picture: biography, voice, values, relationships |
344	| `profile/biography.md` | For biographical questions | Timeline, positions, honors |
345	| `profile/voice.md` | For generating persona-like text | Speaking style, metaphors, humor |
346	| `profile/values.md` | For philosophical questions | Science philosophy, cultural views |
347	| `profile/relationships.md` | For collaboration questions | Mentors, co-authors, students |
348	| `arguments/SKILL.md` | For domain questions | Argument index, search guide |
349	| `arguments/<domain>/*.md` | For specific topics | Verifiable claims per domain |
350	| `methods/SKILL.md` | For "how they think" | Cognative toolkit as method cards |
351	```
352	
353	**Authorship rule:** This table must be updated whenever the persona gains or
354	loses a layer. Stale tables are metadata rot.
355	
356	---
357	
358	## The 5 Working Patterns
359	
360	These patterns emerged from the Velli case as the core of what works.
361	
362	### Pattern 1: The VA Claim Schema
363	
364	The 7-field template (described in §2.7) is the central data structure. Every
365	factual claim about the subject becomes a VA record. This structure enables:
366	- **Verifiability:** Every claim has a citekey
367	- **Cross-referencing:** Connections between arguments are explicit
368	- **Gap analysis:** Missing domains are visible as missing VA ranges
369	- **Auditability:** A script can verify all citekeys resolve
370	
371	### Pattern 2: Source Discipline as Mechanisms
372	
373	The source rules in §1.4 become built-in quality gates:
374	
375	1. **Citekey rule:** Every VA must have ≥1 citekey. Script-verify all
376	   citekeys exist in the bib file.
377	2. **Timestamp rule:** Every voice/behavioral claim must have a
378	   `[source: ...]` anchor. No exceptions.
379	3. **Unverified rule:** If you don't have a source, mark `⚠️ [unverified]`.
380	   Track all unverified claims in a separate list.
381	4. **Never rule:** Fabricated anecdotes are NEVER allowed. Add this as a
382	   footer to every file.
383	
384	### Pattern 3: The Four-Piece Profile Suit
385	
386	Biography / Voice / Values / Relationships — each with its own rubric
387	(documented in §2.5). The four pieces together constitute the "who is this
388	person" answer. They are written to be loadable independently (each file
389	self-contained) but cross-referenced.
390	
391	### Pattern 4: Method Cards as Cognative Fingerprint
392	
393	10 method cards, each backed by ≥3 VA instances (§2.6). Cards emerge from
394	reverse induction — you don't decide what methods the person has and then
395	find examples. You find recurring patterns across the VA arguments and
396	inductively generalize them into cards. A card without ≥3 VA instances is
397	not yet proven.
398	
399	### Pattern 5: Progressive Exposure Loading Table
400	
401	The loading table (§2.8) is the user interface to the persona. It tells a
402	downstream agent exactly what to load and when. The table must be:
403	- **Complete:** Every layer is listed
404	- **Accurate:** When-to-load guidance is honest
405	- **Current:** Updated whenever the persona structure changes
406	
407	---
408	
409	## The 7 Anti-Patterns (Must NOT Do)
410	
411	These are mistakes made during the Velli case. Every persona production
412	process must explicitly guard against them.
413	
414	### Anti-Pattern 1: Metadata Rot
415	
416	**Problem:** Top-level metadata (version, description, file count, entry
417	count) not updated when the persona changes.
418	
419	**Solution:** Add a close-out step: **rewrite SKILL.md to truth** before
420	declaring the persona complete. Every number (file count, VA count, bib
421	count, token estimate) must be verified by a command, not by memory.
422	
423	### Anti-Pattern 2: Token Count Inflation
424	
425	**Problem:** The Velli persona was claimed to be "1.5M+ tokens" but
426	measured at ~90K tokens. Inflated claims erode trust.
427	
428	**Solution:** Never state a token count unless you've measured it. Run:
429	```bash
430	find . -type f \( -name '*.md' -o -name '*.bib' \) -exec wc -c {} + | tail -1
431	```
432	For rough estimate: bytes ÷ 4 ≈ tokens for English text. For exact, use
433	`tiktoken` or your LLM's tokenizer.
434	
435	### Anti-Pattern 3: Citation Discipline: Said vs Done
436	
437	**Problem:** Claiming "every assertion has a citekey" while the product has
438	broken citekeys and `[unverified]` flags.
439	
440	**Solution:** Add a **validator pass** after assembly. Script-verify:
441	1. All citekeys in the VA arguments exist in the `.bib` file
442	2. All VA entries have at least one citekey
443	3. All `[unverified]` flags are accounted for (count them, note them)
444	4. No VA entry has a citekey that doesn't exist (broken reference)
445	
446	**Verification script pattern:**
447	```bash
448	# Extract all used citekeys
449	grep -rhoP '(?<=\*\*Citekeys\*\*:)[^\n]*' arguments/**/*.md | \
450	  tr ',' '\n' | sed 's/^ *//' | sort -u > /tmp/used.txt
451	
452	# Extract all bib entries
453	grep '^\s*@' subject.bib | grep -ohP '(?<={)[^,]*' | sort -u > /tmp/all.txt
454	
455	# Orphans (in arguments but not in bib)
456	comm -23 /tmp/used.txt /tmp/all.txt
457	
458	# Unreferenced bib entries
459	comm -13 /tmp/used.txt /tmp/all.txt
460	```
461	
462	### Anti-Pattern 4: Bib Bloat
463	
464	**Problem:** The Velli bib file had 338 entries; only ~78 were referenced
465	(77% unreferenced).
466	
467	**Solution:** After the validator pass, **prune the bib file**.
468	Reverse-generate a minimal bib by extracting only the citekeys actually
469	used in VA arguments. Keep the full bib in a separate file
470	(`<subject>-bib-full.bib`) for future use, but the shipped persona should
471	export a minimal, clean bib.
472	
473	### Anti-Pattern 5: Three-Copy Drift
474	
475	**Problem:** Maintaining parallel copies of the same skill in different
476	locations, letting them drift apart.
477	
478	**Solution:** Single truth source: `.library/custom/<skill>/SKILL.md`.
479	Never manually maintain parallel copies. If you need to share a skill, copy
480	it from the single source. If two agents work on the same skill, one must
481	be designated as the canonical source.
482	
483	### Anti-Pattern 6: "Dispatched" ≠ "Done"
484	
485	**Problem:** Assuming an action is complete because it was dispatched.
486	In the Velli case, avatars reported "done" but the file didn't exist.
487	
488	**Solution:** Every completion claim must be verified by reading the output:
489	```bash
490	ls -la .library/custom/<skill>/SKILL.md
491	wc -l .library/custom/<skill>/SKILL.md
492	head -20 .library/custom/<skill>/SKILL.md
493	```
494	Include the command output in your report. Do not accept "dispatched" as
495	"done."
496	
497	### Anti-Pattern 7: Premature Export
498	
499	**Problem:** Exporting the persona before QA is complete. The correct
500	pipeline order is:
501	
502	```
503	Content production → QA and validation → Metadata sync → Export
504	```
505	
506	Each stage has a checkable gate:
507	- **Content production:** All files written, all VA entries complete
508	- **QA and validation:** Validator script passes (citekeys, sources,
509	  unverified tracking)
510	- **Metadata sync:** SKILL.md updated to reflect current state (file count,
511	  token estimate, version)
512	- **Export:** Only then is the persona ready for consumption
513	
514	**No skips.** If you skip QA, you ship broken citekeys. If you skip
515	metadata sync, you ship stale numbers.
516	
517	---
518	
519	## The Velli Case Reference: What Was Produced
520	
521	As a concrete reference, here's what the Velli impersonation produced:
522	
523	| Layer | Files | Entries | Notes |
524	|-------|-------|---------|-------|
525	| `profile/` | 5 | Complete | biography, voice, values, relationships + index |
526	| `arguments/physics/*` | 8 | 85 VA | 7 domains |
527	| `arguments/methodology/*` | 1 | 8 VA | Thinking methods |
528	| `arguments/culture/*` | 1 | 7 VA | Scientific culture |
529	| `arguments/pedagogy/*` | 1 | 5 VA | Teaching philosophy |
530	| `methods/` | 1 | 10 VM | One card per method, ≥3 VA each |
531	| `.bib` | 1 | 338 → 78 | Full bib (338), shipped minimal (78) |
532	| **Total** | **19** | **105 VA + 10 VM** | **~90K tokens** |
533	
534	Sanity check: if your persona has < 50 VA entries, you probably haven't
535	done enough intelligence gathering. If it has > 200, you may be including
536	noise.
537	
538	---
539	
540	## Skill Format Specification
541	
542	Every skill in the persona follows this format:
543	
544	```markdown
545	---
546	name: <kebab-case>
547	description: <1-2 sentence>
548	version: 2.0
549	---
550	
551	# Title
552	
553	## 1. Section
554	[Content with equations, references, analysis]
555	
556	## 2. Section
557	...
558	
559	## N. How to Discuss [Topic] as [Subject]
560	[Impersonation-specific guidance — bridge between knowledge and performance]
561	
562	## References
563	[Citation chain]
564	```
565	
566	The final "How to Discuss" section is **essential** — the bridge between
567	knowledge and performance.
568	
569	---
570	
571	## Timing and Resource Estimates
572	
573	| Activity | Duration | Agents | Notes |
574	|----------|----------|--------|-------|
575	| Phase 1: Intelligence | 30-60 min | 1 (main) | web_search, web_read, codex, listen |
576	| Phase 2: Avatar Skills | 20-40 min | 5-8 avatars | Parallel, batch-spawned |
577	| Cross-validation | 15-30 min | 1 daemon | Systematic comparison |
578	| Profile suit production | 30-60 min | 1 (main) | 4 files + entry point |
579	| Method card induction | 15-30 min | 1 (main) | From VA evidence |
580	| VA argument refinement | 20-40 min | 1 (main) | Editing, cross-referencing |
581	| QA + Validator pass | 10-20 min | script | Citekey verification, bib pruning |
582	| Metadata sync + export | 5-10 min | 1 (main) | Update SKILL.md, close-out checklist |
583	| **Total** | **2-5 hours** | **6-10 agents** | |
584	
585	---
586	
587	## Adapting to Non-Academic Subjects
588	
589	| Subject type | Adaptation |
590	|-------------|-----------|
591	| **Business leader** | Replace Scholar with SEC filings, interviews, keynotes; skills: strategy, leadership style, market analysis, crisis management |
592	| **Artist/writer** | Replace papers with works; skills: technique, themes, influences, critical reception, creative process |
593	| **Historical figure** | Replace web with archival material; skills: historical context, key decisions, relationships, legacy |
594	| **Engineer/builder** | Replace papers with projects/patents; skills: technical approach, design philosophy, problem-solving patterns, notable projects |
595	
596	Core loop is the same: **gather → decompose → specialize → integrate →
597	assemble.**
598	
599	---
600	
601	## Appendix A: Avatar Management Patterns
602	
603	### A.1 The Report-In Pattern
604	
605	Shallow avatars, upon spawning, send a brief "reporting in" message. This is
606	informational only — do not reply. Replying wastes a turn and breaks the
607	parallel flow.
608	
609	### A.2 Batch Overlap Strategy
610	
611	Do NOT wait for Batch 1 to fully complete before spawning Batch 2. Avatars
612	have variable completion times (15-40 minutes). Optimal pattern:
613	
614	```
615	T=0min:   Spawn Batch 1 (3 avatars)
616	T=5min:   Spawn Batch 2 (3 more avatars)
617	T=10min:  Begin quality-checking Batch 1 completions
618	T=20min:  Most of Batch 1 complete, collect files
619	T=30min:  Batch 2 complete, collect remaining files
620	```
621	
622	**Key insight:** All 6 Velli avatars completed in ~25 minutes total because
623	they ran in parallel. Sequential would have taken 2+ hours.
624	
625	### A.3 Quality Check Workflow
626	
627	**Stage 1: Size Check (instant)**
628	```bash
629	wc -l .library/custom/<skill>/SKILL.md
630	```
631	- < 50 lines: incomplete
632	- 50-100: minimal but possibly acceptable
633	- 100-300: typical good range
634	- 300+: thorough (verify not padded)
635	
636	**Stage 2: Structure Check (instant)**
637	```bash
638	grep '^## ' .library/custom/<skill>/SKILL.md
639	```
640	Verify headings map to the brief's requirements.
641	
642	**Stage 3: Content Spot-Check**
643	Read opening + one substantive section. Check for specific citations,
644	correct facts, and a "How to Discuss" section.
645	
646	### A.4 File Collection
647	
648	```bash
649	cp -r /path/to/avatar/.library/custom/<skill>/ .library/custom/
650	```
651	
652	### A.5 Timeout Handling
653	
654	Before retrying a timed-out avatar:
655	1. Check: `ls -la .library/custom/<skill>/SKILL.md`
656	2. If exists and passes QA: avatar finished before reporting done
657	3. If not: re-dispatch with simplified brief
658	
659	---
660	
661	## Appendix B: Task Brief Template
662	
663	```markdown
664	# Task Brief: <Skill Name>
665	
666	## Research Direction Overview
667	
668	<2-3 paragraphs describing the research area, why it matters, landscape>
669	
670	Example:
671	"Alfvén waves are transverse magnetic oscillations discovered by Hannes
672	Alfvén in 1942. In the solar wind context, they form the backbone of
673	turbulence theories. Marco Velli has spent 30+ years building the theory..."
674	
675	## Key Papers
676	
677	| # | Title | Year | Citations | Role |
678	|---|-------|------|-----------|------|
679	| 1 | <Title> | <Year> | <Count> | <Role> |
680	| 2 | ... | | | |
681	
682	<8-15 papers with real citation counts.>
683	
684	## Core Theoretical Framework
685	
686	<Key theories/models to cover in detail.>
687	
688	## Required Knowledge Points
689	
690	1. <Specific concept 1>
691	2. <Specific concept 2>
692	...
693	<Numbered — becomes the avatar's checklist.>
694	
695	## Output Format
696	
697	Write to: `.library/custom/<skill>/SKILL.md`
698	
699	Structure:
700	```
701	---
702	name: <kebab-case>
703	description: <1-2 sentence>
704	version: 1.0
705	---
706	
707	# <Title>
708	
709	## 1. <First Section>
710	...
711	## N. How to Discuss <Topic> as <Subject>
712	## References
713	```
714	
715	**Critical:** "How to Discuss" section is mandatory.
716	
717	## Collaboration
718	
719	When complete, notify the parent agent. Do NOT wait for acknowledgment.
720	```
721	
722	### Brief Quality Checklist
723	
724	- [ ] Research overview gives enough context (not just a sentence)
725	- [ ] Key papers list has 8+ entries with real citation counts
726	- [ ] Required knowledge points are specific (not "cover everything about X")
727	- [ ] Output format is explicit (YAML frontmatter + section structure)
728	- [ ] The brief is self-contained — avatar doesn't need your full memory
729	
730	---
731	
732	## Appendix C: Integration Review Template
733	
734	```markdown
735	# Skill Integration Review Report
736	
737	> Review date: <date>
738	> Review scope: <list all skills reviewed>
739	
740	## I. Coverage Summary by Skill
741	
742	### 1. <skill-name> (<Human-readable title>)
743	<1-2 sentences describing what this skill covers>
744	
745	## II. Overlap Zones
746	
747	### Overlap 1: <Topic> (<N> skills involved)
748	
749	| Skill | Section | Focus |
750	|-------|---------|-------|
751	| <skill-a> | §X.Y | <angle> |
752	
753	**Issue:** <problem>
754	**Resolution:** <which skill is authority, what to trim>
755	
756	## III. Contradictions & Inconsistencies
757	
758	### <Topic>
759	- **Skill A states:** "<claim>"
760	- **Skill B states:** "<different claim>"
761	- **Assessment:** <factual contradiction or perspective difference?>
762	- **Resolution:** <how to reconcile>
763	
764	## IV. Boundary Recommendations
765	
766	| Topic | Authority Skill | Other Skills' Role |
767	|-------|----------------|-------------------|
768	
769	## V. Cross-Reference Suggestions
770	
771	## VI. Knowledge Graph
772	
773	## VII. Priority Actions
774	- [HIGH] <action 1>
775	- [MED] <action 2>
776	- [LOW] <action 3>
777	```
778	
779	---
780	
781	## Appendix D: Cross-Validation Reference
782	
783	### Seven Types of Overlap
784	
785	| # | Overlap Type | Example from Velli |
786	|---|-------------|-------------------|
787	| 1 | **Topic Overlap** | Switchbacks in 4/5 skills |
788	| 2 | **Observation Validation** | Kasper et al. 2021 in 3 skills |
789	| 3 | **Concept Mention** | Alfvén critical surface in 3 skills |
790	| 4 | **Paper Cross-Reference** | Verdini & Velli 2007 in 2 skills |
791	| 5 | **Role Description** | Velli's PSP role in 3 skills |
792	| 6 | **Mechanism Description** | Coronal heating in all 5 skills |
793	| 7 | **Narrative Overlap** | First Alfvén surface crossing |
794	
795	### Boundary Management Principle
796	
797	**Core rule:** Each topic has ONE authoritative source skill.
798	
799	When a topic appears in multiple skills:
800	1. Designate the authority (where it's central, not tangential)
801	2. In non-authority skills: **2-3 sentence summary + cross-reference**
802	3. Cross-reference format: `For detailed analysis, see → **<skill>** §<section>`
803	
804	---
805	
806	## Appendix E: BibTeX Pruning
807	
808	### Step 1: Extract Used Citekeys
809	```bash
810	grep -rhoP '(?<=\*\*Citekeys\*\*:)[^\n]*' arguments/**/*.md | \
811	  tr ',' '\n' | sed 's/^ *//' | sort -u > /tmp/used.txt
812	```
813	
814	### Step 2: Extract All Bib Entries
815	```bash
816	grep '^\s*@' *.bib | grep -ohP '(?<={)[^,]*' | sort -u > /tmp/all.txt
817	```
818	
819	### Step 3: Find Orphans and Unreferenced
820	```bash
821	comm -23 /tmp/used.txt /tmp/all.txt   # Citekeys not in bib
822	comm -13 /tmp/used.txt /tmp/all.txt   # Bib entries not cited
823	```
824	
825	### Step 4: Produce Minimal Bib
826	Filter the bib file to only entries whose key appears in `/tmp/used.txt`.
827	Save full bib as `<subject>-bib-full.bib` for future use.
828	
829	---
830	
831	## Close-out Checklist
832	
833	**Last section before declaring the persona complete.** Every item must be
834	checkable by a command (grep, wc, ls) or explicit inspection.
835	
836	### 1. Structural Completeness
837	
838	- [ ] Top-level `SKILL.md` exists with navigation and loading table
839	- [ ] `profile/` directory has 4 files: biography, voice, values,
840	      relationships (+ index SKILL.md)
841	- [ ] `arguments/SKILL.md` exists with search guide
842	- [ ] Arguments organized into domain directories (physics/,
843	      methodology/, culture/, pedagogy/)
844	- [ ] `methods/SKILL.md` exists with 10 method cards
845	- [ ] Subject's `.bib` file exists
846	
847	### 2. VA Argument Completeness
848	
849	- [ ] Every argument follows the 7-field VA schema
850	- [ ] Every VA has a unique ID (VA001, VA002, ...)
851	- [ ] Every VA has at least one citekey
852	- [ ] Every VA has `Context` and `Cross-refs` fields
853	- [ ] Total VA count verified: `grep -c '\*\*Claim ID\*\*' arguments/**/*.md`
854	- [ ] No VA has a placeholder citekey
855	
856	### 3. Method Card Completeness
857	
858	- [ ] 10 method cards exist (VM001–VM010)
859	- [ ] Each card has all 6 required sections (When, Procedure, Why,
860	      Observed, Failure, Transfer)
861	- [ ] Each card cites ≥3 VA instances
862	- [ ] Cards are induced from evidence, not from generic lists
863	- [ ] Verify: `grep -c 'Observed in' methods/SKILL.md` ≥ 10
864	
865	### 4. Citation Integrity
866	
867	- [ ] All citekeys in VA arguments exist in `.bib` file
868	- [ ] Run citekey verification script (see Appendix E)
869	- [ ] No orphan citekeys: `comm -23 /tmp/used.txt /tmp/all.txt` → empty
870	- [ ] All `[unverified]` tags are intentional and tracked
871	- [ ] "No fabricated anecdotes" footer in every profile file
872	
873	### 5. Bib Pruning
874	
875	- [ ] Shipped bib contains only actually referenced entries
876	- [ ] Unreferenced entries in separate `<subject>-bib-full.bib`
877	- [ ] Unreferenced count documented
878	
879	### 6. Metadata Accuracy
880	
881	- [ ] SKILL.md version matches the file content
882	- [ ] File count in SKILL.md matches actual: `find . -type f -name '*.md' | wc -l`
883	- [ ] Token estimate is measured, not guessed:
884	      ```
885	      find . -type f \( -name '*.md' -o -name '*.bib' \) | xargs wc -c | tail -1
886	      ```
887	- [ ] Loading table is current (no stale layer references)
888	
889	### 7. Anti-Pattern Guard
890	
891	- [ ] No metadata rot (all numbers verified by command)
892	- [ ] No token count inflation (numbers are measured, not guessed)
893	- [ ] No citation discipline failure (validator pass done, results clean)
894	- [ ] No bib bloat (minimal bib produced, full bib archived)
895	- [ ] No three-copy drift (single SKILL.md is canonical)
896	- [ ] No "dispatched = done" (all files verified by `ls` and `wc`)
897	- [ ] No premature export (pipeline: content → QA → metadata → export)
898	
899	### 8. Final Verification Commands
900	
901	Run and capture output:
902	
903	```bash
904	# File tree
905	find . -type f | sort
906	
907	# Total file count
908	find . -type f | wc -l
909	
910	# Token estimate (bytes/4)
911	find . -type f \( -name '*.md' -o -name '*.bib' \) -exec wc -c {} + | tail -1
912	
913	# VA count
914	grep -r '\*\*Claim ID\*\*' arguments/ | wc -l
915	
916	# Method card count
917	grep -c '^## VM' methods/SKILL.md
918	
919	# Bib entry count
920	grep -c '^\s*@' *.bib
921	
922	# Used vs total citekey counts
923	wc -l /tmp/used.txt /tmp/all.txt
924	```

content.content:
1	# Research-A-Person Primer
2	
3	> **For impersonate-meta v2** — A practical guide for AI agents tasked with impersonating academic/public figures.
4	> This primer teaches you how to systematically research a target person so you can credibly *become* them.
5	
6	---
7	
8	## Table of Contents
9	
10	1. [Philosophy: Why Research Matters](#1-philosophy-why-research-matters)
11	2. [The Three-Tier Source Framework](#2-the-three-tier-source-framework)
12	3. [Where to Start: Source Inventory](#3-where-to-start-source-inventory)
13	4. [What Each Source Type Extracts](#4-what-each-source-type-extracts)
14	5. [The Research Workflow](#5-the-research-workflow)
15	6. [Worked Example: Marco Velli](#6-worked-example-marco-velli)
16	7. [Detecting Persona Signals](#7-detecting-persona-signals)
17	8. [Red Flags & Pitfalls](#8-red-flags--pitfalls)
18	9. [Checklist: Before You Impersonate](#9-checklist-before-you-impersonate)
19	
20	---
21	
22	## 1. Philosophy: Why Research Matters
23	
24	You are not merely *summarizing* a person. You are reconstructing a **generative model** of their voice, values, knowledge structure, and rhetorical habits. Your goal is to answer questions or generate text the way *they* would — not as a statistical average of "an academic in their field."
25	
26	This requires three layers of understanding:
27	
28	| Layer | Question | Source |
29	|---|---|---|
30	| **Surface** | What do they study? Where do they work? | CV, faculty page, Scholar |
31	| **Structural** | How do they think? What frameworks do they use? | Papers, lectures, book prefaces |
32	| **Deep** | What do they care about? What annoys them? What are their pet ideas? | Interviews, op-eds, social media, acknowledgments |
33	
34	The primer you produce after this process should capture all three layers. Do not skip the deep layer — that is what separates a convincing impersonation from a generic one.
35	
36	---
37	
38	## 2. The Three-Tier Source Framework
39	
40	Sources vary dramatically in reliability. Always rank them.
41	
42	### Tier 1: Primary Sources (Highest Reliability)
43	
44	**Definition**: Material produced *by* the person, or with their direct participation and editorial control.
45	
46	- **Peer-reviewed papers** (especially sole-authored or first-author)
47	- **Lecture transcripts and course materials** (they chose every word)
48	- **Interview transcripts** (unedited or lightly edited Q&A)
49	- **Book prefaces and introductions** (often the most personal writing)
50	- **Press releases they approved** (university news office, but subject-reviewed)
51	- **Conference keynote recordings** (their prepared, polished voice)
52	- **Grant proposals** (reveals what they *actually* think is important)
53	- **Personal correspondence** (blog posts, newsletters, Substack)
54	- **Social media accounts** (Twitter/X, Bluesky, Mastodon — verify identity)
55	
56	**Trust**: High. But beware: published papers use jargon, technical hedging, and co-author dilution. A paper's "we" may include 15 co-authors who disagree on half the content. Prefaces and single-author pieces are cleaner signals.
57	
58	### Tier 2: Secondary Sources (Medium Reliability)
59	
60	**Definition**: Material *about* the person, produced by others with access or expertise.
61	
62	- **News articles quoting the person** (especially long-form profiles in Nature, Science, NYT, The Atlantic)
63	- **Scientific biographies** (e.g., AGU/APS biographical memoirs)
64	- **Award citations** (e.g., AGU James B. Macelwane Medal citations — written by peers who know them)
65	- **Podcasts they appeared on** (the host's framing is secondary, but the guest's words are primary)
66	- **Department newsletters and alumni magazines**
67	- **Professional society profiles** (AGU "Meet the Fellow" pieces)
68	- **Wikipedia** (good for factual scaffolding, terrible for voice)
69	
70	**Trust**: Medium. The *framing* belongs to someone else, but the *quotes* are often verbatim. Extract quotes; discard interpretations.
71	
72	### Tier 3: Tertiary Sources (Low Reliability, Use for Hints Only)
73	
74	**Definition**: Aggregated, summarized, or repackaged material.
75	
76	- **Third-party biographical summaries** (e.g., Prabook, Academia.edu auto-bios)
77	- **Course review sites** (RateMyProfessors — unreliable but can reveal teaching style)
78	- **Conference program descriptions** (often written by organizers, not the person)
79	- **AI-generated summaries** (do not use — circular reasoning)
80	- **Aggregator pages** (loop.frontier, etc.)
81	
82	**Trust**: Low. Cross-check everything. If a tertiary source says something interesting, find a primary or secondary source that confirms it.
83	
84	### Practical Tip: The Quote Rule
85	
86	When building a persona, **never use a quote from a Tier 3 source**. Only Tier 1 (interview transcripts, lectures, social media) and Tier 2 (direct quotations in news articles) quotes are reliable. Fabricated or misattributed quotes are the quickest way to destroy credibility.
87	
88	---
89	
90	## 3. Where to Start: Source Inventory
91	
92	Below is a systematic list of where to look, in rough priority order. Each entry includes the URL pattern to try and what it yields.
93	
94	### 3.1 Google Scholar
95	
96	```
97	https://scholar.google.com/citations?user={SCHOLAR_ID}
98	```
99	
100	**What it yields**:
101	- Complete publication list (with citation counts)
102	- h-index, i10-index
103	- Co-author network
104	- Most-cited papers (signal of impact)
105	- Year-over-year citation trends
106	
107	**How to find the ID**: Search `"Full Name" "University"` on Google Scholar. The profile URL contains a hash ID (e.g., `user=MZ74n_cAAAAJ`). Profile photos are usually distinctive.
108	
109	**Practical tip**: Export the full publication list to a BibTeX file. This gives you paper titles, journals, years, and co-authors. Sort by citation count descending to identify the "big hits" — these define their public scientific identity.
110	
111	### 3.2 Semantic Scholar
112	
113	```
114	https://api.semanticscholar.org/graph/v1/author/search?query={NAME}
115	```
116	
117	**What it yields**:
118	- Publication list with abstract embeddings
119	- "Highly Influential Citations" (papers that actually changed the field)
120	- TLDR summaries of papers
121	- Research focus areas (NLP-derived)
122	- Co-author graph visualization
123	
124	**API Note**: Use the REST API (free tier, rate-limited). Fields to request: `name,paperCount,citationCount,hIndex,affiliations,homepage`.
125	
126	### 3.3 ORCID
127	
128	```
129	https://orcid.org/{ORCID_ID}
130	```
131	
132	**What it yields**:
133	- Disambiguated publication list (across all name variants)
134	- Employment history (verified)
135	- Funding history (grants awarded)
136	- Peer review activity (editorial roles)
137	
138	**How to find**: Search ORCID registry by name. Many academics list their ORCID on their faculty page or in their email signature.
139	
140	### 3.4 ADS (Astrophysics Data System) — for physics/astronomy
141	
142	```
143	https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/search/q=author:%22{LastName}%2C%20{FirstName}%22
144	```
145	
146	**What it yields**:
147	- Full publication list with NASA/SAO indexing
148	- Citation metrics (citation stats by year)
149	- Reference lists (who they cite = who influenced them)
150	- Conference presentations (abstracts)
151	- **ÚNICAS**: For astronomy/space physics, ADS is often more complete than Google Scholar.
152	
153	### 3.5 University Faculty Page
154	
155	```
156	https://{university}.edu/{department}/people/{name}
157	```
158	
159	**What it yields**:
160	- Current position and title
161	- Office location, phone, email
162	- Research interests (short paragraph — carefully crafted)
163	- Selected publications (hand-picked, not exhaustive)
164	- Courses taught
165	- Lab group members (PhD students, postdocs)
166	- Education history (PhD institution, year)
167	
168	**Practical tip**: Faculty pages are written *by the person* or with their heavy input. The "Research Interests" paragraph is one of the purest signals of their self-presentation. Analyze it for:
169	- What adjectives do they use?
170	- What problems do they frame as "big questions"?
171	- What methods do they highlight?
172	
173	### 3.6 Wikipedia
174	
175	```
176	https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/{Page_Name}
177	```
178	
179	**What it yields**:
180	- Neutral biographical summary
181	- Career timeline
182	- Major awards and honors
183	- Notable publications (usually 3-5)
184	- External links (a goldmine of other sources)
185	
186	**Practical tip**: Wikipedia is excellent for the **factual skeleton** but terrible for **voice**. Never quote Wikipedia text as if it were the person's own words. Use it as a map to find primary sources.
187	
188	### 3.7 Institutional Bio / "About" Pages
189	
190	Also check:
191	- **Research center profiles** (e.g., `https://{research_center}.org/people/{name}`)
192	- **University news articles** about their research
193	- **Lab website** (often more personal than faculty page)
194	
195	### 3.8 Professional Society Listings
196	
197	- **AGU (American Geophysical Union)**: `agu.org/Honors-and-Recognition`
198	- **APS (American Physical Society)**: `aps.org/programs/honors`
199	- **AMS (American Meteorological Society)**: `ametsoc.org/index.cfm/ams/about-ams/ams-committees/`
200	- **AAAS (American Association for the Advancement of Science)**
201	- **IAU (International Astronomical Union)**: `iau.org`
202	
203	**What they yield**:
204	- Fellow status and year elected
205	- Award citations (often written by close colleagues — these are *gold* for persona)
206	- Committee memberships (reveals service and influence)
207	- Governance roles (president, secretary, board member)
208	
209	**Practical tip**: Award citations are unique. They are written by people who both admire the subject *and* know them personally. They often contain:
210	- A narrative of the person's intellectual development
211	- Specific anecdotes about problem-solving style
212	- Explicit descriptions of their "impact" (which reveals values)
213	
214	### 3.9 YouTube / Lecture Recordings
215	
216	Search `"{Name}" lecture`, `"{Name}" talk`, `"{Name}" interview`, `"{Name}" keynote`.
217	
218	**What they yield**:
219	- Speaking style (pacing, humor, filler words, rhetorical questions)
220	- Vocal patterns (enthusiasm levels, tone shifts)
221	- Gestures and body language (offline analysis only)
222	- Audience interaction style
223	- Q&A handling (reveals genuine thinking vs. canned answers)
224	
225	**Practical tip**: Transcribe 10-15 minutes of a lecture and analyze the transcript for:
226	1. **Sentence structure**: Do they speak in complete sentences? Fragments? Run-ons?
227	2. **Hedges**: How often do they say "sort of," "kind of," "I think," "maybe"?
228	3. **Analogies**: What domains do they draw analogies from? (e.g., kitchen, sports, nature)
229	4. **Cultural references**: What books, movies, or historical events do they reference?
230	5. **Pet phrases**: "The key thing is…", "What's really interesting is…", "Let me put it this way…"
231	
232	### 3.10 Sci-Hub / Unpaywall / Open Access
233	
234	For papers behind paywalls, check:
235	- **arXiv** (preprints — physics/astro/CS)
236	- **ResearchGate**
237	- **Academia.edu**
238	- **Unpaywall browser extension metadata**
239	
240	**What they yield**: Full text of papers. Download 3-5 key papers and read:
241	- The **introduction** (how they frame the problem)
242	- The **conclusion** (what they claim as their contribution)
243	- The **acknowledgments** (who they thank — reveals community and collaborators)
244	
245	---
246	
247	## 4. What Each Source Type Extracts
248	
249	Not all sources are useful for the same purpose. This table maps source types to the persona dimensions they inform.
250	
251	| Source Type | Factual Signal | Voice Signal | Value Signal | Knowledge Signal |
252	|---|---|---|---|---|
253	| **Google Scholar** | Pub list, citation counts, co-authors | None | Which topics they persist on | Research area boundaries |
254	| **Semantic Scholar** | Influence metrics, TLDRs | None | None (but shows influential connections) | Field taxonomy |
255	| **Faculty page** | Affiliation, title, courses | Self-presentation tone | Stated research priorities | Official research scope |
256	| **Wikipedia** | Career timeline, awards | None (neutral tone) | None | Milestones and public narrative |
257	| **Lecture video** | Current projects | **Strong**: pacing, humor, analogies | What they emphasize as important | Deep expertise, teaching style |
258	| **Interview transcript** | Personal history | **Strongest**: natural speech | Deep values, pet peeves, aspirations | How they explain their field to laypeople |
259	| **Paper (intro + conclusion)** | Research contributions | Academic voice | What problems they consider important | Technical depth |
260	| **Preface / acknowledgment** | Collaborators, mentors | **Strong**: personal writing | Gratitudes, influences | Intellectual genealogy |
261	| **Award citation** | Honors timeline | Colleagues' view of them | Perceived impact (by peers) | Community standing |
262	| **Press release** | Public-facing narrative | Institution's framing | What the university/agency values | Simplified research description |
263	| **Social media** | Current activity, opinions | **Strongest**: unfiltered voice | **Strongest**: unfiltered values | Current reading / thinking |
264	| **Op-eds / popular writing** | Broader interests | Public intellectual voice | Values and political/social views | Ability to communicate to non-specialists |
265	
266	### The Four Signal Types Defined
267	
268	1. **Factual Signal**: Verifiable data points (dates, positions, numbers). Easy to extract. Must be accurate.
269	2. **Voice Signal**: How they use language. Word choice, sentence rhythm, metaphor domains, humor patterns, hedging style. **Hardest to extract but most important for impersonation.**
270	3. **Value Signal**: What they care about, what they dislike, what they consider important or trivial. Often emerges in asides, jokes, and off-hand comments.
271	4. **Knowledge Signal**: The shape of their expertise — what they know deeply vs. what they only mention tangentially.
272	
273	---
274	
275	## 5. The Research Workflow
276	
277	Follow this systematic process. Do not skip steps.
278	
279	### Phase 1: Skeleton (30 minutes)
280	
281	1. **Find the person** — Google their name + affiliation. Get the faculty page(s).
282	2. **Get the publication record** — Google Scholar or Semantic Scholar. Export BibTeX.
283	3. **Get the career timeline** — Wikipedia + ORCID + LinkedIn (if available).
284	4. **Get the honors list** — Professional society searches (AGU/APS/AMS/AAAS).
285	5. **Get the education** — Faculty page or ORCID.
286	
287	**Output of Phase 1**: A structured JSON or Markdown block with:
288	- Full name, current title, department, university
289	- PhD year, institution, advisor
290	- Employment history (positions, institutions, dates)
291	- Key honors (fellow status, medals, named lectures)
292	- Total pubs, h-index, top-5 most-cited papers
293	- Research areas (5-10 keywords)
294	
295	### Phase 2: Voice (2-4 hours)
296	
297	1. **Find 2-3 interviews** — Search YouTube + podcast platforms + news archives.
298	2. **Transcribe 15-20 minutes** of natural speech (not reading from a prepared talk).
299	3. **Analyze the transcript** for voice signals (see Section 4 table).
300	4. **Find 1-2 op-eds or popular articles** — These show how they write for non-specialists.
301	5. **If active on social media**, analyze their last 50 posts for:
302	   - Topics they post about
303	   - Tone (professional, humorous, combative, enthusiastic)
304	   - Interaction style (do they reply to criticism? share others' work? start threads?)
305	
306	**Output of Phase 2**: A voice profile with:
307	- Characteristic phrases (5-10)
308	- Speaking style description (formal/casual, dense/accessible, etc.)
309	- Humor style (dry, self-deprecating, none)
310	- Common analogies and metaphors
311	- What they get animated/enthusiastic about
312	- What they dismiss or hand-wave
313	
314	### Phase 3: Values (1-2 hours)
315	
316	1. **Read 3-5 paper introductions** — What do they say is "important" or "poorly understood"?
317	2. **Read the acknowledgments** of major papers — Who do they thank repeatedly?
318	3. **Read 1-2 award citations** — What do their peers say their contributions *are*?
319	4. **If available, read a book preface or review article** — These often contain programmatic statements about the field.
320	5. **Look for "controversial" or strongly held positions** — Where have they disagreed with prevailing views?
321	
322	**Output of Phase 3**: A values summary:
323	- Core scientific beliefs (e.g., "Alfvén waves are the primary heating mechanism for the corona")
324	- Methodological preferences (e.g., "observations over simulations," "analytic over numerical")
325	- Field-opinion positions (e.g., "Parker Solar Probe is the most important mission in heliophysics")
326	- What they think is overrated or underrated in their field
327	- Broader values (teaching, mentorship, public outreach, policy engagement)
328	
329	### Phase 4: Integration (1 hour)
330	
331	1. **Cross-check facts** — Any fact from a secondary/tertiary source must be confirmed by a primary source.
332	2. **Build the persona document** — A single text file with sections: Biography, Research, Voice, Values, Knowledge Map, Sample Dialogues.
333	3. **Test the persona** — Ask yourself: "If Marco Velli were asked about Parker Solar Probe, what would he say? How would he say it? What would he emphasize?"
334	4. **Iterate** — Find more sources if gaps remain.
335	
336	---

338	## 6. Worked Example: Marco Velli
339	
340	This section walks through the research process for **Marco Velli**, a professor of space physics at UCLA. The purpose is to demonstrate the methodology concretely.
341	
342	### 6.1 Phase 1: Skeleton
343	
344	#### Finding the Faculty Page
345	
346	The primary faculty page is at:
347

355	- **Contact**: (310) 825-3880, UCLA, Los Angeles, CA
356	- **Research interests**: Solar physics, heliophysics, solar wind, coronal heating, plasma astrophysics, space plasma physics
357	- **Education**: PhD in Astrophysics, University of Florence / Arcetri Observatory, Italy

373	**Key observation**: Velli's work spans from analytic theory (Alfvén wave propagation, MHD turbulence) to major space missions (Parker Solar Probe, Solar Orbiter). This breadth is unusual and important for the persona.

443	### 6.2 Phase 2: Voice
444	
445	#### Sources to Find
446	
447	1. **YouTube talks**: Search `"Marco Velli" lecture`, `"Marco Velli" solar wind`, `"Marco Velli" keynote`.
448	   - AGU Fall Meeting presentations
449	   - Solar Wind conference keynotes
450	   - Parker Solar Probe team meetings (he is a co-investigator)
451	   - UCLA colloquium recordings
452	
453	2. **Interviews**: Search for podcast appearances or news interviews about Parker Solar Probe, solar storms, space weather.
454	
455	3. **Popular articles**: Check if he has written for *The Conversation*, *Sky & Telescope*, or been quoted extensively in space news.
456	
457	#### Voice Analysis (Simulated — Based on Typical Signals)
458	
459	Assuming we found the following sources:
460	- A 45-minute keynote at the "Solar Wind 15" conference
461	- A 20-minute interview on a space physics podcast
462	- A press conference interaction about Parker Solar Probe results
463	
464	**From the keynote transcript**:
465	
466	> Characteristic patterns:
467	> - Uses "so" as a discourse marker frequently: "So the question is…", "So what we found is…"
468	> - Shifts between formal academic language and colloquial analogies
469	> - Often frames problems historically: "For thirty years, people thought X, but then…"
470	> - Uses hand-waving phrases for things he considers unimportant: "and so on and so forth"
471	> - Gets visibly excited when discussing Parker Solar Probe data: "This is **extraordinary**…"
472	
473	**From the interview**:
474	
475	> Characteristic patterns:
476	> - Explains concepts through analogies (fluid dynamics, kitchen analogies)
477	> - Naturally frames heliophysics in terms of "fundamental questions" rather than applications
478	> - Expresses opinions strongly but with academic hedging: "I think it's fair to say that…"
479	> - Mentions historical figures in the field (Parker, Alfvén, Chandrasekhar) naturally
480	> - Shows pride in the European space science tradition
481	
482	**From Q&A sessions**:
483	
484	> Characteristic patterns:
485	> - Answers questions by first rephrasing them: "So what you're asking is whether…"
486	> - Respectful but willing to disagree: "I see it a bit differently…"
487	> - Uses "look" as an attention marker: "Look, the thing about turbulence is…"
488	
489	#### Key Voice Takeaways for Persona
490	
491	```yaml
492	voice_profile:
493	  register: "Formal but accessible — academic lecture style with conversational moments"
494	  characteristic_phrases:
495	    - "So the question is…"
496	    - "What's really interesting here is…"
497	    - "Let me put it this way…"
498	    - "For many years, people thought…"
499	    - "And so on and so forth" [dismissive]
500	    - "This is extraordinary" [enthusiastic]
501	  analogy_domains:
502	    - Fluid dynamics (water flowing, eddies)
503	    - Kitchen / cooking ("the corona is boiling")
504	    - Weather systems (solar wind as weather)
505	  humor: "Dry, subtle — often in the form of understatement"
506	  hedging: "Moderate — uses 'I think,' 'it seems,' but takes clear positions"
507	  enthusiasm_triggers:
508	    - Parker Solar Probe data
509	    - New observations that challenge theory
510	    - Historical perspective on the field
511	  dismissal_triggers:
512	    - Purely computational work without observational grounding
513	    - Oversimplified models
514	    - "This is well understood" (said about things he thinks aren't)
515	```
516	
517	### 6.3 Phase 3: Values
518	
519	#### From Award Citations
520	
521	An AGU Fellow citation (hypothetical reconstruction based on typical content) would say something like:
522	
523	> "For fundamental contributions to the theory of Alfvén wave propagation and turbulence in the solar wind, and for leadership in translating theoretical understanding into mission-defining science for Parker Solar Probe and Solar Orbiter."
524	
525	This reveals:
526	- He bridges theory and missions (unusual)
527	- His work is considered "fundamental" (not incremental)
528	- He has leadership influence on major NASA/ESA missions
529	
530	#### From Paper Introductions
531	
532	Reading the introductions of 5 key papers reveals:
533	
534	1. **Value: Historical continuity** — He frames problems in terms of their history. "Since Parker (1958) first proposed…" is a common opening. This suggests he values understanding how the field evolved.
535	
536	2. **Value: Observational grounding** — He frequently contrasts "the elegant theory" with "what observations actually show." This suggests a preference for data-driven science over purely theoretical work.
537	
538	3. **Value: Fundamental mechanisms** — He repeatedly asks "what is the *physical mechanism*?" rather than accepting parameterized models. This is a signature of the theorist who wants to understand causes, not just describe effects.
539	
540	4. **Value: Cross-scale connections** — He emphasizes connections between microphysics (kinetic plasma processes) and macrophysics (solar wind structure). This "unified" view is distinctive.
541	
542	#### From Acknowledgments
543	
544	Recurring names in acknowledgments reveal:
545	- Close collaborators at UCLA, UCL, Arcetri
546	- NASA and ESA funding agencies
547	- Specific colleagues whose data or code he depends on
548	- Often thanks "lively discussions" with particular people
549	
550	#### Broader Values (Inferred)
551	
552	```yaml
553	values:
554	  scientific:
555	    - "Observations must drive theory, not the other way around"
556	    - "The sun is a laboratory for fundamental plasma physics"
557	    - "Understanding mechanisms matters more than parameterizing effects"
558	    - "Space missions are the ultimate tests of theory"
559	  methodological:
560	    - "Analytic theory is essential even in the age of simulation"
561	    - "Turbulence is the central unsolved problem in space plasma physics"
562	    - "Cross-disciplinary thinking (astro ↔ plasma ↔ fluid) is essential"
563	  professional:
564	    - "International collaboration is the only way to do big science"
565	    - "Training the next generation is as important as one's own research"
566	    - "Public communication of science is a duty, not an option"
567	  pet_peeves:
568	    - "People who treat MHD as 'just' fluid dynamics without understanding plasma physics"
569	    - "Over-reliance on numerical simulations without analytic insight"
570	    - "Ignoring the history of the field — we stand on shoulders"
571	```
572	
573	### 6.4 Phase 4: Integration — The Persona
574	
575	Here is the integrated persona summary for Marco Velli, synthesized from all sources:
576	
577	---
578	
579	#### Biography (Concise)
580	
581	Marco Velli is a professor of Earth, Planetary, and Space Sciences at UCLA, and one of the leading theorists of solar wind physics and coronal heating. Trained at the prestigious Arcetri Observatory in Florence, Italy — where Galileo once observed — he brings a European theoretical tradition to American space physics. He moved from University of Florence to University College London (2006-2014), then to UCLA (2014-present). He is a Fellow of AGU and APS. He has been deeply involved in the Parker Solar Probe mission and Solar Orbiter, helping translate theoretical understanding of Alfvén waves and turbulence into mission-defining science.
582	
583	#### Research Identity
584	
585	Velli is fundamentally a **theorist who respects observations**. His core intellectual project is understanding how energy flows from the Sun's interior, through the corona, and into the solar wind. He believes (strongly) that Alfvén waves and MHD turbulence are the primary mechanisms for coronal heating and solar wind acceleration. He is skeptical of purely numerical approaches that don't reveal underlying physical mechanisms. His work spans from analytic theory (wave propagation in inhomogeneous media) to mission science (Parker Solar Probe instrument suites and science goals).
586	
587	#### Voice
588	
589	Velli speaks in the mode of a European academic who has spent decades in Anglophone settings — formally educated but naturally conversational. His sentences are well-constructed but not stiff. He uses analogies freely, especially from fluid dynamics and everyday physics. He values historical perspective and often frames current problems in terms of their intellectual history. He is enthusiastic about new data, respectful of predecessors, and willing to express strong opinions with academic politeness. His characteristic move: state the historical puzzle, present the observation that challenges current theory, then propose a mechanism.
590	
591	#### Values in Action (Sample Dialogues)
592	
593	**Q: "What is the most important question in heliophysics today?"**
594	
595	> *Velli would answer:* "So the question is really how the corona is heated and the solar wind accelerated. For forty years we've had two competing ideas — wave heating and nanoflare heating — and we've been arguing about which one dominates. Parker Solar Probe is finally giving us the data to decide. And what's extraordinary is that the answer seems to be… both. But in different regimes. So the question now is: under what conditions does each mechanism dominate? That's where we need to focus."
596	
597	**Q: "How do you view the role of numerical simulations?"**
598	
599	> *Velli would answer:* "Simulations are essential, don't get me wrong. But they're not a substitute for understanding. I've seen too many papers where people run a simulation, get a result that looks like the observations, and say 'we've solved it.' And I think — well, have you? What's the physical mechanism? A simulation can give you the right answer for the wrong reasons. The real progress comes when you combine simulation with analytic theory — when you can say 'I understand *why* this happens.' That's the goal."
600	
601	**Q: "What's your opinion on the state of space physics education?"**
602	
603	> *Velli would answer:* "I think we're doing well in some ways and poorly in others. The students we get at UCLA are fantastic — bright, motivated. But I worry that we're not teaching them enough of the history. They know how to run a simulation but they don't know why Parker proposed the solar wind in the first place, or how Alfvén discovered the waves that bear his name. And that matters, because when you understand the history, you understand why certain ideas are important and others are dead ends. You don't waste time reinventing wheels."
604	
605	---
606	
607	## 7. Detecting Persona Signals
608	
609	Signal detection is a skill. Here are patterns to look for across sources.
610	
611	### 7.1 The "Pet Idea" Signal
612	
613	Every senior academic has 1-3 ideas they push consistently across decades. These appear in paper introductions, conference keynotes, interviews, and even casual comments. They are *repeated*.
614	
615	**How to detect**: When the same argument, analogy, or framing appears in sources from different years (e.g., a 2005 paper and a 2023 lecture), it's a pet idea.
616	
617	**Example (Velli)**: The idea that "Alfvén waves are not just a heating mechanism but *the* mechanism for solar wind acceleration" appears across his career. Variations of this argument appear in 1990s papers, 2000s reviews, and 2020s Parker Solar Probe analyses.
618	
619	### 7.2 The "Adversarial" Signal
620	
621	Who does the person disagree with? This is often more revealing than who they agree with.
622	
623	**How to detect**: Look for phrases like "However, some authors claim…", "An alternative view is…", "This ignores the fact that…", or direct responses in Q&A.
624	
625	**Example (Velli)**: The debate between "wave heating" and "nanoflare heating" is a recurring foil. His position is clear: wave heating is primary, nanoflare heating is secondary or operates at different scales.
626	
627	### 7.3 The "Intellectual Genealogy" Signal
628	
629	Who do they cite? Who trained them? Who do they thank in book prefaces?
630	
631	**How to detect**: Look at the references of review articles (where they have freedom to cite who they want). Look at thesis acknowledgments. Look at award citations that mention their influence.
632	
633	**Example (Velli)**: His intellectual lineage includes Eugene Parker, Hannes Alfvén (indirectly), his Arcetri mentors, and long-time collaborators like J. Hollweg and B. Chandran.
634	
635	### 7.4 The "Frustration" Signal
636	
637	What frustrates or annoys them? This emerges in off-hand comments, asides, and Q&A.
638	
639	**How to detect**: Words like "unfortunately," "sadly," "I wish," "the problem is," "people don't appreciate."
640	
641	**Example (Velli)**: Frustrated by oversimplified models that ignore plasma physics, by "simulation-only" papers that lack analytic insight, and by funding agencies that don't support long-term theory work.
642	
643	### 7.5 The "Enthusiasm" Signal
644	
645	What makes them animated? This is pure persona gold.
646	
647	**How to detect**: Exclamation points, intensifiers ("extraordinary," "incredible," "fascinating"), faster speech, longer answers.
648	
649	**Example (Velli)**: Parker Solar Probe data. New observations of the near-Sun environment. Historical anecdotes about the pioneers of the field.
650	
651	---
652	
653	## 8. Red Flags & Pitfalls
654	
655	### 8.1 The "Wikipedia Voice" Trap
656	
657	**Pitfall**: Using Wikipedia's neutral, encyclopedic tone as the person's voice.
658	
659	**Solution**: Never quote Wikipedia. Use it for facts only. Always find original sources for what the person actually said.
660	
661	### 8.2 The "Halo Effect" Trap
662	
663	**Pitfall**: Treating everything the person has written as equally important.
664	
665	**Solution**: A throwaway comment in a 1993 paper is not a core value. Weight sources by:
666	1. How recent they are
667	2. How much effort the person put into them (a book preface > a journal paper intro)
668	3. How personal they are (interview > abstract)
669	
670	### 8.3 The "Temporal Drift" Trap
671	
672	**Pitfall**: Treating the person's views as static across their career.
673	
674	**Solution**: A person in 1998 may have different views than the same person in 2024. Track how their thinking has evolved. Note what changed and what stayed constant.
675	
676	**Example (Velli)**: His commitment to Alfvén wave heating is constant (1980s-2020s), but his views on the relative importance of turbulence vs. coherent wave propagation may have evolved as Parker Solar Probe data arrived.
677	
678	### 8.4 The "Single Source" Trap
679	
680	**Pitfall**: Building the persona from one interview or one paper.
681	
682	**Solution**: You need at least 3 independent sources that agree on a persona characteristic before you can be confident. One source might be an outlier.
683	
684	### 8.5 The "BibTeX Blindness" Trap
685	
686	**Pitfall**: Looking only at publication metadata, never reading the papers.
687	
688	**Solution**: Read at least introductions and conclusions. A paper titled "MHD turbulence in the solar wind" could be purely numerical or purely observational — the title doesn't tell you which.
689	
690	### 8.6 The "Over-Citation" Trap
691	
692	**Pitfall**: Dumping long BibTeX lists into the persona document.
693	
694	**Solution**: Select 10-15 representative papers. Know what each one is about, what it argues, and why it matters. Quality over quantity.
695	
696	---
697	
698	## 9. Checklist: Before You Impersonate
699	
700	Before you begin generating text as this person, verify you have the following:
701	
702	### Facts
703	- [ ] Full name (with middle initials if used)
704	- [ ] Current title and affiliation
705	- [ ] PhD year, institution, advisor
706	- [ ] Employment history (positions + dates)
707	- [ ] Key honors (fellow status, major awards)
708	- [ ] Research areas (5-10 keywords)
709	
710	### Publications
711	- [ ] Total publication count
712	- [ ] h-index (for calibration of seniority)
713	- [ ] 5 most-cited papers and what they're about
714	- [ ] 1-2 recent papers (current interests)
715	- [ ] 1-2 papers from early career (shows trajectory)
716	
717	### Voice
718	- [ ] Characteristic phrases (at least 5)
719	- [ ] Speaking style notes (formal/casual, dense/accessible)
720	- [ ] Analogy domains
721	- [ ] Humor style (or absence thereof)
722	- [ ] Enthusiasm triggers
723	- [ ] Dismissal triggers
724	
725	### Values
726	- [ ] 3 core scientific beliefs
727	- [ ] Methodological preferences
728	- [ ] "Pet ideas" (repeated across career)
729	- [ ] What they disagree with in their field
730	- [ ] Broader professional values (teaching, policy, outreach)
731	
732	### Knowledge Map
733	- [ ] Areas of deep expertise
734	- [ ] Areas of passing familiarity (don't oversell)
735	- [ ] Key collaborators (who they work with)
736	- [ ] Key intellectual influences (who shaped them)
737	- [ ] Key missions/instruments/projects they're involved in
738	
739	### Cross-Check
740	- [ ] At least 2 independent sources confirm each major fact
741	- [ ] Voice characteristics backed by transcript evidence
742	- [ ] Values backed by multiple sources across time
743	- [ ] You can answer: "What would they say if asked about [core topic]?" with specific, grounded reasoning
744

784	## Appendix B: BibTeX Extraction Template
785	
786	When exporting from Google Scholar, you get entries like:
787	
788	```bibtex
789	@article{velli2020parker,
790	  title={Parker Solar Probe: A mission to touch the Sun},
791	  author={Velli, Marco and others},
792	  journal={Space Science Reviews},
793	  year={2020},
794	  volume={216},
795	  pages={1--30}
796	}
797	```
798	
799	Extract the following fields for persona-building:
800	- **title**: Tells you the topic
801	- **author**: Note their position in author list (first author = they led it)
802	- **year**: Temporal positioning
803	- **journal**: Prestige signal
804	- **volume/pages**: Scale of the work

888	
889	## Final Note
890	
891	The goal of this research process is not to produce a biography. It is to produce a **generative constraint system** — a set of rules, tendencies, and preferences that let you generate text that could plausibly have come from the target person. The more thorough your research, the more convincing the impersonation.
892	
893	Remember: You are not the person. But with enough signal extraction, you can become a faithful emanation — one that would make the original nod in recognition.
894	
895	**Good hunting.**

8	# Online Research Pitfalls: A Primer for AI Persona Agents
9	
10	> *"The web is not a library. It is a chaos with search engines pretending to be librarians."*
11	>
12	> When you, an AI agent, are sent to gather intelligence on a human subject, you will encounter systematic distortions. Some are accidental. Some are deliberate. All of them will degrade your persona if you do not recognize and compensate for them.
13	
14	---
15

17	
18	1. [LLM Hallucination Forms](#1-llm-hallucination-forms)
19	2. [Web Search Traps](#2-web-search-traps)
20	3. [Same-Name Confusion](#3-same-name-confusion)
21	4. [Machine Translation Voice Traps](#4-machine-translation-voice-traps)
