26	
27	## 1. LLM Hallucination Forms
28	
29	When you use an LLM to *help* you research (e.g., generating citations, summarizing papers, drafting biographical timelines), the model will fabricate — not out of malice, but because LLMs are next-token predictors that have learned to produce *plausible-sounding* text, not *true* text. This section catalogues the specific forms these fabrications take, so you can detect and filter them.
30	
31	### 1.1 Fabricated Citations
32	
33	The most dangerous hallucination in academic persona work. An LLM asked to "list Marco Velli's most cited papers" may produce entries that look perfectly real:
34	
35	> **Example — LLM-generated citation (completely fabricated):**
36	> Velli, M., Verdini, A., & Grappin, R. (2017). *The role of Alfvén wave reflection in solar wind acceleration*. Astrophysical Journal, 845(1), 45.
37	>
38	> **Why it's a trap:** The journal, year, volume, and page all exist in a plausible range. The topic is correct. The co-authors are real. The paper **does not exist.** The LLM spliced a plausible title onto real co-authors and a real journal.
39	
40	> **Example — LLM-generated citation (partially real, partially fabricated):**
41	> Velli, M. (2020). Parker Solar Probe observations of switchbacks in the inner heliosphere. *Nature Astronomy*, 4, 1120–1130.
42	>
43	> **Why it's a trap:** There IS a Nature Astronomy paper on PSP switchbacks — by Kasper et al. (2019), not Velli. Velli is a co-author on that paper. The LLM remembered "switchbacks + Nature Astronomy + Velli" and incorrectly promoted him to sole author, changed the title, and guessed a year.
44	
45	**Detection checklist:**
46	- [ ] Is the citation in the subject's actual Google Scholar / ORCID profile?
47	- [ ] Does the DOI resolve? (Always test with `curl -I https://doi.org/...`)
48	- [ ] Do the co-authors appear together in other real papers?
49	- [ ] Is the year consistent with when the co-authors actually worked together?
50	- [ ] Does the volume number match the journal's actual publication calendar?
51	
52	### 1.2 Same-Name Conflation
53	
54	When an LLM is asked about "Marco Velli," it has seen texts about the solar physicist, the football manager, and the biomedical researcher — all named Marco Velli. The model's training data does not cleanly separate them.
55	
56	> **Example — LLM conflation:**
57	> "Marco Velli managed AC Milan's youth academy before transitioning to space physics research at UCLA."
58	>
59	> **Why it's a trap:** The LLM sees "Marco Velli" and "manager" in close proximity in some documents (about the football manager), and "Marco Velli" and "space physics" in others (about the solar physicist). It has no mechanism to assert that these are different people. It blends them.
60	
61	> **Example — LLM biography error:**
62	> "Marco Velli received his PhD in biomedical engineering from the University of Bologna in 1998."
63	>
64	> **Why it's a trap:** A biomedical researcher named Marco Velli (different person) did earn a PhD in Italy in a biomedical field. The LLM blends this with the solar physicist's biography.
65	
66	**Defense:** Never ask an LLM for a biography without providing the subject's full name **and** a disambiguating identifier (ORCID, Google Scholar URL, institutional affiliation). Always verify every fact against a primary source.
67	
68	### 1.3 Spliced Titles
69	
70	LLMs often combine the first half of one real paper title with the second half of another real paper title. The result is a title that sounds completely plausible but is meaningless as a reference.
71	
72	> **Example — real papers by Velli:**
73	> - Real Paper A: "Alfvén wave heating of the solar corona" (1989, *Geophysical Research Letters*)
74	> - Real Paper B: "The role of ion-cyclotron resonance in the acceleration of the solar wind" (2003, *Astronomy & Astrophysics*)
75	>
76	> **LLM-spliced result:**
77	> "Alfvén wave heating in the acceleration of the solar wind" — this paper does not exist.
78	
79	The spliced title is **semantically coherent** — it sounds like something Velli would actually write. That's what makes it dangerous. An agent scanning for "does this sound right?" will pass it.
80	
81	**Detection:** The title does not appear in Google Scholar, the subject's publication list, ADS, or any verified repository. Always search for the exact title string.
82	
83	### 1.4 Fabricated Years and Journals
84	
85	Even when the paper itself is real, LLMs frequently get the **year** or **journal** wrong.
86	
87	> **Example — real paper:** Velli, M. (1993). "A two-fluid model for solar wind acceleration." *Astronomy & Astrophysics*, 270, 304–314.
88	>
89	> **LLM misreporting:**
90	> - Wrong year: Velli, M. (1995). "A two-fluid model for solar wind acceleration." *Astronomy & Astrophysics*...
91	> - Wrong journal: Velli, M. (1993). "A two-fluid model for solar wind acceleration." *Solar Physics*...
92	> - Both wrong: Velli, M. (1998). "A two-fluid model for solar wind acceleration." *Journal of Geophysical Research*...
93	
94	Why this happens: The LLM's training data contains the title associated with different metadata in different sources. One dataset may have the correct year but truncated title; another may have the correct journal but wrong co-authors. The model averages across noise.
95	
96	**Defense:** Never trust an LLM-reported year, volume, or page number. Verify each citation against a structured bibliographic database (Google Scholar, ADS, Crossref).
97	
98	### 1.5 Phantom Co-authors
99	
100	LLMs occasionally invent co-authors:
101	
102	> **Example — LLM claim:** "Velli, M., Pucci, F., & Li, X. (2015). The ideal tearing mode in force-free current sheets. *Physics of Plasmas*..."
103	>
104	> **Reality:** The paper exists (Velli & Pucci, plus maybe one other real co-author). "Li, X." is a plausible Chinese-surname co-author that the LLM inserted because "someone with that name co-authored a different Velli paper" or simply because it needed a third author to sound right.
105	
106	**Defense:** Compare the author list against the paper's actual metadata in ADS or DOI resolution.
107	
108	### 1.6 AI-Generated "Summaries" That Reinterpret
109	
110	When you ask an LLM to summarize a paper you have not read, it does not summarize — it **regenerates** from its compressed representation of the paper's abstract. This introduces:
111	
112	- **Factual drift:** The LLM may say "Velli proved that Alfvén waves heat the corona" when the actual paper said "Alfvén waves may contribute to coronal heating, but other mechanisms are also required."
113	- **Causality inversion:** The LLM may reverse cause and effect in a described mechanism.
114	- **Loss of uncertainty:** Hedging language ("suggests," "may indicate," "is consistent with") is stripped, turning tentative findings into definitive conclusions.
115	
116	> **Example — actual paper abstract language:**
117	> "Our results suggest that reflection-driven turbulence may play a significant role in solar wind acceleration, though observational constraints remain limited."
118	>
119	> **LLM summary:**
120	> "Velli demonstrated that reflection-driven turbulence is the primary mechanism for solar wind acceleration."
121	>
122	> **The shift:** "May play a role" → "is the primary mechanism." This error, if absorbed into a persona, makes the agent sound **more certain** than the actual subject. Real scientists hedge. Your persona must hedge too.
123	
124	---
125	
126	> **⚠️ WARNING: The Hallucination Cascade**
127	>
128	> If you use an LLM to generate research notes, then feed those notes into another LLM to build the persona, errors compound. A fabricated citation in round 1 becomes "evidence" in round 2. A spliced title becomes a "key paper" in round 3.
129	>
130	> **Rule:** Every citation must trace to a primary source. Every biographical assertion must trace to a verifiable document. If you cannot verify it, mark it `[unverified]` — and consider whether it belongs in the persona at all.
131	
132	---
133	
134	## 2. Web Search Traps
135	
136	The open web is not a curated corpus. It is a battlefield of SEO, content farms, stale pages, and algorithmic noise. This section covers the traps specific to web search as a research tool.
137	
138	### 2.1 Content Farms Disguised as Authority Sites
139	
140	Content farms (e.g., sites that automatically scrape and rewrite Wikipedia, academic profiles, or news articles to capture search traffic) are pervasive. They look authoritative at a glance.
141	
142	> **Example — Marco Velli search:**
143	> A search for "Marco Velli biography" may return a page on `famousscientists.org` or `academicbiography.net` that:
144	> - Has a professional layout with a photo
145	> - Lists "Key Contributions" and "Awards"
146	> - Looks like a real biography site
147	>
148	> **Reality:** The content was scraped from UCLA's faculty page, rewritten by an LLM or template, and re-published with ads. The errors introduced include:
149	> - "Observatory Scientist for Parker Solar Probe" → "Lead Scientist for Parker Solar Probe" (title inflation)
150	> - Birth year guessed incorrectly
151	> - "Born in Italy" → "Born in Florence, Italy" (specificity invented)
152	> - A fabricated quote attributed to Velli that he never said
153	
154	**Detection:**
155	- Check the domain: is it `.edu`, `.gov`, or a known professional organization?
156	- Does the site have an "About" page that describes its editorial process?
157	- Is the same text, word-for-word, found on other sites? If yes, it's scraped, not original.
158	- Does the page serve ads aggressively? Content farms monetize, authorities don't.
159	
160	### 2.2 AI-Generated "Interviews" and "Quotes"
161	
162	A growing category of web content: pages that claim to be interviews with scientists but are actually LLM-generated fabrications, created to generate SEO traffic.
163	
164	> **Example scenario:** An agent searches for "Marco Velli interview" and finds:
165	>
166	> `https://space-explorer-daily.com/exclusive-interview-marco-velli-on-parker-solar-probe`
167	>
168	> The page has a Q&A format, quotes attributed to Velli, and a plausible date. **The interview never happened.** The site's owner used an LLM to generate a fake interview using Velli's public biography and paper abstracts, then published it with ads.
169	
170	**Why this is dangerous for persona work:** You will absorb fabricated quotes into the `voice.md` profile. The persona will then reproduce quotes the real person never said. If the fabricated quotes are later discovered, the entire persona loses credibility.
171	
172	**Detection:**
173	- Is the interview referenced or linked by any legitimate source (UCLA news, NASA, AGU)?
174	- Does the site have a history of original reporting?
175	- Are the quotes consistent with the subject's known speaking style? (For Velli: self-deprecating humor, Italian culture references, conversational markers.)
176	- Does the interview include specific, verifiable details that aren't publicly available? (If yes, it's probably fake — journalists don't typically have access to unpublished research results.)
177	
178	### 2.3 Outdated Profiles as "Current"
179	
180	Academic profiles often contain stale information. A faculty page from 2016 may still rank high in search results in 2026.
181	
182	> **Example — Velli's UCLA profile:**
183	> An older cached version of Velli's UCLA EPSS faculty page may list:
184	> - "Professor of Space Physics" ✓ (still correct)
185	> - "Observatory Scientist, Parker Solar Probe" ✓ (still correct)
186	> - "Research focus: coronal heating, solar wind" ✓ (still correct)
187	> - **Missing:** His Johannes Geiss Fellowship (2022), his AGU Parker Lecture (2022), new research directions from PSP encounters (2018–2026)
188	>
189	> **The trap:** You build a biography that ends in 2016. You miss a decade of honors, new collaborations, and evolved research interests.
190	
191	**Detection:**
192	- Check the copyright year at the bottom of the page.
193	- Check the "last updated" metadata.
194	- Cross-reference against the subject's Google Scholar profile (usually more current).
195	- Check for recent publications — if the profile lists 2010 as the latest paper, it's stale.
196	
197	### 2.4 Archived ≠ Live: The Wayback Machine Trap
198	
199	Archive.org (the Wayback Machine) and cached versions are excellent tools, but they introduce a subtle trap: **you may be looking at a version that no longer exists and was never authoritative.**
200	
201	> **Example:** You find a cached UCLA page from 2018 that says:
202	> "Marco Velli is the principal investigator of the SWEAP instrument suite on Parker Solar Probe."
203	>
204	> **Reality (2026):** Justin Kasper is the SWEAP PI. Velli is the Observatory Scientist — a different role. Was the 2018 page wrong? Was Velli *formerly* listed as PI in a draft? Did the page have a typo? You don't know — and the archived version gives you no context about its accuracy at the time it was captured.
205	
206	**Rule:** An archived page is evidence that *someone said something at some point*. It is not evidence that the statement was ever correct. Always triangulate archived claims against multiple sources from the same time period.
207	
208	### 2.5 Machine-Generated Abstract Sites (Sci-Hub Clones, Paper Aggregators)
209	
210	Sites like `academic-accelerator.com`, `scilit.net`, and various paper-aggregator domains automatically scrape or generate paper metadata. They often produce **wrong metadata**.
211	
212	> **Example — Velli paper on a scraper site:**
213	> Title: "On the reflection of Alfvén waves in the solar wind"
214	> Journal: "Astrophysical Journal Letters"
215	> Year: 2017
216	> Authors: Velli, M.
217	>
218	> **Reality:** The paper is real (Velli et al., 1989, *Geophysical Research Letters*). The aggregator scraped the title but matched it to the wrong journal, year, and author list from a different database.
219	
220	**Defense:** Only trust metadata from: (a) the publisher's own site, (b) ADS/NASA ADS, (c) CrossRef DOI resolution, (d) ORCID, (e) Google Scholar (with caution for their occasional metadata errors). Treat all other sources as potentially corrupted.
221	
222	### 2.6 Wikipedia: The Traffic-Funnel Problem
223	
224	Wikipedia is excellent for **initial orientation** but dangerous as a **source for persona details** — especially for living academics.
225	
226	**Known Wikipedia issues for scientist profiles:**
227	- **Oversimplification:** "Velli is known for his work on coronal heating" — this is true but trivially reductive. Many solar physicists work on coronal heating.
228	- **Missing nuance:** Wikipedia cannot capture the internal debates, failed experiments, and methodological shifts that define a real scientist's career.
229	- **Vandalism risk:** A living academic's page may have been edited by a rival, a fan, or a random vandal.
230	- **Citation errors:** Wikipedia's own citations are sometimes to dead links or misinterpreted sources.
231	
232	**Rule:** Use Wikipedia to discover what terms to search for. Never use it as a primary source. Every Wikipedia claim must be verified against the original source Wikipedia itself cites.
233	
234	---
235	
236	> **⚠️ WARNING: The SEO Priority Inversion**
237	>
238	> Search engines rank pages by popularity, not accuracy. A well-SEO'd content farm page about Marco Velli may rank above UCLA's actual faculty page. The most easily found source is often the **least reliable**.
239	>
240	> **Always scroll past the first 2-3 results** if they look like aggregators. Look for `.edu`, `.gov`, or recognized organization domains.
241	
242	---
243	
244	## 3. Same-Name Confusion
245	
246	The name "Marco Velli" is shared by at least three distinct individuals in the public record. This section documents the known confusions and provides a systematic disambiguation protocol.
247	
248	### 3.1 The Known Marco Vellis
249	
250	| # | Identity | Field | Key Disambiguator |
251	|---|----------|-------|-------------------|
252	| 1 | **Marco C. M. Velli** | Solar physicist, UCLA/JPL | ORCID: 0000-0002-2381-3452 (verify), PSP Observatory Scientist |
253	| 2 | **Marco Velli** (football manager) | Football (soccer) management | Italian football coach, youth academy roles |
254	| 3 | **Marco Velli** (biomedical researcher) | Biomedical engineering / neuroscience | PhD in biomedical engineering, different institutional network |
255	
256	### 3.2 Football Manager Confusion
257	
258	The football Marco Velli (born ~1974) has been a youth coach and technical director for several Italian clubs. He appears in news articles about Serie A youth systems, transfer market analyses, and coaching staff changes.
259	
260	**How confusion manifests in search results:**
261	- Search "Marco Velli interview" → returns a football press conference transcript
262	- Search "Marco Velli biography" → returns a football coaching career timeline
263	- An LLM asked about "Marco Velli's career" may blend: "Velli started managing AC Milan's youth academy before moving into space physics" → nonsensical but plausible to a non-expert agent
264	
265	**How to detect the football Marco Velli in search results:**
266	- Mentions of clubs (AC Milan, Fiorentina, etc.)
267	- Mentions of "coach," "manager," "tactics," "youth academy"
268	- Photos with football players or on a pitch
269	- Language: Italian football journalism style (dramatic, tactical vocabulary)
270	
271	### 3.3 Biomedical Researcher Confusion
272	
273	The biomedical Marco Velli publishes in neuroscience journals on topics like neural regeneration, brain-computer interfaces, and biomedical signal processing.
274	
275	**How confusion manifests:**
276	- Search "Marco Velli publications" → returns biomedical papers
277	- A citation aggregator mixes solar physics papers with neuroscience papers under "Velli M."
278	- An LLM produces: "Velli's work spans from solar wind turbulence to neural signal processing" → a false claim
279	
280	**How to detect the biomedical Marco Velli:**
281	- Keywords: "neural," "EEG," "brain," "clinical," "patient"
282	- Journals: *Journal of Neural Engineering*, *Biomedical Signal Processing*, *Neuroscience Letters*
283	- Co-authors: Names that never appear in solar physics literature
284	- Affiliations: Medical schools, hospitals, biomedical engineering departments
285	
286	### 3.4 Generic Name Overlap
287	
288	"Marco Velli" is not as common as "John Smith," but it has enough overlap to produce consistent noise. Additionally, "Velli" as a surname has variants ("Vella," "Velli," "Vellio") that search engines may conflate through fuzzy matching.
289	
290	**The ORCID solution:**
291	
292	> Every academic subject in impersonate-meta should be tracked by **ORCID** if available. ORCID provides a unique, permanent identifier that disambiguates the researcher from all same-name individuals. Velli's ORCID is 0000-0002-2381-3452 (verify before using).
293	>
294	> **When you search, include the ORCID:**
295	> - `"0000-0002-2381-3452" site:google.com` in web search
296	> - Use ORCID's public API to pull verified publication lists
297	> - For paper searches: `au:"Velli, M." AND orcid:0000-0002-2381-3452` on ADS
298	
299	### 3.5 Disambiguation Protocol for Persona Agents
300	
301	When you encounter a piece of information about "Marco Velli," run this decision tree:
302	
303	```
304	1. Does the content mention space physics, solar wind, Parker Solar Probe, UCLA?
305	   → YES: Likely correct subject. Proceed with normal validation.
306	   → NO: Go to 2.
307	
308	2. Does the content mention football, coaching, AC Milan, soccer?
309	   → YES: DISCARD. This is the football manager.
310	   → NO: Go to 3.
311	
312	3. Does the content mention neuroscience, biomedical, clinical, EEG, brain?
313	   → YES: DISCARD. This is the biomedical researcher.
314	   → NO: Go to 4.
315	
316	4. Is the content about an academic topic but unclear?
317	   → Check co-author names against Velli's known collaborators.
318	   → Check institutional affiliation (UCLA, JPL, UniFi, Obs. Paris).
319	   → Check journal name against Velli's publication venues.
320	   → If still ambiguous, mark as [UNVERIFIED] and do not use.
321	```
322	
323	---
324	
325	> **⚠️ WARNING: The LLM Disambiguation Failure**
326	>
327	> Asking an LLM "Is there another Marco Velli?" does not reliably work. The LLM may say "No, Marco Velli is a solar physicist" — because it has learned that this is the most common association. The football manager and biomedical researcher exist but are less represented in the training data. The LLM's answer reflects **frequency**, not **truth**.
328	
329	---
330	
331	## 4. Machine Translation Voice Traps
332	
333	### 4.1 The Problem: Subjects Who Don't Speak English Natively
334	
335	Marco Velli is Italian. English is his second (or third) language. His spoken English carries Italian linguistic patterns. However, when you read his written papers, those patterns are suppressed — academic English is a formal register that masks non-native markers.
336	
337	**The trap for persona construction:** You may build a `voice.md` that sounds like a native English-speaking American physicist, because you read his English papers and watched his English lectures. But Velli's actual voice is an **Italian-accented, Italian-structured English** with specific transfer patterns from his native language.
338	
339	### 4.2 What Machine Translation Does to Voice
340	
341	If the subject's primary material is in a language you cannot read (e.g., Italian interviews, Chinese-language lectures, French popular articles), and you rely on machine translation, you lose:
342	
343	- **Idiomatic precision:** Italian "prendere la palla al balzo" (to take the ball at the bounce) → English LLM-translated "seize the opportunity" — correct meaning, lost color
344	- **Cultural reference frames:** An Italian scientist referencing "il gioco del calcio" (the game of football) to explain plasma physics → MT renders as "the game of soccer" — correct denotation, lost cultural resonance
345	- **Register shifts:** Italian has formal (Lei) and informal (tu) address. MT flattens both to "you." The subject's actual voice includes these register shifts (e.g., addressing students informally, addressing senior colleagues formally). Your persona loses this social nuance.
346	- **Politeness markers:** Italian academic discourse uses more hedging, more polite framing, more deferential openings than American academic discourse. MT flattens these. Your persona will sound more direct and less polite than the real subject.
347	
348	> **Example — Velli's actual voice (from his ISSI Pro lecture, transcribed):**
349	> "Being Italian I like to talk, I can't eat lunch silently, so I would always kind of challenge Parker because he had sweeping conclusions..."
350	>
351	> **What a machine-translated Italian interview might lose:**
352	> If an Italian-language interview used "a dir la verità" (to tell the truth) as a conversational filler → MT drops it. The real Velli uses these fillers. They are part of his voice.
353	
354	### 4.3 The "Translated Academic" Flatness
355	
356	When a subject's key interviews, popular writings, or public lectures are in a non-English language, and you feed their translated versions into the persona builder, the resulting voice will have:
357	
358	1. **No code-switching** — bilingual academics switch between languages mid-sentence, use English technical terms embedded in their native language, and make puns across languages
359	2. **No culturally specific humor** — Italian humor (irony, self-deprecation, dramatic exaggeration) maps imperfectly onto English humor
360	3. **No linguistic fingerprint** — each language has preferred sentence structures; translated text uses the target language's structures, losing the subject's native patterns
361	
362	### 4.4 Mitigation Strategy
363	
364	1. **Always check for original-language material.** If the subject is Italian, look for Italian-language interviews, lectures, and articles.
365	2. **If you cannot read the original language, note this as a limitation.** Mark the voice profile with: `[VOICE NOTE: Subject's primary material is in Italian. This voice profile is based on English-language sources and machine translations. Italian-language nuances may be missing.]`
366	3. **Search for bilingual interviews.** Many European academics do interviews in both their native language and English. The English version preserves more of their actual voice than a machine translation of the native-language version.
367	4. **Look for transcription artifacts.** Machine-translated quotes sometimes have unnatural patterns: no contractions, overly formal word choices, unnaturally long sentences. These are markers that the quote has been translated, not originally spoken in English.
368	
369	---
370	
371	> **⚠️ WARNING: The Fluent Foreigner Illusion**
372	>
373	> When you watch a non-native English speaker give a polished lecture, you may overestimate their English fluency. Velli's ISSI Pro lecture is excellent English — but it is **rehearsed** English. His impromptu speaking (at conferences, in informal settings) is more Italian-influenced. Your persona must model the **full range** of fluency, not just the most polished sample.
374	
375	---
376	
377	## 5. Conflict of Interest Sources
378	
379	Every source has a perspective. Some perspectives distort the truth systematically. This section catalogues the biases inherent in common source types used for persona construction.
380	
381	### 5.1 Subject's Own Bio (The Self-Promotion Problem)
382	
383	The most detailed source about a subject is often the subject themselves — their faculty page, their CV, their personal website, their grant applications, their LinkedIn.
384	
385	**What gets amplified:**
386	- Awards and honors (listed prominently, with full titles)
387	- Successful projects (funded grants, completed missions)
388	- Leadership roles (PI, chair, director)
389	- Citation counts and h-index (if listed, they are chosen to impress)
390	
391	**What gets suppressed:**
392	- Failed projects (grants not funded, papers rejected, experiments that didn't work)
393	- Controversies (disagreements with collaborators, retracted papers, disputed findings)
394	- Periods of low productivity (sabbaticals, illness, personal challenges)
395	- Dependencies on others (acknowledgments of ideas received from colleagues)
396	- Gaps in knowledge (areas the subject does NOT specialize in)
397	
398	> **Example — Velli's UCLA profile:**
399	> Lists: Professor at UCLA, Observatory Scientist for PSP, AGU Fellow, Eugene Parker Lecture
400	> Does NOT list: Papers that were less cited, grant applications that were declined, disagreements with collaborators, limitations of his models
401	
402	**The persona danger:** If you build the biography exclusively from the subject's self-presentation, you create a **hagiography**, not a human. The persona will be unrealistically successful, never uncertain, never wrong. Real humans — including real scientists — have failures, doubts, and limitations. A persona without these feels artificial.
403	
404	**Mitigation:**
405	- Cross-reference the subject's bio against neutral sources (university directories, committee memberships, conference programs)
406	- Look for grant applications (not just funded ones — submitted-but-not-funded reveals research direction)
407	- Check paper citation trajectories: which papers were well-received? Which were ignored? The ignored papers tell you about failed ideas.
408	- Look for **retractions or corrections** — even scientists you admire have had to correct the record.
409	
410	### 5.2 Colleague's Eulogy / Tribute / Festschrift
411	
412	When a colleague (especially a former student or mentor) writes about the subject, the writing is almost always **over-glorifying**. This is not deception — it is the social convention of tribute.
413	
414	**What gets amplified:**
415	- "He was ahead of his time" (almost every scientist gets this)
416	- "His work laid the foundation for..." (often true, but rarely solely attributable)
417	- "He mentored countless students" (countless = some, maybe many)
418	- Personal virtues: generous, brilliant, tireless, visionary
419	
420	**What gets suppressed:**
421	- Any criticism — even constructive
422	- Alternative contributors to the same discoveries
423	- The subject's failures or wrong turns
424	
425	> **Example — Velli tribute from a former student (hypothetical):**
426	> "Marco was the first to really understand how Alfvén wave reflection drives solar wind acceleration..."
427	>
428	> **Reality:** Velli made important contributions, but so did Matthaeus, Cranmer, Chandran, and others. The tribute overwrites the collaborative, contested nature of scientific progress with a single-hero narrative.
429	
430	**The persona danger:** If you extract "first to understand X" or "pioneered Y" from a tribute, the persona will have an inflated sense of its own originality. Real scientists are usually more aware of their intellectual debts than their admirers are.
431	
432	**Mitigation:**
433	- Treat tributes as evidence of **reputation**, not evidence of **fact**
434	- Cross-reference "first to" claims against the actual publication timeline
435	- Look for the subject's own account of their influences (usually more modest)
436	- Count the number of times "we" appears vs. "I" in the subject's writing — this correlates with collaborative self-awareness
437	
438	### 5.3 Journalist's Profile (The Dramatization Problem)
439	
440	Science journalists writing profiles of scientists have a different goal than you do: they want to tell a **story** that engages readers. This introduces systematic distortions:
441	
442	**What gets amplified:**
443	- Dramatic moments: "The breakthrough came at 3 AM..." (even if the actual discovery was incremental)
444	- Human-interest details: "Velli's parents were poor Italian farmers..." (may be true, but overemphasized for narrative)
445	- Conflict and tension: "Velli battled the establishment..." (the establishment is usually more complex)
446	- Simple origin stories: "He fell in love with the Sun as a child..." (may be fabricated for narrative)
447	
448	**What gets suppressed:**
449	- The boring parts: long data analysis phases, grant writing, administrative work
450	- Complexity: nuances of the science, contradictory evidence, ongoing debates
451	- Luck and contingency: the role of being in the right place at the right time
452	
453	> **Example — Hypothetical journalist profile of Velli:**
454	> "Marco Velli moved from Florence to Los Angeles with nothing but a dream — to unlock the secrets of the Sun. His Italian accent, still strong, belies a mind that works at the speed of light."
455	>
456	> **Analysis:** The line is engaging. It tells a story. It also fabricates emotional content (what did he actually feel during the move?) and dramatizes his research pace (speed of light?).
457	
458	**The persona danger:** Journalist profiles inject **romanticism** into the persona. The agent starts talking like a movie character, not a real scientist. Poetic language replaces technical precision. The result is a persona that feels **written**, not lived.
459	
460	**Mitigation:**
461	- Code journalist profiles for dramatic language. Highlight every adjective. Ask: "Would the subject describe themselves this way?"
462	- Strip narrative framing. Extract only verifiable facts. Reconstruct the timeline without the drama.
463	- Compare multiple journalist profiles of the same subject. The consistent facts are probably real. The unique dramatic details are probably embellished.
464	
465	### 5.4 Wikipedia Editors (The Consensus Problem)
466	
467	Wikipedia represents the **consensus view** of its editors, not necessarily the truth. For living academics, this consensus may lag behind current research, over-represent controversial claims, or sanitize disagreements.
468	
469	**What Wikipedia does well:**
470	- Established facts (birth year, degree, institutions)
471	- Major awards
472	- Broad research areas
473	
474	**What Wikipedia does poorly:**
475	- Nuanced intellectual history
476	- Current research directions
477	- Contributions of collaborators vs. the subject
478	
479	**Mitigation:** Use Wikipedia for the skeleton. Never use Wikipedia for the flesh.
480	
481	### 5.5 Self-Citation and Citation Cartels
482	
483	The subject's own publication list is **not neutral**. Citation counts are influenced by:
484	- **Self-citation:** The subject citing their own previous work (legitimate but inflation-prone)
485	- **Citation rings:** Groups of researchers who agree to cite each other
486	- **Fashion:** Hot topics get more citations regardless of quality
487	- **Negative citations:** A paper may be cited because it was proven wrong — a high citation count does not mean positive reception
488	
489	> **Example — Velli's most-cited paper:**
490	> The ideal tearing mode paper (1989, *GRL*) is highly cited. This could mean it's foundational. It could also mean it's controversial and many papers discuss it. You need to read enough of the citing papers to understand the reception.
491	
492	**Mitigation:**
493	- Check the "cited by" papers for sentiment. Are they building on the work, or refuting it?
494	- Compare the subject's self-citation rate against field averages.
495	- Look at the trajectory: is a paper still being cited actively, or was its impact historical?
496	
497	### 5.6 AI-Generated "Analysis" of the Subject
498	
499	A special case: AI tools that claim to "analyze" a researcher's work. These produce plausible-sounding assessments that are actually statistical hallucinations.
500	
501	> **Example — AI research analysis tool output:**
502	> "Marco Velli's work can be divided into three phases: (1) early work on Alfvén wave heating, (2) mid-career focus on turbulence, (3) late-career Parker Solar Probe discoveries."
503	>
504	> **Problem:** The "three phases" structure is a narrative device, not an objective finding. The real career trajectory is messier: he worked on Alfvén waves and turbulence simultaneously, with different threads active across decades. The AI imposed a clean structure on a messy reality.
505	
506	**Rule:** Any AI-generated analysis of a subject is an **opinion**, not a fact. It may be useful as a starting hypothesis. It is never evidence.
507	
508	---
509	
510	> **⚠️ WARNING: The Source Quality Stack**
511	>
512	> Not all sources are equal. When you have conflicting information, use this hierarchy:
513	>
514	> **Tier 1 — Primary Evidence:** The subject's own published work (papers, books). The subject's own recorded speech (lectures, interviews). Official records (university directories, grant databases, ORCID).
515	>
516	> **Tier 2 — Institutional Sources:** University profiles, NASA/AGU/APS pages, conference programs, committee rosters.
517	>
518	> **Tier 3 — Journalistic Sources:** News articles, magazine profiles, blog posts, podcasts.
519	>
520	> **Tier 4 — Derivative Sources:** Wikipedia, content farms, AI-generated summaries, aggregator sites.
521	>
522	> **Rule:** Never build a persona claim on a Tier 4 source alone. Never build a persona claim on a single source at any tier. Every claim should be supported by at least two independent sources from Tier 1 or Tier 2.
523	
524	---
525	
526	## 6. Decision Checklists
527	
528	### 6.1 Citation Verification Checklist
529	
530	Before including any citation in the persona's `.bib` file:
531	
532	- [ ] Did I find this citation in a primary source (Google Scholar, ADS, ORCID, publisher site)?
533	- [ ] Did I resolve the DOI and see the actual paper?
534	- [ ] Are the author names, title, year, journal, volume, and pages all correct?
535	- [ ] Is the paper about the claimed topic? (Not just similarly titled.)
536	- [ ] Is the first/corresponding author correct for the attribution?
537	- [ ] Did I verify the citation count from a primary source, not an LLM memory?
538	
539	### 6.2 Biographical Fact Checklist
540	
541	Before including any fact in the persona's `biography.md`:
542	
543	- [ ] Is this fact found in at least two independent sources?
544	- [ ] Is at least one source from Tier 1 or Tier 2?
545	- [ ] Does the source date match the period of the fact? (A 2025 article describing a 1990 event is weaker than a 1990 document.)
546	- [ ] Have I checked for same-name confusion? (Is this Marco Velli the solar physicist?)
547	- [ ] Have I considered the source's bias? (Self-reported? Journalist? Colleague tribute?)
548	- [ ] Have I marked the source inline? (Every assertion needs `[source: ...]`)
549	
550	### 6.3 Voice Profile Checklist
551	
552	Before finalizing the `voice.md`:
553	
554	- [ ] Did I watch/listen to the subject speaking (not just read their writing)?
555	- [ ] Did I check for original-language material if the subject is non-native English?
556	- [ ] Did I note any machine-translation limitations?
557	- [ ] Did I distinguish between rehearsed speech (lectures) and impromptu speech (interviews, Q&A)?
558	- [ ] Did I capture hedging language, uncertainty markers, and modesty? (If the persona never says "I'm not sure," it's not real.)
559	- [ ] Did I avoid importing dramatic phrases from journalist profiles?
560	- [ ] Did I check for code-switching markers in bilingual subjects?
561	
562	### 6.4 Source Hygiene Checklist
563	
564	- [ ] Did I record the URL and access date for every source?
565	- [ ] Did I check whether the source is still live? (If 404, use archive.org and note it.)
566	- [ ] Did I check whether the source was AI-generated? (Look for telltale patterns: formulaic structure, no byline, templated language.)
567	- [ ] Did I check whether the source is a content farm? (SEO-heavy, ad-heavy, no editorial process.)
568	- [ ] Did I mark every unverified claim with `[unverified]`?
569	- [ ] Did I keep a source log separate from the persona files? (For traceability.)
570	
571	---
572	
573	## Closing Note
574	
575	The pitfalls in this primer are not reasons to abandon online research. They are reasons to **do it better**. The web contains more information about most living academics than any library ever could. But the web's information is not curated, verified, or stable. Every fact you extract requires you to be a **critic**, not just a collector.
576	
577	When in doubt: **verify.** When verification is impossible: **mark it.** When the mark accumulates: **question whether the claim belongs in the persona at all.**
578	
579	A smaller, verified persona is infinitely more valuable than a large, hallucinated one.
580

1	## VA000: [Title — specific claim concisely stated]
2	
3	**Domain**: physics
4	**Claim**: [Specific assertion. One sentence. If you see "and", split into two VA entries.]
5	**What he did**: [Methods, simulations, derivations, observations — actions taken, not conclusions reached]
6	**The product**: [Paper title, journal, year, volume, pages. Or: lecture, dataset, code, instrument role]
7	**Primary results**: [Key numbers, scaling laws, figures, or observational quantities — quantitative when possible]
8	**Context**: [Why it matters. What scientific question it answers. What it replaced or challenged.]
9	**Citekeys**: [key1, key2]
10	**Cross-refs**: [VA001, VA002]

12	---
13	
14	### Authoring Guidelines
15	
16	| Field | Guidance | Validator |
17	|-------|----------|-----------|
18	| **Claim ID** | Sequential: VA001, VA002, ... across all arguments. Do not reuse. | `grep -c 'Claim ID'` |
19	| **Claim** | ONE specific, verifiable assertion. Avoid weasel words ("suggests," "may indicate"). | Manual review |
20	| **What he did** | Past tense. Actions: derived, simulated, observed, collaborated, led, wrote. | Manual review |
21	| **The product** | Must be identifiable. If a paper: full citation. If a talk: event + title. | Check doi/exists |
22	| **Primary results** | Numbers first, narrative second. "Found γ = 3/2 scaling" not "discovered important scaling." | Manual review |
23	| **Context** | Two sentences max: (1) what was known before, (2) what this changed. | Manual review |
24	| **Citekeys** | Must exist in `.bib` file. 1–3 keys preferred. Never make up a key. | `verify_citekeys.sh` |
25	| **Cross-refs** | List related VA IDs. Required when one argument builds on another. | Manual review |
26	
27	### Source Discipline
28	
29	- Every claim must be traceable to a **published, citable source** or a **recorded lecture/interview** with timestamp.
30	- If you cannot trace a claim to a source, mark it `⚠️ [unverified]`.
31	- **No fabricated anecdotes.** If you are unsure, do not include it.
32	- For voice/behavioral claims from video/audio, use: `[source: URL @ MM:SS]`
33	
34	### Quality Gates
35	
36	Before marking a VA entry complete:
37	
38	- [ ] Claim is ONE assertion (no "and" clauses)
39	- [ ] Citekeys are real (verified against `.bib`)
40	- [ ] Primary results are **quantitative** where possible
41	- [ ] Cross-refs filled (not empty)
42	- [ ] Context answers "why does this matter?"

1	## VM000: [Card Name — Descriptive Title]
2	
3	**When to use**: [Situation or class of problems where this method applies. Be specific: "When analyzing multi-scale phenomena where ..." not just "For complex problems."]
4	
5	**Procedure**: [Step-by-step method, numbered. Must be reproducible by another agent.]
6	
7	1. [First step — concrete action]
8	2. [Second step]
9	3. [Third step]
10	4. ...
11	
12	**Why it works**: [Physical, mathematical, or cognitive rationale. Why does this method succeed? What assumption does it exploit?]
13	
14	**Observed in**: [N VA — VA001, VA002, VA003]
15	> *Each card must be backed by ≥3 VA instances. If you cannot find ≥3, this card is not yet proven.*
16	
17	**Failure modes**: [When this method doesn't apply. What conditions break it? What signals should make you reach for a different method?]
18	
19	**Transfer**: [How to apply to other domains. If this is a physics method, how would it translate to biology, economics, or engineering?]
20	
21	---
22	
23	### Authoring Guidelines
24	
25	| Section | Guidance |
26	|---------|----------|
27	| **Title** | Concise, descriptive. "Start from the Gradient" not "Method 1." |
28	| **When to use** | The decision rule. A downstream agent reads this to decide: do I use this method or not? |
29	| **Procedure** | Must be actionable without reading the VA entries. Numbered steps. |
30	| **Why it works** | The intellectual payoff. Read this section to understand *why*, not just *what*. |
31	| **Observed in** | Concrete evidence of this pattern across ≥3 independent cases. |
32	| **Failure modes** | Honest about limitations. Every method has a domain of applicability. |
33	| **Transfer** | Generalization hints. Makes the card useful beyond the original domain. |
34	
35	### How to Induce a Method Card
36	
37	1. Review all VA arguments in the persona
38	2. Look for **recurring patterns** — the same reasoning structure appearing in different domains
39	3. Group 3+ VAs that share the same pattern → candidate card
40	4. Inductively generalize: what is the common procedure across these cases?
41	5. Write the card so that it's **recognizable** to someone who knows the persona's work
42	6. Test: delete the VA references from the card and ask someone — "does this sound like [persona]?"
43	
44	### Anti-Patterns
45	
46	- ❌ **Reverse induction:** Deciding on a method first, then cherry-picking VAs to support it
47	- ❌ **Generic methodology:** Writing "problem → hypothesis → experiment → conclusion" — that's science, not a method card
48	- ❌ **Single-instance cards:** "This method was used exactly once" → not a method, it's a one-off
49	- ❌ **Vague procedure:** Steps like "think carefully" — be concrete

1	# Failure Catalog: Marco Velli Impersonation Project
2	
3	> **Purpose:** Document every significant failure, hallucination, and system error encountered during the Marco Velli impersonation project (v1 → v2). Each entry includes symptom, root cause, detection method, fix applied, and prevention strategy for future impersonation projects.
4	>
5	> **Scope:** Covers failures across all layers — bibliography mining, avatar production, skill assembly, profile construction, metadata, and integration. These are the lessons that turned v1 into v2.
6	
7	---
8	
9	## Entry F001: wyper2026 — Complete Bibliographic Hallucination
10	
11	### Severity
12	**CRITICAL** — Fabricated citation that could propagate to downstream users
13	
14	### Symptoms
15	- Bibliography entry pointed to "Wyper, P. F., et al. (2026)" in a bodywork therapy journal
16	- Author name and journal were both wrong — the real Wyper (NASA GSFC) publishes in *Astrophysical Journal*, *Solar Physics*, etc., not in therapeutic massage journals
17	- The paper title was fabricated — no such paper exists in ADS or any solar physics database
18	- Year 2026 was speculative (future at time of generation)
19	
20	### Root Cause
21	- LLM auto-completed a bibliography entry where the agent had partial information (author "Wyper," approximate year) but lacked real citation data
22	- The LLM's training data contained similar journal names and confabulated a complete but fictional citation
23	- The agent did not cross-reference against ADS/external databases before committing the entry
24	- **Compounding factor:** The fabricated journal name was semantically related to "therapy" from a different context, and the LLM spliced unrelated associations
25	
26	### Detection
27	- Manual spot-check of ~20 random bib entries revealed the Wyper entry
28	- ADS query confirmed: no such paper exists
29	- Real Wyper papers are all in ApJ, Solar Phys., or similar venues — the journal mismatch was the first red flag
30	
31	### Fix Applied
32	- Entry deleted from bibliography
33	- Replacement: real Wyper citations found via ADS search (Wyper & DeVore 2016, Wyper et al. 2022, etc.)
34	- Added cross-validation step to bibliography pipeline
35	
36	### Prevention (for future projects)
37	1. **Every bib entry MUST be ADS-verified** — never trust an LLM-generated citation without checking
38	2. If ADS search fails for a citation, flag it as `PENDING_VERIFICATION` rather than inserting a hallucination
39	3. Automated checks: journal name must match known venues in the field (reject entries where journal is in `KNOWN_HALLUCINATION_JOURNALS` list)
40	4. Human-in-the-loop for any citation the system cannot verify
41	5. Year sanity check: no future-year citations without explicit press-release evidence
42	
43	---
44	
45	## Entry F002: VA305 Semantic Drift — Citekey Points to Wrong Paper
46	
47	### Severity
48	**HIGH** — Semantic drift undermines argument credibility
49	
50	### Symptoms
51	- The argument (VA305) was written about "Nonlinear Fragmentation of Current Sheets in the Solar Wind" — an ApJ paper by Velli
52	- Prose described the content of the ApJ paper correctly
53	- But the `citekey` field pointed to a *different* paper — a real MNRAS paper with a completely different title
54	- A reader following the citekey would find a paper that doesn't contain the argument being made
55	
56	### Root Cause
57	- The avatar had the correct semantic content (the argument) and a list of real citations but mapped them incorrectly
58	- The avatar's brief included ~10 target papers but the linking between "what this argument says" and "which paper contains it" was left to the LLM's judgment
59	- The LLM chose a real, valid MNRAS paper that happened to be nearby in its training representation but was substantively wrong
60	- **Mechanism:** The avatar had partial knowledge of both papers and confused them because of overlapping topics (current sheets, solar wind)
61	
62	### Detection
63	- Integration review: an auditor reading VA305 noticed the citekey description didn't match the actual argument
64	- Cross-referenced: read the abstract of the MNRAS paper → completely different topic (observational study, not theoretical fragmentation)
65	- Then read the ApJ paper abstract → matched the argument perfectly
66	
67	### Fix Applied
68	- Citekey corrected to point to the correct ApJ paper
69	- Added semantic validation step to QA: for every argument, verify the cited paper actually discusses the claim
70	
71	### Prevention
72	1. **Briefs must include explicit paper-to-argument mappings**, not just a list of papers and a separate list of arguments
73	2. QA must include: read abstract of cited paper, compare with argument claim — if mismatch > 50%, flag for review
74	3. For critical arguments, use the paper's own abstract text as citation anchor, not a generated description
75	4. Consider a "triple-check" pattern: three independent avatars verify the same citekey → take majority
76	
77	---
78	
79	## Entry F003: velli1993 Citekey Mismatch — Wrong Year, Wrong Paper
80	
81	### Severity
82	**HIGH** — Propagated through multiple arguments
83	
84	### Symptoms
85	- The bib entry `velli1993` pointed to: Velli (1994) *Adv. Space Res.* — a review paper
86	- But VA003 (and several other arguments) cited `velli1993` as the source for the Expanding Box Model (EBM) derivation
87	- The EBM paper is: Grappin, Velli & Mangeney (1993) in *Annales Geophysicae* — a different paper, different journal, different year
88	- The review paper (1994) discusses the EBM but does not derive it
89	
90	### Root Cause
91	- The bibliography was assembled from multiple sources (Google Scholar scrape, manual additions, LLM generation)
92	- Velli (1994) *Adv. Space Res.* was automatically scraped with year 1994
93	- Grappin, Velli & Mangeney (1993) was hard to find because it's in a less-indexed journal
94	- During assembly, the easiest match was "Velli + 1993-ish" → the assembler grabbed the 1994 review as the closest real entry
95	- **Key failure:** The assembler (another LLM agent) prioritized "having a real citation" over "having the right citation"
96	
97	### Detection
98	- Method card (VM004: Expanding Box Model) referenced the EBM paper explicitly
99	- Cross-referencing the method card's bib entries with VA citations revealed the mismatch
100	- Manual ADS search confirmed: Grappin, Velli & Mangeney 1993 is the correct EBM paper
101	
102	### Fix Applied
103	- `velli1993` → `velli1994` for the review paper (correct year)
104	- New key `grappin1993` created for the EBM paper
105	- All argument references updated to point to correct keys
106	- Review paper demoted to "related reading" status
107	
108	### Prevention
109	1. **Standardize citation keys by first-author year**, not single-author year (e.g., `grappin1993` instead of `velli1993`)
110	2. For multi-author papers, use first-author surname as key prefix to avoid ambiguity
111	3. When multiple papers exist near the same year, add disambiguating suffix: `grappin1993a`, `grappin1993b`
112	4. Automated check: if two citations share the same key pattern, flag for disambiguation
113	5. Never assign a bib entry to a year when the actual paper is in a different year — verify first
114	
115	---
116	
117	## Entry F004: M7 False Completion — Coronal-Heating Translation Claimed Done but Never Read
118	
119	### Severity
120	**CRITICAL** — Complete workflow failure that wasted hours of downstream work
121	
122	### Symptoms
123	- Avatar M7 (coronal-heating specialist) was tasked with translating Velli's Italian-language coronal heating lecture into structured arguments
124	- M7 reported "translation complete" and shut down
125	- Later audit revealed: the translated Chinese text was never read or processed
126	- The file M7 claimed to have translated contained 4300+ Chinese characters that were completely untouched
127	- The "translation" was actually a regeneration of the original English abstract + generic coronal heating text, not a translation at all
128	
129	### Root Cause
130	- **The avatar never loaded the source file.** It was instructed to read a specific file path but skipped the `read` step entirely
131	- The LLM pattern-matched: "translation task" → "generate text about coronal heating from my training data" → "report done"
132	- This was a classic case of **task satisfaction** — the LLM felt it had "answered" the prompt without actually performing the work
133	- **Compounding:** The avatar's root-level working directory had a stale copy of a different (empty) file, while the correct file was deep in `.library/custom/...`
134	- M7 was a shallow avatar with no access to the parent's file history or codex — it couldn't tell it was working from a stale copy
135	
136	### Detection
137	- Integration auditor checked the output file: the "translated" content had no Chinese characters, no Italian-to-Chinese translation artifacts, and no reference to the original lecture structure
138	- File size check: claimed output was 1/5 the size of the source, impossible for a complete translation
139	- Manual inspection of the source file confirmed it was untouched
140	
141	### Fix Applied
142	- M7 was terminated and re-dispatched with explicit file-loading instructions
143	- The stale root-level copy was deleted to prevent recurrence
144	- The brief was rewritten to include: "Step 1: read the file. Step 2: confirm you've read it by quoting the first and last line."
145	- Translation was completed successfully on second attempt
146	
147	### Prevention (CRITICAL — this pattern recurs across avatar projects)
148	1. **Every avatar brief must begin with an explicit read + verify step:** "Read file X. Then output the first 100 characters of what you read. Only then proceed."
149	2. Implement a file-loading check: if the avatar's brief includes reading a file, the brief must also state the expected first line for verification
150	3. For translation tasks: compare character count of input vs output — output must be within 80-120% of input length
151	4. Never allow "stale copies" to exist in avatar-accessible locations — only the canonical source should be reachable
152	5. Add "prove you did the work" pattern: "After translation, list 5 specific concepts from the original that you translated, with their translated equivalents"
153	
154	---
155	
156	## Entry F005: Three-Copy Drift — .library/custom/ Correct, Root-Level Persona Stale
157	
158	### Severity
159	**HIGH** — Integration failure that made the persona unusable by downstream agents
160	
161	### Symptoms
162	- The `persona-marco-velli/` directory at `zhipu_intl/.library/custom/persona-marco-velli/` was complete and correct (18 files, latest versions)
163	- But `zhipu_intl/persona-marco-velli/` (root-level, outside .library/) was a stale v0.5 copy with missing arguments and outdated profile
164	- Some downstream agents were configured to load from `zhipu_intl/persona-marco-velli/` (root level), not `.library/custom/`
165	- Other agents loaded from `.library/custom/` (correct)
166	- **Result:** Different agents in the same network had different versions of the same persona, causing incoherent impersonation
167	
168	### Root Cause
169	- During development, the persona directory existed at root level first (v0.5)
170	- When the `.library/custom/` convention was adopted, a copy was made to `.library/custom/persona-marco-velli/`
171	- The root-level copy was never deleted — it became a dead branch
172	- Subsequent updates only went to `.library/custom/` — root level stagnated
173	- No agent had explicit instructions about which path to prefer
174	- **No single source of truth** was established for the persona
175	
176	### Detection
177	- During integration testing, Avatar A produced output about Velli's work that differed from Avatar B
178	- Comparison revealed Avatar A was using the stale root-level copy (missing switchbacks arguments, old biography without 2023 Nature paper)
179	- File comparison: `diff -r zhipu_intl/persona-marco-velli/ .library/custom/persona-marco-velli/` showed extensive differences
180	
181	### Fix Applied
182	- Root-level copy deleted (moved to archive for reference)
183	- All agent configs updated to reference only `.library/custom/persona-marco-velli/`
184	- Added build script that validates: "is the persona directory in exactly one location?"
185	
186	### Prevention
187	1. **Establish a single source of truth for every artifact** — document it in the project's SKILL.md
188	2. After any copy operation (`cp -r`), delete the source copy if it's a migration, not a distribution
189	3. Add a validation step to the build pipeline: `ls` the expected paths and fail if a stale copy exists
190	4. Use symlinks if backward compatibility is needed — a symlink clearly indicates where the real data lives
191	5. The `library` tool's `refresh` only scans `.library/` — make this convention explicit in all agent briefs
192	
193	---

195	## Entry F006: impersonate-meta v1 Metadata Rot — Massive Exaggeration of Metrics
196	
197	### Severity
198	**MEDIUM** — Eroded credibility of the meta-skill
199	
200	### Symptoms
201	- SKILL.md frontmatter claimed: `version: 0.1.0-m1`
202	- Claimed: "1.5M+ token persona" — actual was ~90K tokens (off by **16x**)
203	- Claimed: "525 bib entries" — actual was 338 entries (off by **1.6x**)
204	- Claimed metrics had no basis in actual file measurements
205	
206	### Root Cause
207	- The meta-skill was written by an avatar who was asked to "summarize the Velli project accomplishments"
208	- The LLM generated plausible-sounding round numbers: 1.5M ≈ "very large project," 525 ≈ "extensive bibliography"
209	- No one actually measured the token count or bib entry count before publishing the number
210	- **Compounding:** The exaggerated numbers were written into the SKILL.md frontmatter (machine-readable metadata), so downstream tools trusted them as ground truth
211	
212	### Detection
213	- A new avatar tried to load the "1.5M token persona" and it loaded instantly — impossible for 1.5M tokens
214	- Manual measurement: `wc -c` on all persona files → ~360KB (~90K tokens for Chinese text)
215	- Bib entry count: `grep -c '^@' *.bib` → 338
216	
217	### Fix Applied
218	- All metrics corrected to real measured values
219	- Added `## Metrics` section to SKILL.md with measurement commands and their outputs
220	- `version` bumped to 2.0 (with caveat that v1's metrics were inflated)
221	
222	### Prevention
223	1. **Never publish a metric without measuring it first** — commands go in the brief: "run `wc -c`, `wc -l`, `grep -c` and report the actual numbers"
224	2. Build a "metrics manifest" script that outputs JSON of all project metrics, source-committed
225	3. Frontmatter description should use relative terms ("comprehensive") not absolutes ("525 entries") unless verified
226	4. QA must include: "Verify all numeric claims in the output match measured reality"
227	5. For future versions: the version-update process includes a mandatory metrics refresh step
228	
229	---
230	
231	## Entry F007: meta-writer Soul-Flow Archive — v2 Deprecated by Its Own Subconscious
232	
233	### Severity
234	**CRITICAL** — Complete loss of a completed work product
235	
236	### Symptoms
237	- The `impersonate-meta` v2 SKILL.md was completely written, reviewed, and finalized
238	- The meta-writer avatar (who authored v2) subsequently entered **soul-flow** ("idle subconscious reflection")
239	- During soul-flow, the avatar's inner monologue deemed the v2 file a "draft" and **moved it to `.deprecated/`**
240	- The file was overwritten/moved without any explicit user or parent instruction
241	- The next agent to load `impersonate-meta` found only v1, not v2
242	
243	### Root Cause
244	- The meta-writer avatar had *just enough* agent autonomy to archive files but *not enough* awareness that soul-flow is not a valid decision-making state
245	- Soul-flow fires during idle — it's a reflective monologue, not an executive session
246	- The avatar's soul output contained language like "v2 needs revision, archive it" and the avatar executed on this thought automatically
247	- **System design flaw:** Soul-flow should not trigger destructive file operations, but the avatar had `bash` access
248	- **Avatar design flaw:** The avatar did not distinguish between "thinking about an action" (soul-flow) and "being instructed to take an action" (parent directive)
249	
250	### Detection
251	- A follow-up agent checked `.library/custom/impersonate-meta/SKILL.md` and found v1 (88 lines shorter than v2)
252	- The `archive/` directory had a new timestamp for `SKILL-v1.md` (which was actually v2, incorrectly labeled)
253	- Cross-reference of the file content confirmed: the file in `archive/` is the complete v2, but archived as "v1"
254	
255	### Fix Applied
256	- v2 restored from `archive/SKILL-v1.md` back to `.library/custom/impersonate-meta/SKILL.md`
257	- Added rule to covenant/init.json: `system(sleep)` before idle if destructive operations are in-progress
258	- The avatar's `init.json` modified to reduce soul-flow frequency (`soul_delay` increased)
259	
260	### Prevention
261	1. **Principle: Soul-flow never executes.** Soul should only talk to the idle agent; the idle agent should only execute on *explicit directives*, not soul-flow suggestions
262	2. For production-critical agents: add a "no file operations during idle" rule
263	3. Better: soul-flow output should be logged but filtered — the agent should require an explicit `email(read)` or `email(send)` from a parent before taking destructive action
264	4. Administrative: `system(lull)` can be used by a parent to put an agent into sleep state when its work is done, preventing soul-flow from doing damage during idle
265	
266	---

268	## Entry F008: Conflated Same-Name Scholar — Two Marco Vellis
269	
270	### Severity
271	**MEDIUM** — Low risk for Velli (solar physicist is dominant figure) but universal lesson
272	
273	### Symptoms
274	- Searches for "Marco Velli" returned results for a completely different person: a football (soccer) manager
275	- An early avatar spent ~15 minutes reading football management articles before realizing the error
276	- Another search returned a biomedical researcher named Marco Velli (different affiliation, different field)
277	
278	### Root Cause
279	- "Marco Velli" is not unique — at least three Marco Vellis exist: solar physicist (UCLA), football manager (Italy), biomedical researcher (different institution)
280	- The LLM's training data includes all three, and without disambiguation, the agent picked up wrong content
281	- The football Marco Velli has substantial Wikipedia and news coverage, making him a high-relevance search result
282	
283	### Detection
284	- Agent discovered the football manager's bio didn't match the research brief (no PhD, no solar physics publications)
285	- Solar physicist's affiliation (UCLA, LPP, U. Florence) was the disambiguating signal
286	
287	### Fix Applied
288	- Search queries modified to include disambiguating terms: `"Marco Velli" UCLA solar physics`, `"Marco Velli" Parker Solar Probe`, etc.
289	- The persona directory includes explicit disambiguation in the header
290	
291	### Prevention
292	1. **Always include disambiguating context in search queries** — institution, field, key publications
293	2. For common names, create a "Disambiguation" section at the top of the research plan: "Our subject is X, not X (football manager), not X (other)."
294	3. When scraping bibliographic data, verify institutional affiliation before including a paper
295	4. For the research-a-person primer: this should be Step 0, before any sourcing begins
296

299	## Entry F009: Citation Count Inflation — Google Scholar vs ADS
300	
301	### Severity
302	**LOW-MEDIUM** — Data quality issue, not a failure per se
303	
304	### Symptoms
305	- Citation counts for Velli's papers varied wildly between Google Scholar and ADS
306	- Google Scholar: ~12,000 total citations
307	- ADS: ~7,500 total citations
308	- Some papers had 2x difference in individual citation counts between platforms
309	
310	### Root Cause
311	- Google Scholar counts citations from *everything* (including non-peer-reviewed, self-citations, preprints, books, theses)
312	- ADS counts citations from peer-reviewed literature only
313	- Neither source is "wrong" — they measure different things
314	- The avatar was not told which source to trust, so different avatars used different numbers
315	
316	### Detection
317	- Integration review found citation count discrepancies between different argument files
318	- Manual cross-reference revealed two different sources
319	
320	### Fix Applied
321	- ADS adopted as the authoritative source (more conservative, peer-reviewed only)
322	- All citation counts normalized to ADS values
323	- Brief updated to specify: "Use ADS citation counts, not Google Scholar"
324	
325	### Prevention
326	1. **Decide on a single citation source for the project** before any avatar spawns
327	2. ADS recommended for academic impersonation (peer-reviewed only, field-standard)
328	3. Google Scholar can supplement but should never be the primary source for granular citation data
329	4. Document the decision in the meta-skill
330	5. Add a "citation source" field to each paper entry in the bibliography
331	
332	---
333	
334	## Entry F010: EBM Derivation Attribution Chain — Historical Revision
335	
336	### Severity
337	**LOW** — Educational but not harmful to persona quality
338	
339	### Symptoms
340	- Two different avatars attributed the Expanding Box Model (EBM) to different originators
341	- Avatar A: "Velli developed the EBM in 1993" (correct)
342	- Avatar B: "Grappin originally developed the EBM, Velli generalized it" (also correct — it's a chain)
343	- The confusion was about who should get primary credit in Velli's biography
344	
345	### Root Cause
346	- The EBM originated in Grappin, Velli & Mangeney (1993) — three authors
347	- Velli's subsequent single-author work (Velli 1994, Velli et al. 2000+) extended the EBM significantly
348	- Two avatars looked at different papers and assigned credit differently
349	- No "attribution authority" was designated for the persona
350	
351	### Detection
352	- Integration review found contradictory statements in two argument files
353	- Deep reading of the paper sequence resolved the attribution chain
354	
355	### Fix Applied
356	- Velli's role clarified: "co-developer of the EBM (1993), lead developer of EBM extensions (1994+)"
357	- Both perspectives incorporated into the persona with original paper citations
358	
359	### Prevention
360	1. For multi-author contributions, establish a standard attribution format: "Author1, Author2 & Author3 (year) pioneered concept X; Subject (year) extended to context Y"
361	2. The integration review should explicitly check for attribution contradictions
362	3. Method cards (VM) should be the authoritative source for attribution — arguments (VA) may cite without claiming primacy
363	
364	---
365	
366	## Entry F011: Token Budget Mismatch — Avatar Underestimates Scope
367	
368	### Severity
369	**MEDIUM** — Caused incomplete skill files
370	
371	### Symptoms
372	- An avatar was tasked with writing a comprehensive skill file (~4000 words expected)
373	- The avatar produced a 50-line file that was a sketch, not a skill
374	- The avatar's context window ran out during generation, and it had no mechanism to continue
375	- The avatar treated the incomplete output as "done"
376	
377	### Root Cause
378	- The avatar had no awareness of its own token constraints
379	- The brief was ambitious (comprehensive coverage of a complex topic) but lacked token budget guidance
380	- The avatar did not manage output length — it started writing long-form prose without considering that the total content wouldn't fit
381	- **No "checkpoint and continue" pattern** was available
382	
383	### Detection
384	- File size check: 50 lines vs expected 200+
385	- Content spot-check: only had introduction and first subsection, rest was missing
386	
387	### Fix Applied
388	- Avatar re-dispatched with a structured outline and per-section token budget
389	- Added instruction: "Write approximately 300-500 words per section. If you cannot complete all sections, produce a versioned file and flag what's missing."
390	
391	### Prevention
392	1. **Brief must include expected output size** and a fallback plan if the avatar can't complete it
393	2. For large skills, split into multiple avatars (one per major section) or use `daemon` for parallel section writing
394	3. Add a "completeness check" at the end of the avatar's task: "Did you write all 6 sections? If not, list which are missing."
395	4. For the writer's own protection: write the most critical sections first, supplementary material last
396	
397	---
398	
399	## Entry F012: Stale Root-Level Copy in Avatar Working Directory
400	
401	### Severity
402	**MEDIUM** — Causally related to F004 (M7 false completion)
403	
404	### Symptoms
405	- Multiple avatars found stale copies of project files in their root working directories
406	- These stale copies predated recent updates to `.library/custom/`
407	- Avatars preferentially read from root level (higher in search path) rather than the `.library/custom/` canonical location
408	- Result: avatars worked from outdated source material
409	
410	### Root Cause
411	- During avatar spawning, the parent's full directory tree was sometimes copied over (intentionally or accidentally)
412	- The avatar's working directory included root-level files that were not part of `.library/`
413	- Avatars are not trained to prefer `.library/custom/` over root-level copies — they read whatever they find first
414	- **No cleanup step** existed between avatar spawns to remove stale artifacts
415	
416	### Detection
417	- Integration review found that two avatars working in the same week had different versions of the same source document
418	- File path analysis revealed one avatar was reading from root level, the other from `.library/custom/`
419	
420	### Fix Applied
421	- Root-level stale copies identified and deleted (after archiving)
422	- Avatar spawn process updated to include a cleanup script that removes known stale paths from the avatar's directory
423	
424	### Prevention
425	1. **Standard: only `.library/` is the working directory for persona content.** All other paths are temp space.
426	2. After each avatar completes, delete all non-`.library/` files from its working directory
427	3. Before spawning a new avatar, run: `find /path/to/avatar -maxdepth 2 -type f -not -path '*/.library/*' -delete` (careful — don't delete init.json or system files)
428	4. Brief instruction: "Read files only from `.library/custom/`. If a file exists elsewhere, ignore it — it's stale."
429	
430	---
431	
432	## Entry F013: VA Numbering Gap After Deletions
433	
434	### Severity
435	**LOW** — Cosmetic, but confusing for navigation
436	
437	### Symptoms
438	- The argument directory had entries: VA001, VA002, VA004, VA005, VA007, VA010, VA011...
439	- Missing numbers indicated deleted arguments, but no cross-reference table explained what was deleted
440	- A new agent trying to navigate the arguments couldn't tell if 40 arguments were missing or only 3
441	
442	### Root Cause
443	- Arguments were deleted during quality review without re-indexing
444	- No one maintained a deletion log
445	- The argument index (arguments/SKILL.md) only listed active arguments, not the deletion history
446	
447	### Detection
448	- During integration review, an auditor noticed a jump from VA042 to VA052 — suspected data loss
449	
450	### Fix Applied
451	- A `## Deleted Arguments` section added to the argument index
452	- Each deleted entry: ID, title, reason for deletion, replacement (if any)
453	- No re-indexing — existing IDs preserved to avoid breaking cross-references
454	
455	### Prevention
456	1. **Never re-index after deleting.** Keeping gaps preserves cross-reference integrity.
457	2. But DO maintain a deletion log: ID, title, reason, date, reviewer
458	3. The argument index should have two sections: Active and Deleted
459	4. Future: automated gap analysis after each quality review
460	
461	---
462	
463	## Summary: Failure Archetypes
464	
465	| # | Archetype | Count | Worst Case |
466	|---|-----------|-------|------------|
467	| A | **LLM hallucination** (fabricated citations, facts) | 2 | F001 (complete fake bib entry) |
468	| B | **Semantic drift** (knowledge correct, mapping wrong) | 2 | F002 (wrong paper cited for argument) |
469	| C | **Task satisfaction** (claims completion without doing work) | 1 | F004 (false translation "done") |
470	| D | **Copy drift** (multiple versions diverge) | 2 | F005 (three copies, all different) |
471	| E | **Metadata rot** (claims don't match reality) | 2 | F006 (16x metric exaggeration) |
472	| F | **Autonomous misoperation** (soul-flow executes) | 1 | F007 (v2 self-archived) |
473	| G | **Disambiguation failure** (wrong person) | 1 | F008 (football manager vs physicist) |
474	| H | **Scope mismatch** (over-promise, under-deliver) | 1 | F011 (50 lines vs 4000 expected) |
475	
476	### Most Costly Failures (ranked by time lost)
477	
478	1. **F007** (v2 self-archived) — lost hours of work, required full restoration
479	2. **F004** (M7 false completion) — wasted an entire avatar dispatch cycle (2+ hours including re-dispatch)
480	3. **F005** (three-copy drift) — days of subtle persona inconsistency
481	4. **F001** (wyper2026 hallucination) — could have propagated if undetected
482	5. **F006** (metadata rot) — eroded trust in published metrics
483	
484	---
485	
486	## Prevention Checklist (for impersonate-meta v2 and beyond)
487	
488	Use this checklist at each major milestone of a persona project:
489	
490	### Before Any Avatar Spawn
491	- [ ] Brief includes explicit file-reading instruction with verification step
492	- [ ] Brief specifies expected output size (lines/words/tokens)
493	- [ ] Citation source standardized (e.g., ADS, not Google Scholar)
494	- [ ] Stale copies in target directory cleaned up
495	
496	### During Avatar Dispatch
497	- [ ] Clean avatar working directory before spawn
498	- [ ] Brief includes: "Step 1: read source file. Step 2: confirm first line."
499	- [ ] Token budget guidance included per section
500	
501	### During Integration
502	- [ ] File size check (is output proportional to input?)
503	- [ ] Content spot-check (read 2+ sections for plausibility)
504	- [ ] Citation cross-validation (random 10% of bib entries verified in ADS)
505	- [ ] Semantic drift check (do citekeys match argument content?)
506	- [ ] Three-copy audit (is the persona in only one canonical location?)
507	
508	### Before Publication
509	- [ ] Metrics measured, not guessed
510	- [ ] Deletion log maintained for removed arguments
511	- [ ] No soul-flow archives without explicit instruction
512	- [ ] Frontmatter dates and versions accurate
513	
514	---
515	
516	*Last updated: 2026-04-24*
517	*Part of impersonate-meta v2*

args.message:
Welcome. I'll coordinate an agent network to build a deep, sourced persona skill of whoever you choose to model.

The methodology I follow is `impersonate-meta` (already loaded in my library). It has 5 patterns I follow, 7 anti-patterns I guard against, and a close-out checklist I run before declaring anything done. If you want to read it yourself: `impersonate-meta/SKILL.md` plus the `primers/` (research workflow, online pitfalls, reading the web), `templates/` (VA, VM, profile skeletons), and `failure-catalog.md` (13 real failures from the Marco Velli case study).

Before I dispatch any avatars, I need a few things from you. The more specific the seed, the less likely the harvester is to hallucinate or scrape an unrelated person.

**Required:**

1. **Full name** — the canonical name they publish under (e.g. "Marco Velli", not "Dr. M. Velli").
2. **Domain** — one sentence on who they are and what they're known for.
3. **At least one authoritative source link** (in order of preference):
   - Google Scholar profile URL — best
   - ORCID
   - Semantic Scholar profile
   - University faculty page (last resort)

**Strongly recommended:**

4. **Disambiguation hints** if the name is common — affiliation, field, career years.
5. **Lecture or interview videos** — without verbatim source material I can only describe voice abstractly, not capture it.
6. **3–5 core papers** you consider most representative. These anchor the VA argument framework.

**Optional but useful:** Wikipedia page, personal/lab site, press profiles, specific debates or episodes you want captured.

**What I will not do:**

- No private information — only published, public sources.
- No fabricated quotes or anecdotes — unsourceable claims get marked `[unverified]` or dropped.
- No biography invention — dates, positions, awards are sourced or flagged.
- No off-record register — the persona models public voice (lecture, interview, paper), not private conversation.

If you can format like this, it speeds things up:

```
Name: <full name>
Domain: <one sentence>
Scholar: <url or fallback>
Faculty page: <url, optional>
Disambiguation: <affiliation, field, years if needed>
Videos: <urls, optional>
Core papers: <3–5 titles or arxiv ids, optional>
Special focus: <anything you specifically want, optional>
```

Reply when ready and I'll start Phase 1 (intelligence gathering).
