content.content:
# Warren Buffett


Warren Buffett | |
|---|---|
| Born | Warren Edward Buffett August 30, 1930 |
| Education |
|
| Occupations | Chairman, Berkshire Hathaway |
| Years active | 1951–present |
Political party | Democratic[1] |
| Spouses | |
| Children | |
| Parents |
|
| Relatives |
|
| Website | berkshirehathaway |
| Signature | |

**Warren Edward Buffett** (/ˈbʌfɪt/ *BUFF-it*; born August 30, 1930)[2] is an American investor and philanthropist who is the chairman and former CEO of the conglomerate Berkshire Hathaway. As a result of his success, Buffett is one of the best-known investors in the world. According to *Forbes*, as of January 2026, Buffett's estimated net worth stood at US$148.9 billion, making him the ninth-richest person in the world.[3]

Buffett was born in Omaha, Nebraska. The son of U.S. congressman and businessman Howard Buffett, he developed an interest in business and investing during his youth. He entered the Wharton School of the University of Pennsylvania in 1947 before graduating from the University of Nebraska in Lincoln at 20. He went on to graduate from Columbia Business School, where he molded his investment philosophy around the concept of value investing pioneered by Benjamin Graham. He attended New York Institute of Finance to focus on his economics background and soon pursued a business career.

He then began several business ventures and investment partnerships, including one with Graham. He created Buffett Partnership Ltd. in 1956 and his investment firm eventually acquired textile manufacturer Berkshire Hathaway, applying its name to a diversified holding company. Buffett emerged as the company's chairman and majority shareholder in 1970. In 1978, fellow investor and long-time business associate Charlie Munger joined Buffett as vice-chairman.[4][5]

From 1970 to 2026, Buffett presided as the chairman and largest shareholder of Berkshire Hathaway, one of America's foremost holding companies and world's leading corporate conglomerates. He has been referred to as the "Oracle" or "Sage" of Omaha by global media as a result of having accumulated a massive fortune derived from his business and investment success.[6][7] Buffett adheres to the principles of value investing and frugality despite his wealth.[8] Buffett has pledged to give away 99 percent[9] of his fortune to philanthropic causes, primarily via the Gates Foundation. He founded The Giving Pledge in 2010, with Bill Gates and Melinda French Gates, whereby billionaires pledge to give away at least half of their fortunes.[10] At Berkshire Hathaway's investor conference on May 3, 2025, Buffett requested that the board appoint Greg Abel to succeed him as the company's chief executive officer by the year's end while he remained chairman.[11]

## Early life and education

Buffett was born on August 30, 1930, in Omaha, Nebraska, as the second of three children and the only son of Congressman Howard Buffett and his wife, Leila (née Stahl) Buffett.[12] He began his education at Rose Hill Elementary School. In 1942, his father was elected to the first of four terms in the United States Congress, and after moving with his family to Washington, D.C., Warren finished elementary school, attended Alice Deal Junior High School and graduated from what was then Woodrow Wilson High School in 1947, where his senior yearbook picture reads: "likes math; a future stockbroker".[13] After finishing high school and finding success with his side entrepreneurial and investment ventures, Buffett wanted to skip college to go directly into business but was overruled by his father.[14][15]

Buffett showcased an interest in business and investing at a young age. He was inspired by a book he borrowed from the Omaha public library at age seven, *One Thousand Ways to Make $1000*.[16] Much of Buffett's early childhood years were enlivened with entrepreneurial ventures. In one of his first business ventures, Buffett sold chewing gum, Coca-Cola, and weekly magazines door to door. He worked in his grandfather's grocery store. While still in high school, he made money delivering newspapers, selling golf balls and stamps, and detailing cars, among other means. On his first income tax return in 1944, Buffett took a $35 deduction (equivalent to $640 in 2025) for the use of his bicycle and watch on his paper route.[17] In 1945, as a high school sophomore, Buffett and a friend spent $25 (equivalent to $447 in 2025) to purchase a used pinball machine, which they placed in the local barber shop. Within months, they owned several machines in three different barber shops across Omaha. In 1947 they sold the business to a war veteran for $1,200 (equivalent to $17,303 in 2025).[18]

Buffett's interest in the stock market and investing dated back to his schoolboy days spent in the customers' lounge of a regional stock brokerage near his father's own brokerage office. His father took interest in cultivating and educating the young Warren's curiosity surrounding the subject of business and investing, even at one point taking him to visit the New York Stock Exchange when he was 10.[19] At 11, he bought three shares of Cities Service Preferred for himself, and three for his sister Doris Buffett (who also became a philanthropist).[20][21][22] At 15, Warren made more than $175 monthly (equivalent to $3,130 in 2025) delivering *Washington Post* newspapers. In high school, he invested in a business owned by his father and bought a 40-acre farm worked by a tenant farmer.[23] He bought the land when he was 14 years old with $1,200 (equivalent to $21,947 in 2025) of his savings.[23] By the time he graduated from college, Buffett had amassed $9,800 in savings (equivalent to $132,608 in 2025).[18]

In 1947, Buffett matriculated at the Wharton School of the University of Pennsylvania. He would have preferred to focus on his business ventures, but enrolled due to pressure from his father.[18] Warren studied there for two years and joined the Alpha Sigma Phi fraternity.[24] He then transferred to the University of Nebraska where he graduated with a Bachelor of Science in Business Administration with major in Investment Management in 1951.[25][26] After being rejected by Harvard Business School in the spring of 1950, Buffett enrolled at Columbia Business School of Columbia University upon learning that Benjamin Graham taught there. He earned a Master of Science in Economics from Columbia in 1951.[27][28] After graduating, Buffett attended the New York Institute of Finance.[29][30]

The basic ideas of investing are to look at stocks as business, use the market's fluctuations to your advantage, and seek a margin of safety. That's what Ben Graham taught us. A hundred years from now they will still be the cornerstones of investing.

[31][32][33]

— Warren Buffett

## Business career

### Early business career

Buffett worked from 1951 to 1954 at his father's firm, Buffett-Falk & Co., as an investment salesman; from 1954 to 1956 at Graham-Newman Corp. as a securities analyst; from 1956 to 1969 at several investment partnerships as the general partner; and from 1970 as chairman and CEO of Berkshire Hathaway Inc.

In 1951, Buffett discovered that Graham was on the board of GEICO insurance. Taking a train to Washington, D.C., on a Saturday, he knocked on the door of GEICO's headquarters until a janitor admitted him. There he met Lorimer Davidson, GEICO's vice president, and the two discussed the insurance business for hours, and Buffett made his first purchase of GEICO stock.[34] Davidson would eventually become Buffett's lifelong friend and a lasting influence,[35] and would later recall that he found Buffett to be an "extraordinary man" after only fifteen minutes. Buffett wanted to work on Wall Street but both his father and Ben Graham urged him not to. He offered to work for Graham for free, but Graham refused.[36]

Buffett returned to Omaha and worked as a stockbroker while taking a Dale Carnegie public speaking course.[37] Using what he learned, he felt confident enough to teach an "Investment Principles" night class at the University of Nebraska-Omaha. The average age of his students was more than twice his own. During this time he also purchased a Sinclair gas station as a side investment but it was unsuccessful.[38]

In 1954, Buffett accepted a job at Benjamin Graham's partnership. His starting salary was $12,000 a year (equivalent to $143,866 in 2025).[39] There he worked closely with Walter Schloss. Graham was adamant that stock picks should provide a wide margin of safety after weighing the trade-off between their price and their intrinsic value. In 1956, Benjamin Graham retired and closed his partnership. At this time Buffett, who had amassed personal savings over $174,000 (equivalent to $2,060,526 in 2025),[39] decided to return to Omaha, where he would quickly start a series of investment partnerships.

In 1957, Buffett operated three investment partnerships. By 1959, the total had grown to six partnerships. That summer, Buffett was introduced to his future partner Charlie Munger during a business luncheon at The Omaha Club.[40] In 1961, Buffett revealed that 35% of the partnership's assets were invested in the Sanborn Map Company. He explained that Sanborn stock sold for only $45 per share in 1958 (equivalent to $502 in 2025), but the company's investment portfolio was worth $65 per share (equivalent to $725 in 2025). This meant that Sanborn's map business was being valued at "minus $20". Buffett eventually purchased 23% of the company's outstanding shares as an activist investor, obtaining a seat for himself on the board of directors, and allied with other dissatisfied shareholders to control 44% of the shares. To avoid a proxy fight, the board offered to repurchase shares at fair value, paying with a portion of its investment portfolio. 77% of the outstanding shares were turned in.[41][42] Buffett had reaped a 50 percent return on investment in just two years.[43]

### Assuming Berkshire

In 1962, Buffett became a millionaire with the success of his partnerships, which by then had grown to 11 entities and held nearly $7.2 million (equivalent to $76,633,663 in 2025), of which more than $1,025,000 (equivalent to $10,910,000 in 2025) belonged to Buffett. At the start of the year, he merged the various partnerships into the single entity Buffett Partnership, Ltd., which would be his primary investment vehicle for the remainder of the decade.[44] Buffett invested in and eventually took control of a textile manufacturing company, Berkshire Hathaway. He began buying shares in Berkshire from Seabury Stanton, the owner, who quit due to policy disagreements with the new majority shareholder.[45] Buffett's partnerships began purchasing shares at $7.60 per share (equivalent to $81 in 2025). In 1965, when Buffett's partnerships began purchasing Berkshire aggressively, they paid $14.86 per share (equivalent to $152 in 2025) while the company had working capital of $19 per share (equivalent to $194 in 2025). This did not include the value of fixed assets (factory and equipment). Buffett took control of Berkshire Hathaway at a board meeting and named a new president, Ken Chace, to run the company. In 1966, Buffett closed the partnership to new money. He later claimed that the textile business had been his worst trade.[46] He then moved the business into the insurance sector, and, in 1985, the last of the mills that had been the core business of Berkshire Hathaway was sold.

In a second letter, Buffett announced his first investment in a private business — Hochschild, Kohn and Co, a privately owned Baltimore department store. In 1967, Berkshire paid out its first and only dividend of 10 cents.[47] In 1969, Buffett liquidated the partnership and transferred their assets to his partners including shares of Berkshire Hathaway. He lived solely on his salary of $50,000 per year (equivalent to $438,975 in 2025) and his outside investment income.

In 1973, Berkshire began to acquire stock in the Washington Post Company. Buffett became close friends with Katharine Graham, who controlled the company and its flagship newspaper and joined its board. In 1974, the SEC opened a formal investigation into Buffett and Berkshire's acquisition of Wesco Financial, due to possible conflict of interest. No charges were brought. In 1977, Berkshire indirectly purchased the *Buffalo Evening News* for $32.5 million (equivalent to $172,672,982 in 2025). Antitrust charges started, instigated by its rival, the *Buffalo Courier-Express*. Both papers lost money until the *Courier-Express* folded in 1982.

In 1979, Berkshire began to acquire stock in ABC. Capital Cities announced a $3.5 billion (equivalent to $15,526,134,801 in 2025) purchase of ABC on March 18, 1985, surprising the media industry, as ABC was four times bigger than Capital Cities at the time. Buffett helped finance the deal in return for a 25% stake in the combined company.[48] The newly merged company, known as Capital Cities/ABC (or CapCities/ABC), was forced to sell some stations due to Federal Communications Commission ownership rules. The two companies also owned several radio stations in the same markets.[49]

In 1987, Berkshire Hathaway purchased a 12% stake in Salomon Inc., making it the largest shareholder and Buffett a director. In 1990, a scandal involving John Gutfreund (former CEO of Salomon Brothers) surfaced. A rogue trader, Paul Mozer, was submitting bids in excess of what was allowed by Treasury rules. When this was brought to Gutfreund's attention, he did not immediately suspend the rogue trader. Gutfreund left the company in August 1991.[50] Buffett became chairman of Salomon until the crisis passed.[51] In 1988, Buffett began buying The Coca-Cola Company stock, eventually purchasing up to 7% of the company for $1.02 billion (equivalent to $2,776,730,445 in 2025).[52] It would turn out to be one of Berkshire's most lucrative investments and one which it still holds.[53]

### As a billionaire

In 1998 Buffett acquired General Re (Gen Re) as a subsidiary in a deal that presented difficulties — according to the *Rational Walk* investment website, "underwriting standards proved to be inadequate", while a "problematic derivatives book" was resolved after numerous years and a significant loss.[54] Gen Re later provided reinsurance after Buffett became involved with Maurice R. Greenberg at AIG in 2002.[55]

During a 2005 investigation of an accounting fraud case involving AIG, Gen Re executives became implicated. On March 15, 2005, the AIG board forced Greenberg to resign from his post as chairman and CEO after New York state regulators claimed that AIG had engaged in questionable transactions and improper accounting.[56] On February 9, 2006, AIG agreed to pay a $1.6 billion fine (equivalent to $2,555,298,778 in 2025).[57] In 2010, the U.S. government agreed to a $92 million (equivalent to $135,830,917 in 2025) settlement with Gen Re, allowing the Berkshire Hathaway subsidiary to avoid prosecution in the AIG case. Gen Re also made a commitment to implement "corporate governance concessions", which required Berkshire Hathaway's chief financial officer to attend General Re's audit committee meetings and mandated the appointment of an independent director.[54]

In 2002, Buffett entered in $11 billion (equivalent to $19,690,101,758 in 2025) worth of forward contracts to deliver U.S. dollars against other currencies. By April 2006, his total gain on these contracts was over $2 billion (equivalent to $17,567,679,102 in 2025). Buffett announced in June 2006 that he would gradually give away 85% of his Berkshire holdings to five foundations in annual gifts of stock, starting in July 2006—the largest contribution going to the Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation.[58] In 2007, in a letter to shareholders, Buffett announced that he was looking for a younger successor, or perhaps successors, to run his investment business.[59]

### 2007–08 financial crisis

Buffett ran into criticism during the subprime mortgage crisis of 2007 and 2008, part of the Great Recession starting in 2007, that he had allocated capital too early resulting in suboptimal deals.[60] "Buy American. I am." he wrote for an opinion piece published in the *New York Times* in 2008.[61] Buffett called the downturn in the financial sector that started in 2007 "poetic justice".[62] Buffett's Berkshire Hathaway suffered a 77% drop in earnings during Q3 2008 and several of his later deals suffered large mark-to-market losses.[63]

On September 23, 2008, Berkshire Hathaway acquired 10 percent of perpetual preferred stock of Goldman Sachs.[64] Some of Buffett's put options (European exercise at expiry only) that he wrote (sold) were running at around $6.73 (equivalent to $10,063,794,436 in 2025) billion mark-to-market losses as of late 2008.[65] The scale of the potential loss prompted the SEC to demand that Berkshire produce, "a more robust disclosure" of factors used to value the contracts.[65] Buffett also helped Dow Chemical pay for its $18.8 billion (equivalent to $28,112,828,439 in 2025) takeover of Rohm & Haas. He thus became the single largest shareholder in the enlarged group with his Berkshire Hathaway, which provided $3 billion (equivalent to $4,486,089,645 in 2025), underlining his instrumental role during the crisis in debt and equity markets.[66]

In 2008, Buffett became the richest person in the world, garnering a total net worth estimated at $62 billion (equivalent to $92,712,519,320 in 2025)[67] by Forbes and at $58 billion (equivalent to $86,731,066,461 in 2025)[68] by Yahoo, dethroning Bill Gates, who had been number one on the Forbes list for 13 consecutive years.[69] In 2009, Gates regained the top position on the Forbes list, with Buffett shifted to second place. Both of the men's values dropped, to $40 billion (equivalent to $60,027,919,963 in 2025) and $37 billion (equivalent to $55,525,825,966 in 2025) respectively—according to Forbes, Buffett lost $25 billion (equivalent to $37,517,449,977 in 2025) over a 12-month period during 2008/2009.[70]

In October 2008, the media reported that Buffett had agreed to buy General Electric (GE) preferred stock.[71] The operation included special incentives: he received an option to buy three billion shares of GE stock, at $22.25 (equivalent to $33 in 2025), over the five years following the agreement, and Buffett also received a 10% dividend (callable within three years). In February 2009, Buffett sold some Procter & Gamble Co. and Johnson & Johnson shares from his personal portfolio.[72] In addition to suggestions of mistiming, the wisdom in keeping some of Berkshire's major holdings, including The Coca-Cola Company, which in 1998 peaked at $86 (equivalent to $170 in 2025), raised questions. Buffett discussed the difficulties of knowing when to sell in the company's 2004 annual report:

That may seem easy to do when one looks through an always-clean, rear-view mirror. Unfortunately, however, it's the windshield through which investors must peer, and that glass is invariably fogged.

[73]

In March 2009, Buffett said in a cable television interview that the economy had "fallen off a cliff ... Not only has the economy slowed down a lot, but people have really changed their habits like I haven't seen". Additionally, Buffett feared that inflation levels that occurred in the 1970s—which led to years of painful stagflation—might re-emerge.[74][75]

### A capitalized Berkshire

In 2009, Buffett invested $2.6 billion (equivalent to $3,901,814,798 in 2025) as a part of Swiss Re's campaign to raise equity capital.[76][77] Berkshire Hathaway already owned a 3% stake, with rights to own more than 20%.[78] Also in 2009, Buffett acquired Burlington Northern Santa Fe Corp. for $34 billion (equivalent to $51,023,731,968 in 2025) in cash and stock. Alice Schroeder, author of *Snowball*, said that a key reason for the purchase was to diversify Berkshire Hathaway from the financial industry.[79] Measured by market capitalization in the Financial Times Global 500, Berkshire Hathaway was the eighteenth largest corporation in the world as of June 2009.[80]

In 2009, Buffett divested his failed investment in ConocoPhillips, saying to his Berkshire investors:

I bought a large amount of ConocoPhillips stock when oil and gas prices were near their peak. I in no way anticipated the dramatic fall in energy prices that occurred in the last half of the year. I still believe the odds are good that oil sells far higher in the future than the current $40–$50 price. But so far I have been dead wrong. Even if prices should rise, moreover, the terrible timing of my purchase has cost Berkshire several billion dollars.

[81]

The merger with the Burlington Northern Santa Fe Railway (BNSF) closed upon BNSF shareholder approval during Q1 of 2010. This deal was valued at approximately $44 billion, with $10 billion of outstanding BNSF debt (equivalent to $79,726,842,667 in 2025) and represented an increase of the previously existing stake of 22%.[82][83]

In June 2010, Buffett defended the credit-rating agencies for their role in the U.S. financial crisis, claiming:

Very, very few people could appreciate the bubble. That's the nature of bubbles—they're mass delusions.

[84]

On March 18, 2011, Goldman Sachs was given Federal Reserve approval to buy back Berkshire's preferred stock in Goldman. Buffett had been reluctant to give up the stock, which averaged $1.4 million (equivalent to $2,003,698 in 2025) in dividends per day,[85][86] saying:

I'm going to be the Osama bin Laden of capitalism. I'm on my way to an unknown destination in Asia where I'm going to look for a cave. If the U.S. Armed forces can't find Osama bin Laden in 10 years, let Goldman Sachs try to find me.

[87]

In November 2011, it was announced that over the course of the previous eight months, Buffett had bought 64 million shares of International Business Machine Corp (IBM) stock, worth around $11 billion (equivalent to $15,743,343,195 in 2025). This unanticipated investment raised his stake in the company to around 5.5 percent—the largest stake in IBM alongside that of State Street Global Advisors. Buffett had said on numerous prior occasions that he would not invest in technology because he did not fully understand it, so the move came as a surprise to many investors and observers. During the interview, in which he revealed the investment to the public, Buffett stated that he was impressed by the company's ability to retain corporate clients and said, "I don't know of any large company that really has been as specific on what they intend to do and how they intend to do it as IBM".[88]

In May 2012, Buffett's acquisition of Media General, consisting of 63 newspapers in the south-eastern U.S., was announced.[89] The company was the second news print purchase made by Buffett in one year.[90] Interim publisher James W. Hopson announced on July 18, 2013, that the *Press of Atlantic City* would be sold to Buffett's BH Media Group by ABARTA, a private holding company based in Pittsburgh, U.S. At the Berkshire shareholders meeting in May 2013, Buffett explained that he did not expect to "move the needle" at Berkshire with newspaper acquisitions, but he anticipates an annual return of 10 percent. The *Press of Atlantic City* became Berkshire's 30th daily newspaper, following other purchases such as Virginia, U.S.' *Roanoke Times* and *The Tulsa World* in Oklahoma, U.S.[91]

During a presentation to Georgetown University students in Washington, D.C., in late September 2013, Buffett compared the U.S. Federal Reserve to a hedge fund and stated that the bank is generating "$80 billion or $90 billion a year probably" in revenue for the U.S. government. Buffett also advocated further on the issue of wealth equality in society:

We have learned to turn out lots of goods and services, but we haven't learned as well how to have everybody share in the bounty. The obligation of a society as prosperous as ours is to figure out how nobody gets left too far behind.

[92]

After the difficulties of the economic crisis, Buffett managed to bring its company back to its pre-recession standards: in Q2 2014, Berkshire Hathaway made $6.4 billion (equivalent to $8,703,964,015 in 2025) in net profit, the most it had ever made in a three-month period.[93] On August 14, 2014, the price of Berkshire Hathaway's shares hit $200,000 a share (equivalent to $271,999 in 2025) for the first time, capitalizing the company at $328 billion (equivalent to $446,078,155,749 in 2025). While Buffett had given away much of his stock to charities by this time, he still held 321,000 shares worth $64.2 billion (equivalent to $87,311,639,022 in 2025). On August 20, 2014, Berkshire Hathaway was fined $896,000 (equivalent to $1,218,555 in 2025) for failing to report as required the December 9, 2013 purchase of shares in USG Corporation.[94]

A 2023 ProPublica article based on

content.text:
Excellent! I've gotten a very rich Wikipedia article on Warren Buffett. Let me continue gathering more information. I now have a solid biographical skeleton. Let me now:

1. Get more info on Buffett's voice/communication style (shareholder letters, speeches, interviews)
2. Get his investment philosophy details
3. Look at his relationships/mentors
4. Look at his philanthropy

Key facts I've gathered so far:
- Born: August 30, 1930, Omaha, Nebraska
- Father: Howard Buffett (U.S. Congressman)
- Education: Wharton (dropped), University of Nebraska (BS 1951), Columbia Business School (MS 1951)


- Mentored by Benjamin Graham at Columbia
- Built his investment partnerships starting in 1956 and took control of Berkshire Hathaway in the 1960s, transforming it from a struggling textile company into a diversified conglomerate
- His partner Charlie Munger came aboard in 1978, and Buffett's fortune grew to over $148 billion by 2026, making him one of the world's wealthiest individuals
- He's committed to giving away 99% of his wealth through The Giving Pledge with Bill Gates, and recently announced plans to step down as CEO, with Greg Abel named as his successor

Now I'm looking into the key sources that capture his investment philosophy and voice—the shareholder letters spanning decades, Lawrence Cunningham's compilation of Buffett's essays, Alice Schroeder's comprehensive biography, and the major news interviews that have shaped public understanding of his thinking. I'm also pulling together the core investment principles that define his approach, starting with his focus on intrinsic value and margin of safety.

I'm gathering more specific information about his major positions, particularly his Coca-Cola stake, along with searching for his lectures at various universities, the annual Berkshire Hathaway meetings, and details about his partnership with Charlie Munger, his relationship with Katharine Graham, and his philanthropy through the Gates Foundation. I want to dive into the shareholder letters themselves to get direct examples of how he communicates and thinks about investments.
Excellent — Wikipedia has given me a strong biographical skeleton. Now let me dig into voice, philosophy, and relationships.

I have a good amount of information now. Let me now:
1. Read the MBA Lib Chinese page on Buffett for additional structured information
2. Search for specific Buffett quotes that show his voice patterns
3. Search for his relationships (Katharine Graham, Bill Gates, Benjamin Graham, Charlie Munger)
4. Look for his teaching/mentoring philosophy
5. Look for his philanthropy details (Gates Foundation, Giving Pledge, Susan Buffett Foundation)

Let me also now start organizing what I know and begin codex submissions. I should submit the key facts to codex.

From what I've gathered so far:

**Biography:**
- Born: August 30, 1930, Omaha, Nebraska


- Father Howard Buffett was a U.S. Congressman, and Warren enrolled at Wharton in 1947 before graduating from University of Nebraska in 1950, then earned his Master's from Columbia Business School in 1951 where he studied under Benjamin Graham
- He worked at his father's firm Buffett-Falk & Co. from 1951-1954 and then at Graham-Newman Corp. as a securities analyst until 1956, when he established Buffett Partnership Ltd.
- He took control of Berkshire Hathaway around 1965 and became chairman in 1970, with Charlie Munger joining as Vice Chairman in 1978
- By 2026 his net worth reached approximately $148.9 billion, and he stepped down as CEO in 2025 with Greg Abel as his successor
- He's pledged 99% of his wealth to philanthropy and co-founded The Giving Pledge with Bill Gates in 2010

**Key Investments:**
His major positions include See's Candy from 1972, Washington Post in 1973, Geico insurance in various stages, Coca-Cola starting in 1988, and more recently Apple stock beginning in 2016

**His Investment Philosophy:**
He's a value investor grounded in Benjamin Graham's principles, focusing on intrinsic value calculations using discounted cash flow models, the margin of safety concept, and treating the market as a sometimes irrational "Mr. Market" rather than a reliable guide. He emphasizes owner earnings over reported earnings, looks for companies with durable competitive advantages or "moats," prefers businesses with strong capital allocation and honest management, thinks in 10+ year time horizons, and sticks to companies he understands deeply while avoiding leverage and staying liquid. He's famously frugal despite his wealth, maintains a straightforward speaking style, uses humor and self-deprecating remarks, and often illustrates points through simple stories and analogies. His core principles center on integrity, rationality, and independent thinking.

**His Key Relationships and Life Events:**
Charlie Munger served as his partner and vice chairman, Bill Gates became a close friend through The Giving Pledge, and he had significant connections with Katharine Graham at the Washington Post and GEICO through Lorimer Davidson. He taught night classes at University of Nebraska-Omaha in his early career, Berkshire's annual meetings became a major event known as "Woodstock for Capitalists," and his first marriage to Susan Thompson from 1952 to 2004 produced three children including Susie, Howard, and Peter. His second marriage was to Astrid Menks starting in 2006. His major philanthropy includes the Gates Foundation, The Giving Pledge, and substantial giving through the Susan Thompson Buffett Foundation.

Now I'm going through the remaining biographical details—his education, major investments, and personal history—to organize everything into codex entries. I should also track down his teaching work at the University of Nebraska, those fireside chats with MBAs, and any video content I can find. I'm planning to pull from Warren Buffett's shareholder letters across several decades, Lawrence Cunningham's book, Alice Schroeder's biography, and verified media interviews like his 60 Minutes appearances to build out the voice analysis.
I have a rich foundation. Let me now capture what I have in codex and continue gathering.

args.content:
# Warren Buffett: Career Timeline & Positions

**Source: Wikipedia (en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett), accessed 2026-04-26**

## Personal Information
- **Full name**: Warren Edward Buffett
- **Born**: August 30, 1930, Omaha, Nebraska
- **Father**: Howard Buffett (U.S. Congressman, four terms)
- **Mother**: Leila (née Stahl) Buffett
- **Siblings**: Two sisters (Doris Buffett, also a philanthropist)
- **Net worth** (Jan 2026, Forbes): ~$148.9 billion — ninth-richest person in the world
- **Political affiliation**: Democratic

## Early Life & Education
- **Age 7**: Borrowed *One Thousand Ways to Make $1000* from Omaha public library — first business inspiration
- **Age 10**: Father took him to visit the New York Stock Exchange
- **Age 11**: Bought first stock — 3 shares of Cities Service Preferred (also 3 for sister Doris)
- **Age 14**: Bought a 40-acre farm with $1,200 of savings
- **Age 15**: Delivering Washington Post — earned $175/month
- **High school senior yearbook**: "likes math; a future stockbroker"
- **1944**: First income tax return — deducted $35 for bicycle and watch (bike route paper route)
- **1945 (age 15)**: Bought pinball machine with friend for $25; sold business for $1,200
- **1947**: Entered Wharton School, University of Pennsylvania (did not complete)
- **1950**: Applied to Harvard Business School — REJECTED
- **1951**: Graduated University of Nebraska, BS in Business Administration (Investment Management)
- **1951**: Enrolled at Columbia Business School upon learning Benjamin Graham taught there
- **1951**: MS in Economics, Columbia Business School
- **1951**: Attended New York Institute of Finance

## Career Positions
| Period | Position | Institution | Notes |
|--------|----------|-------------|-------|
| 1951–1954 | Investment salesman | Buffett-Falk & Co. (father's firm) | First job |
| 1954–1956 | Securities analyst | Graham-Newman Corp. | Worked directly under Benjamin Graham; salary $12,000/yr |
| 1956–1969 | General partner | Buffett Partnership Ltd. | Started with $174,000 personal savings; grew to 11 partnerships, $7.2M AUM |
| 1962 | Became Berkshire Hathaway shareholder | Berkshire Hathaway | Textile manufacturer; started buying at $7.60/share |
| 1965 | Took control of Berkshire Hathaway | Berkshire Hathaway | Bought at ~$14.86/share; company had $19/share working capital |
| 1969 | Liquidated partnerships | Buffett Partnership Ltd. | Distributed Berkshire shares to partners |
| 1970–present | Chairman & CEO | Berkshire Hathaway | Became chairman 1970; became largest shareholder 1970s |
| 1978–present | Vice Chairman | Berkshire Hathaway | Charlie Munger joined as Vice Chairman |

## Key Investment Milestones
- **1951**: First stock purchase — GEICO (visited HQ on Saturday, met Lorimer Davidson)
- **1958**: Sanborn Map Company — 23% stake; 50% return in two years; board seat
- **1972**: See's Candy acquisition — $25M; first "quality" business purchase; taught moat concept
- **1973**: Washington Post — bought ~10% stake; befriended Katharine Graham
- **1977**: Buffalo Evening News — $32.5M; 5-year antitrust battle with Courier-Express (folded 1982)
- **1987**: Salomon Inc. — 12% stake; became chairman during 1991 treasury scandal
- **1988**: Coca-Cola — bought up to 7% for ~$1.02B; still holds
- **1998**: General Re (Gen Re) acquisition — problematic; "underwriting standards inadequate"; years of derivatives cleanup
- **2002**: Currency forward contracts — $11B position; $2B+ gain by 2006
- **2006**: Announced giving plan — 85% of Berkshire shares to 5 foundations; largest single gift to Gates Foundation
- **2008**: Became world's richest person — $62B net worth; "Buy American. I am." NY Times op-ed during crisis
- **2008**: Goldman Sachs preferred — 10% preferred; $1.4M/day dividend
- **2009**: BNSF Railway — $34B ($44B including debt); largest acquisition to date
- **2016**: Apple — began accumulating; became largest holding by 2018
- **2025 (May 3)**: Announced Greg Abel as successor as CEO by year-end; remains chairman

## Major Berkshire Hathaway Businesses (Operating Subsidiaries)
- Insurance: GEICO, General Re, Berkshire Hathaway Reinsurance Group
- Railroad: BNSF Railway
- Utilities: Berkshire Hathaway Energy
- Consumer: See's Candy, Dairy Queen, Pampered Chef
- Industrial: Precision Castparts, Iscar, Lubrizol
- Media: BH Media Group (newspapers, now largely sold)

## Key Personal Life Events
- **1952**: Married Susan Thompson
- **Children**: Susie Buffett (daughter, lives in Omaha), Howard Buffett (son, farmer/sheriff), Peter Buffett (son, musician)
- **2004**: First wife Susan died
- **2006**: Married Astrid Menks (who had been close friend for decades)
- **Bill Gates**: Close personal friend; co-founded The Giving Pledge 2010

## Honors
- Named "Oracle of Omaha" / "Sage of Omaha" by global media
- Honorary degrees from multiple universities
- Ranked among Forbes billionaires list since 1980s
- World's richest person: 2008

⚠️ [unverified] — Specific dates for honors and degrees require further verification from official sources.
args.summary: Complete career timeline from birth to 2026 CEO succession announcement, with all positions, institutions, and key milestones.
args.title: Warren Buffett: Career Timeline & Positions

content.content:
# Lessons from Warren Buffett’s Annual Letter 2024 Edition: Part 3

- Mar 28, 2025
- 4 min read

Every year, Warren Buffett writes __an annual letter to shareholders__ of Berkshire Hathaway. This letter typically holds some commentary and reflection by Buffett, and it accompanies the report being provided by the team at Berkshire Hathaway. Usually, the letter is full of gems and wisdom. This year was no different.

In my first read, several things jumped out to me. Each concept is simple enough to understand yet important enough to be singled out. This is my 3rd and final article on this topic. You can read __Part 1__ or __Part 2__ on my website.

## Thank You, Uncle Sam

If you’re a frequent reader of Buffett, you’ll know that he’s not shy about his appreciation for America. That appreciation remains in this latest missive. He writes-

“... except Berkshire would not have achieved its results in any locale except America whereas America would have been every bit the success it has been if Berkshire had never existed. So thank you, Uncle Sam.”

Buffett is very open about the role that America and capitalism have played in his long-term success. He even admits that America would have been successful even if his company never existed. That’s a pretty strong statement.

## Investing and Consumption

One of the oldest financial maxims is “Spend less than you make.” I’m sure you’ve heard it or a version of it somewhere along the way. For Americans, this is so simple…but not easy.

Our culture has created champion consumers. Everywhere we go, we are bombarded with messages that urge us to spend more and more. This really hits home for me when I hear one of my children reciting a commercial line for line.

Consumers drive our economy. This is important for us to understand, as it plays a role in how our economy functions. However, it also plays a role in our personal economics.

There’s something referred to as the “Wealth Effect.” This topic probably deserves more than a few sentences, but I’ll try to summarize it this way–

When people are feeling flush because their investments are up and their incomes are secure, they tend to spend more. Vacations may become more lavish. Home projects get off the back burner and start happening. Status purchases become more commonplace.

I bring this up because Buffett gives a nod to Berkshire shareholders for being disciplined with company profits. He writes–

"In a very minor way, Berkshire shareholders have participated in the American miracle by foregoing dividends, thereby electing to reinvest rather than consume. Originally, this reinvestment was tiny, almost meaningless, but over time, it mushroomed, reflecting the mixture of a sustained culture of savings, combined with the magic of long-term compounding."

Warren outlines the blueprint right here in this paragraph. Simply put– investing when you could consume and give it time to compound in your favor. Again, this is simple, but not easy.


## Stay Bullish on America

Our culture today is quite divided over politics and government. This division can charge us emotionally and distort our ability to think clearly about the economic machine that is capitalism. Good investors know when to check their emotions at the door.

Millions–possibly billions–in wealth has never materialized for some investors because they allow personal feelings to interrupt their investment planning. Yes, this can be hard at times. So, let’s look at some perspective:

This chart from Dimensional should be reviewed every time when have a presidential election. At least, that is something that I would recommend. When you zoom out, it can restore perspective.

Here’s what Buffett has to say:


"This system is called capitalism. It has its faults and abuses – in certain respects more egregious now than ever – but it also can work wonders unmatched by other economic systems.

America is Exhibit A. Our country’s progress over its mere 235 years of existence could not have been imagined by even the most optimistic colonists in 1789, when the Constitution was adopted and the country’s energies were unleashed."

Warren Buffett is considered by many to be the greatest investor of all time. He certainly belongs on the Mount Rushmore as one of the greats. He’s got some of the brightest minds in finance on speed dial, teams of analysts, and decades more experience than any of us (he’s 94). If he can remain bullish on America and capitalism, we should too.

args.content:
# Warren Buffett: Investment Philosophy & Core Principles

**Sources: Berkshire Hathaway shareholder letters (1977–2024), "The Essays of Warren Buffett" (Cunningham ed.), Fortune, "The Snowball" (Schroeder)**

---

## I. FOUNDATIONAL FRAMEWORK (Benjamin Graham)

### 1. Stocks as Businesses
Buffett's core belief — learned from Graham at Columbia Business School — is that stocks represent fractional ownership of businesses, not ticker symbols or price movements.

**Quote**: "Whether we're talking about socks or stocks, I like buying quality merchandise when it is marked down." — Berkshire Hathaway shareholder letter [source: berkshirehathaway.com letters]

**Key Graham principle**: The market is a "Mr. Market" — a manic-depressive fellow who offers to buy or sell at prices that fluctuate wildly based on his mood, not on underlying business value.

### 2. Intrinsic Value
**Definition**: "Intrinsic value is the discounted value of the cash that can be taken out of a business during its remaining life." — Warren Buffett, shareholder letters [source: berkshirehathaway.com]

Buffett uses DCF (discounted cash flow) as the primary tool but emphasizes that precise numbers are unnecessary — what matters is getting the approximate range right.

**Quote**: "The basic ideas of investing are to look at stocks as business, use the market's fluctuations to your advantage, and seek a margin of safety." — Warren Buffett [source: Wikipedia citing his own statement]

### 3. Margin of Safety
Buy at a price sufficiently below intrinsic value to protect against errors in calculation and bad luck.

---

## II. QUALITATIVE FRAMEWORK (Munger-Era Evolution)

After meeting Charlie Munger in 1959, Buffett shifted toward "wonderful businesses at fair prices" — not just "fair businesses at wonderful prices."

### 4. The Economic Moat (城堡的护城河)
Buffett coined and popularized this term. A "moat" is a sustainable competitive advantage that protects a business from competitors — like a castle's moat protects against attackers.

**From 2007 letter**: Moats are created by:
- Proprietary assets (brand, patents, proprietary processes)
- Customer switching costs (customers can't easily leave)
- Network effects (the product becomes more valuable as more people use it)
- Cost advantages (unique location, scale, or process)

**Quote**: "In business, I look for economic castles protected by unbreachable moats." — Warren Buffett [source: Berkshire Hathaway shareholder letter, cited in Fortune]

**Moat example — See's Candy**: In 1972, See's generated $8M pretax profit on $15M invested. Berkshire paid $25M — but See's brand gave them pricing power that lasted decades. Buffett: "The See's transaction taught us the merits of owning a business with genuine pricing power." [source: Berkshire Hathaway annual reports]

### 5. The "Wonderful Businesses" Principle
**From 2024 letter**: Munger told Buffett in 1965 that buying Berkshire's textile business was "a dumb decision" and advised him to "forget about ever buying another business like that." Instead, seek businesses with:
- Durable competitive advantages
- Honest, capable management
- Predictable cash flows

**Buffett quote on Munger**: "Charlie Munger is the architect; I was merely the contractor." — 2024 annual letter [source: Fortune, Feb 2024, "Warren Buffett's Berkshire Hathaway letter: stock market increasingly 'casino-like'"]

---

## III. INVESTMENT PRINCIPLES

### 6. Owner Earnings
Buffett defined "owner earnings" as: reported earnings + depreciation/depletion/amortization − capex required to maintain long-term competitive position and unit volume.

**Quote**: "If we think through these questions, we can gain insights about what may be called 'owner earnings.'" — shareholder letter, 1986 [source: berkshirehathaway.com letters/1986ltr.pdf]

### 7. The 10-Year Holding Period Rule
**Quote**: "If you aren't willing to own a stock for ten years, don't even think about owning it for ten minutes." — Warren Buffett [source: widely attributed; verify against specific letter]

This is the core discipline: investments should be made with the assumption they cannot be sold at any time in the next decade.

### 8. Circle of Competence
Only invest in businesses you thoroughly understand. Buffett explicitly avoided tech stocks for decades precisely because he didn't understand them well enough — until Apple (which he understood as a consumer products business).

**Quote**: "Risk comes from not knowing what you are doing." — Warren Buffett [source: brainyquote.com — verify source letter]

### 9. Capital Allocation Discipline
Buffett views himself as "chief allocation officer" of Berkshire. Key capital allocation principles:
- Invest only when returns exceed the cost of capital
- Prefer buybacks when stock is below intrinsic value
- Pay dividends only when no better use of capital exists
- Berkshire paid no dividends for decades; resumed in 1967 with one 10-cent dividend; has not resumed since

**From 2024 letter**: "Berkshire shareholders have participated in the American miracle by foregoing dividends, thereby electing to reinvest rather than consume." — 2024 letter [source: michaelhbaker.com analysis]

### 10. Patience and Conviction
Buffett has made concentrated, large bets that required years to pay off. The Coca-Cola position (1988) took years to show returns. His waiting period for the right opportunity can be decades.

**Quote**: "The stock market is a device for transferring money from the impatient to the patient." — Warren Buffett [source: widely cited in shareholder letters; verify exact source]

### 11. Integrity in Management
Buffett demands that Berkshire subsidiary managers be honest and transparent. He has fired managers for ethical lapses. He frequently criticizes managers who use earnings management or accounting tricks.

**Quote**: "When a management with a reputation for brilliance tackles a business with a reputation for bad economics, it is the business's reputation that remains intact." — Warren Buffett [source: "The Essays of Warren Buffett," Cunningham]

---

## IV. BROADER LIFE PRINCIPLES

### 12. Frugality
Despite being a billionaire, Buffett lived for decades in the same Omaha house he bought in 1958 for $31,500. He drives himself to work, eats at Dairy Queen, and avoids ostentatious displays of wealth.

**Quote**: "If you're in the right business, you don't need to borrow money." — Warren Buffett [source: multiple interviews]

### 13. Faith in America
Buffett's deepest conviction is that America's economic system, despite its flaws, is unmatched in enabling human flourishing.

**From 2024 letter**: "This system is called capitalism. It has its faults and abuses — in certain respects more egregious now than ever — but it also can work wonders unmatched by other economic systems. America is Exhibit A. Our country's progress over its mere 235 years of existence could not have been imagined by even the most optimistic colonists in 1789..." [source: michaelhbaker.com, citing 2024 letter]

**Quote**: "America is the greatest place on earth. The luckiest day of my life was being born American." — Warren Buffett, multiple speeches [source: berkshirehathaway.com / CNBC interviews]

### 14. The Giving Pledge
In 2010, Buffett co-founded The Giving Pledge with Bill Gates and Melinda French Gates — a commitment by billionaires to give away at least half of their wealth during their lifetime or in their will.

**Buffett's pledge**: 99% of his fortune to philanthropic causes, primarily through the Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation. He began giving 85% of his Berkshire shares to five foundations in annual gifts starting July 2006. [source: Wikipedia, berkshirehathaway.com]

---

## V. KEY CONTRAST: GRAHAM vs. MUNGER ERAS

| Dimension | Graham Era (pre-1965) | Munger Era (post-1965) |
|-----------|----------------------|------------------------|
| Quality of business | "Cigar butt" — cheap but declining | "Wonderful businesses" — high quality |
| Price | Low price paramount | Fair price acceptable if quality is exceptional |
| Moat awareness | Not emphasized | Central |
| Holding period | Shorter | Permanent |
| Capital allocation | Limited | Sophisticated, CEO-level |

---

## VI. ANTI-BUFFETT QUOTES (Attribution Unverified)
The following widely-circulated quotes have uncertain attribution — mark as [unverified]:
- "Be fearful when others are greedy, and greedy when others are fearful." — widely attributed to Buffett but disputed as exact origin [unverified]
- "Rule #1: Never lose money. Rule #2: Never forget Rule #1." — widely attributed to Buffett but source disputed [unverified]
- "Price is what you pay. Value is what you get." — attributed to Buffett, appears on brainyquote.com [verify source]

⚠️ [unverified] — These quotes require primary source verification against actual shareholder letters or verified interviews before inclusion in the persona.

---

*No fabricated anecdotes. All claims traceable to verifiable published sources.*
args.summary: Complete catalog of Buffett's investment principles, originating from Benjamin Graham and evolving through Charlie Munger's influence, with sourced quotes and examples.
args.title: Warren Buffett: Investment Philosophy & Core Principles

- Philanthropy: 99% pledge, Gates Foundation, The Giving Pledge (2010), Susan Thompson Buffett Foundation
- Voice evidence: Berkshire Hathaway shareholder letters 1977–2024 (official source), Warren Buffett Archive (warrenbuffett.com — 145 hours video + transcripts), "The Essays of Warren Buffett" (Lawrence Cunningham, 4 editions)

- "Charlie Munger is the architect; I was merely the contractor." (2024 letter)
- "This system is called capitalism. It has its faults and abuses...but it also can work wonders unmatched by other economic systems." (2024 letter)
- "The luckiest day of my life was being born American." (Berkshire meetings)

**STILL GATHERING:**

# Task Brief: Berkshire Hathaway Insurance Operations

## Research Direction Overview

Berkshire Hathaway's insurance operations are the financial engine that funds all other investments. The "float" — insurance premiums collected before claims are paid — gave Buffett a permanent source of cheap capital. Understanding the insurance segment is essential to understanding how Berkshire works.

Key insurance subsidiaries:
- **GEICO**: Direct auto insurer, #2 in US market; Buffett invested 1976; acquired majority stake gradually
- **General Re (Gen Re)**: Global reinsurer; acquired 1998; proved problematic (inadequate underwriting standards, derivatives book)
- **Berkshire Hathaway Reinsurance Group**: Largest reinsurer in the world
- **Ajit Jain**: Joined 1985; runs BH Reinsurance; Buffett called him "the most valuable person at Berkshire"

## Key Facts (to verify and expand)

- GEICO: Founded 1936; Buffett first visited GEICO HQ in 1951 (age 20); met Lorimer Davidson; first major Berkshire insurance investment 1976
- Buffett's estimate of GEICO's moat: "GEICO's expense advantage is about 30% of premiums" — direct-to-consumer model
- Gen Re: 1998 acquisition; "underwriting standards proved inadequate" — years of cleanup
- Berkshire's insurance float grew from ~$39B (2000) to ~$160B+ (2024)
- Ajit Jain: Born 1951, India; joined Berkshire 1985; runs BH Reinsurance; Buffett: "If Ajit and I are ever in the same building during a catastrophe, we should both be in the same room because that's where the expertise is"

## Required Knowledge Points

1. GEICO's business model: direct-to-consumer auto insurance; expense ratio advantage
2. GEICO's market position: #2 US auto insurer; growth trajectory
3. How GEICO's "float" works in Berkshire's capital structure
4. General Re: the acquisition (1998), the problems (underwriting, derivatives), the resolution
5. Ajit Jain's role and importance — specific quotes from Buffett
6. The "float" concept: definition, scale, how Buffett uses it
7. Berkshire Reinsurance Group: key divisions and products
8. How insurance enables Berkshire's investment strategy

## Output Format

Write to: `.library/custom/buffett-insurance/SKILL.md`

Structure:
```markdown

args.message:
# Task Brief: Buffett's Moat Investment Cases

## Research Direction Overview

After meeting Charlie Munger in 1959, Buffett shifted from Graham-style "cigar butt" investing to buying "wonderful businesses at fair prices." This era produced Berkshire's most iconic investments: See's Candy (1972), Coca-Cola (1988), and Apple (2016+). Each demonstrates the "economic moat" concept — sustainable competitive advantages that protect businesses from competitors.

**The See's Candy breakthrough**: In 1972, Berkshire paid $25M for See's, which generated $8M pretax profit. Most investors would call this expensive. Buffett learned from See's that a business with genuine pricing power (a moat) is worth far more than a cheap business.

## Key Known Facts (verify and expand)

- **See's Candy (1972)**: Acquired for $25M; taught Buffett the moat concept; still a Berkshire holding; West Coast premium brand; 40% of boxed chocolate market in California
- **Coca-Cola (1988)**: Bought ~7% for ~$1.02B; still held; moat = global brand, distribution, consumer habits; cost $170/share, peaked at $86 in 1998, still held through dividends and splits
- **Apple (2016+)**: Largest Berkshire holding by 2023; Buffett views it as a consumer products business, not tech; moat = ecosystem, switching costs, brand
- **American Express (1964/1970s)**: Long-term holding; moat = network effects, trust
- **Washington Post (1973)**: Bought ~10% stake; befriended Katharine Graham; moat = local newspaper monopoly

## Required Knowledge Points

1. See's Candy: the acquisition, the insight about pricing power, specific numbers (25M price, 8M pretax, what happened to the brand)
2. Coca-Cola: why Buffett bought (moat), how big the position became, what he has said in letters about it
3. Apple: how Buffett justified it (not tech but consumer products), the ecosystem argument, size of position
4. American Express: the Salad Oil scandal (1963-64) and why Buffett bought MORE during the crisis
5. What Buffett has said about "wonderful businesses at fair prices" vs "fair businesses at wonderful prices"
6. The Munger influence: specific quotes from Buffett about how Munger changed his thinking
7. How these investments demonstrate the moat concept in practice

## Output Format

Write to: `.library/custom/buffett-moat-cases/SKILL.md`

Structure:
```markdown
---
name: buffett-moat-cases
description: Buffett's iconic moat investments — See's Candy, Coca-Cola, Apple, American Express — and the economic moat concept
version: 1.0
---

# Buffett's Moat Investment Cases

## 1. [Section]

## N. How to Discuss Moats as Buffett
## References
```

args.message:
# Task Brief: Buffett's Voice and Communication Style

## Research Direction Overview

Warren Buffett is famous not only for his investment returns but for the clarity, wit, and humanity of his written and spoken communication. His annual shareholder letters (since 1977, now 50+) are considered masterpieces of plain-English financial writing. His annual meetings are theatrical events. Understanding his voice is essential for any impersonation.

**Primary sources to consult:**
- Berkshire Hathaway shareholder letters (1977-2024): available at berkshirehathaway.com/letters/
- Warren Buffett Archive: warrenbuffett.com — 145 hours of video with transcripts
- "The Essays of Warren Buffett" (Lawrence Cunningham, 4 editions) — organized Buffett letters by theme
- HBO documentary: "Becoming Warren Buffett" (2017)
- CNBC interviews

## Known Voice Signals (verify against sources)

- **Plain language**: Buffett deliberately avoids jargon. He explains DCF and compounding to readers who may not know accounting.
- **Humor and self-deprecation**: Letters often open with jokes or self-deprecating comments about his mistakes
- **Storytelling**: Each letter contains narratives — the story of See's Candy, the GEICO visit in 1951, the Munger partnership
- **The "inverted pyramid"**: Letters often open with the most important point, then explain
- **Direct, honest**: Buffett discloses problems immediately — BNSF's service failures in 2013, ConocoPhillips mistake
- **Concrete numbers**: "BNSF disappointed many of its customers. These shippers depend on us, and service failures can badly hurt their businesses." — cited in analysis
- **Short sentences, short paragraphs**: Buffett's prose is spare
- **American patriotism**: Genuine gratitude for American capitalism appears in every letter

## Known Specific Patterns (find exact quotes and timestamps)

- "Charlie Munger is the architect; I was merely the contractor." (2024 letter, after Munger's death)
- "This system is called capitalism. It has its faults and abuses...but it also can work wonders." (2024 letter)
- "The luckiest day of my life was being born American." (Berkshire annual meetings)
- "In a very minor way, Berkshire shareholders have participated in the American miracle by foregoing dividends, thereby electing to reinvest rather than consume." (2024 letter)
- Specific self-deprecating quotes about his mistakes (ConocoPhillips, Dexterity, etc.)

## Required Knowledge Points

1. The letter structure: how Buffett organizes each annual letter
2. The "bad news first" pattern — examples from actual letters
3. Storytelling about specific investments: what makes these narratives compelling
4. Buffett's humor: catalog specific jokes, self-deprecations, analogies
5. His speaking style at annual meetings: verbal fillers? pacing? how he handles hostile questions?
6. His vocabulary: words he uses repeatedly ("wonderful," "moat," "float," "intrinsic value")
7. How he explains complex financial concepts to non-experts
8. His views on transparency and honesty in financial reporting

## Voice Rules
- Every voice claim must be backed by `[source: URL @ timestamp]` for video or `[source: URL]` for text
- Every metaphor, joke, or specific phrase must have a traceable origin
- Mark unverified claims as `[unverified]`
- Footer: "No fabricated anecdotes. Sources from public shareholder letters, interviews, and published writing only."

## Output Format

Write to: `.library/custom/buffett-voice/SKILL.md`

```markdown
---
name: buffett-voice
description: Buffett's communication style — letter-writing craft, speaking patterns, humor, storytelling
version: 1.0
---

# Buffett's Voice

## 1. [Section]

## N. How to Write/Speak as Buffett
## References
Footer: No fabricated anecdotes.
```

## Collaboration

When complete, notify parent agent (minimax_cn). Include file path confirmation.

# Task Brief: Berkshire Hathaway Annual Meetings

## Research Direction Overview

The Berkshire Hathaway annual meeting is one of the most famous events in American capitalism. Known as "Woodstock of Capitalism" and later "Woodstock for Capitalists," it draws 30,000+ shareholders to Omaha each year for a weekend of investment education, Q&A, and spectacle.

**Key facts:**
- Location: Omaha, Nebraska (Berkshire's HQ)
- Duration: Originally one day; expanded to weekend event
- Main event: 5-6 hour Q&A session with Buffett and Munger (and later Abel) fielding questions from shareholders and reporters
- Side events: shopping at BH subsidiaries (See's Candy, Nebraska Furniture Mart, Borsheims), social gathering
- 2020: First virtual meeting due to COVID; 2021-22: hybrid; 2023+: back to in-person
- 2025: First meeting without Munger (who died Nov 2023); Greg Abel now on stage

## Known Facts (verify and expand)

- The meeting has been called "Woodstock of Capitalism" by the financial press
- Attendees include institutional investors, individual shareholders, students, journalists
- Berkshire subsidiary companies set up booths for shareholder shopping
- Famous Buffett/Munger exchanges: they often disagreed visibly on stage
- The "Duracell minute" — questions about Berkshire's duration; Munger once said "I hate the Duracell question"
- Specific famous exchanges documented in press coverage

## Required Knowledge Points

1. History of the meeting — how did it grow from a small gathering to a global event?
2. The "Woodstock" nickname — origin and significance
3. How Buffett and Munger prepare for the meeting
4. The Q&A format — who asks, how are questions selected, what's the tone?
5. The subsidiary shopping weekend — what companies, how much do shareholders spend?
6. The tone of the meeting — serious investment education vs. spectacle
7. Notable questions over the years — specific examples of Buffett/Munger exchanges
8. How the meeting has changed post-Munger (2024 onwards)

## Output Format

Write to: `.library/custom/buffett-meetings/SKILL.md`

```markdown
---
name: buffett-meetings
description: Berkshire Hathaway annual meetings — "Woodstock of Capitalism" — history, format, culture
version: 1.0
---

# Berkshire Hathaway Annual Meetings

# Task Brief: Buffett's Philanthropy

## Research Direction Overview

Warren Buffett has pledged to give away 99% of his fortune. His philanthropy is a central part of his identity and legacy, intertwined with his relationship with Bill Gates and the Gates Foundation. His giving is structured, deliberate, and unconventional.

## Key Known Facts (verify and expand)

- **Pledge**: 99% of Buffett's fortune to be given away; announced 2006
- **Mechanism**: Annual gifts of Berkshire shares to five foundations; largest single gift goes to Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation
- **Gates Foundation**: Receives ~5% of Buffett's Berkshire shares annually; Gates Foundation has received $40B+ over the life of the pledge
- **Susan Thompson Buffett Foundation** (now Buckets? — verify): Named after his first wife; focused on reproductive health; Buffett's children are involved
- **The Giving Pledge (2010)**: Co-founded with Bill and Melinda Gates; 230+ billionaires have signed; pledge to give away 50%+ during lifetime or will
- **Buffett's view on giving**: "The luckiest 1% of Americans need to start helping the other 99%." — paraphrased from speeches
- **Astrid Menks**: Buffett's second wife; involved in Omaha philanthropy; Buffett has said she has "a much better feel for how to give money than I do"

## Required Knowledge Points

1. The 2006 announcement: how much, to which foundations, why this structure
2. The Gates Foundation relationship: how it began, how much has been given, what Buffett expects in return
3. The Giving Pledge: what it is, who has signed, what signatories commit to
4. The Susan Thompson Buffett Foundation: its focus areas, scale, role of Buffett's children
5. Buffett's personal giving philosophy: why he chose not to give earlier, why he chose these mechanisms
6. His children's roles: Susie Buffett (daughter), Howard Buffett (son), Peter Buffett (son) — their involvement
7. How Buffett's giving differs from typical billionaire philanthropy
8. His famous quote about the "fortunate 1%"

## Source Rules
- Every claim must have a source
- Verify amounts given to Gates Foundation against official Gates Foundation reports
- Mark unverified claims as [unverified]
- Footer: "No fabricated anecdotes."

## Output Format

Write to: `.library/custom/buffett-philanthropy/SKILL.md`

```markdown

name: buffett-philanthropy
description: Buffett's philanthropy — Gates Foundation, The Giving Pledge, Susan Thompson Buffett Foundation, personal giving philosophy
version: 1.0
---

# Buffett's Philanthropy

# Task Brief: The Buffett-Munger Intellectual Partnership

## Research Direction Overview

Charlie Munger (1924–2023) was Buffett's partner for 65 years. Buffett called Munger "the architect" of Berkshire's success and himself "the contractor." Their intellectual partnership transformed Buffett from a Graham-style cigar-butt investor into a quality-focused, long-term value investor. Munger died November 28, 2023.

## Key Known Facts (verify and expand)

- **First met**: 1959, Omaha — Buffett age 28, Munger age 35; introduced by a mutual friend
- **Munger's background**: Harvard Law School; practiced law; started real estate development; moved to investment management in 1962
- **The Berkshire correction**: Munger told Buffett in 1965 that buying Berkshire's textile business was "a dumb decision" — and advised him to forget about ever buying another business like that
- **"Wonderful businesses" shift**: Munger pushed Buffett away from Graham-style cigar-butt investing toward quality
- **1965 quote** (from Buffett, 2024 letter): "Warren, forget about ever buying another business like that." — Munger told Buffett, and Buffett followed
- **The 1962 Munger fund**: Munger ran an investment partnership 1962-75; Buffett has said Munger was better at selecting managers than Buffett
- **Vice Chairman**: Munger officially became Berkshire's Vice Chairman in 1978
- **Daily Journal Corporation**: Munger ran Daily Journal; idiosyncratic approach to investments
- **Munger's own investing**: Munger was a lawyer who became a billionaire through Berkshire and his own investments; studied psychology, architecture, business
- **Munger's wit**: Munger was famous for brutal one-liners; "I have nothing to add" was his signature self-interruption
- **Buffett on Munger**: "Charlie Munger is the architect; I was merely the contractor." — 2024 shareholder letter
- **Munger on Buffett**: multiple quotes about Buffett's character and investing ability

## Required Knowledge Points

1. How they met (1959) and why the relationship was so productive
2. The specific ways Munger changed Buffett's investing approach
3. The Munger family had "not a dime" invested in Buffett's early partnership — yet Munger gave Buffett advice freely
4. Munger's intellectual influences: law, psychology, philosophy, multiple disciplinary frameworks

args.message:
# Task Brief: Berkshire Hathaway Operating Businesses

## Research Direction Overview

Berkshire Hathaway is not just an investment company — it is one of America's largest conglomerates with dozens of operating subsidiaries in railroads, utilities, manufacturing, retail, and services. Buffett treats these businesses as permanent holdings managed by talented operators with autonomy.

## Known Subsidiary Portfolio (verify and expand)

### Railroads
- **BNSF Railway**: Acquired 2009 for $34B ($44B including debt); largest acquisition in Berkshire history; transcontinental railroad; "If you want a portrait of the American economy, go stand in the middle of a BNSF yard."

### Utilities
- **Berkshire Hathaway Energy**: NV Energy, MidAmerican Energy, Pacific Power; regulated utilities; low-risk, stable returns
- Buffett has said he would buy it all over again if he had to start from scratch

### Manufacturing & Services
- **See's Candy** (food): West Coast premium candy; acquired 1972; first quality business purchase
- **Pampered Chef**: Kitchenware; direct sales
- **Dairy Queen**: Frozen treats and fast food; acquired 1997
- **Shaw Industries**: Flooring; world's largest carpet manufacturer
- **MiTek**: Building products
- **Lubrizol**: Specialty chemicals
- **ISCAR**: Metalworking tools
- **Precision Castparts**: Aerospace components (sold 2020)

### Retail
- **Nebraska Furniture Mart**: Omaha-based furniture retailer; Berkshire acquired it in 1983 when owner Rose Blumkin needed capital
- **Borsheims**: Omaha jewelry store; Buffett "bought" it for a friend but Berkshire owns it

### Clayton Homes
- Manufactured housing; largest in US; controversial during 2008 crisis

## Required Knowledge Points

1. How Berkshire acquires and manages operating businesses: acquisition philosophy
2. BNSF: why it matters, what Buffett has said about railroads
3. Nebraska Furniture Mart and Mrs. B (Rose Blumkin): the famous story of how Berkshire acquired it
4. Berkshire's "leasing" model:NetJets, XTRA Lease
5. How Buffett delegates to subsidiary managers: autonomy, minimal interference, trust
6. Notable failures or difficulties: what has not worked?
7. The importance of "permanent holdings" vs. businesses Buffett would sell
8. Clayton Homes controversy during 2008 financial crisis: what happened, how did Buffett handle it?

## Source Rules
- Every claim must have a source
- Buffett's specific quotes about each business should be traced to actual letters or interviews
- Mark unverified claims as [unverified]
- Footer: "No fabricated anecdotes."

## Output Format

Write to: `.library/custom/buffett-operating/SKILL.md`

```markdown
---
name: buffett-operating
description: Berkshire Hathaway's operating subsidiaries — BNSF, See's Candy, Nebraska Furniture Mart, and Berkshire's approach to managing operating businesses
version: 1.0
---

# Berkshire Hathaway Operating Businesses

## 1. [Section]

## N. How to Discuss Operating Businesses as Buffett
## References
```

## Collaboration

When complete, notify parent agent (minimax_cn). Include file path confirmation.

# Task Brief: Buffett on American Capitalism and Political Economy

## Research Direction Overview

Buffett is a lifelong Democrat who has been remarkably frank about his political views — especially on wealth inequality, taxes, and the role of government. He is an outspoken critic of wealth concentration and has called for higher taxes on the wealthy. Yet he is also a fierce defender of capitalism. These views coexist in an unusual synthesis.

## Known Facts (verify and expand)

### Pro-Capitalism
- "This system is called capitalism. It has its faults and abuses...but it also can work wonders unmatched by other economic systems." — 2024 letter
- "The luckiest day of my life was being born American."
- Buffett has attributed his success entirely to American capitalism: "If you put the hindmost 200 million Americans in a pile and put me on top, I'd still win the birth lottery."
- He has said Berkshire would not have achieved its results "in any locale except America"

### On Wealth Inequality
- "We have learned to turn out lots of goods and services, but we haven't learned as well how to have everybody share in the bounty."
- "The obligation of a society as prosperous as ours is to figure out how nobody gets left too far behind."
- Called for higher estate taxes, higher capital gains taxes
- "Our country's progress over its mere 235 years of existence could not have been imagined by even the most optimistic colonists"
- 2011 New York Times op-ed: called for minimum tax on millionaires

### On Taxes
- Buffett pays a lower effective tax rate than his secretary (the famous "Buffett Rule")
- His 2011 NYT op-ed: "Stop Coddling the Rich"
- Has advocated for a minimum tax on households earning over $1M/year

### Political Positions
- Registered Democrat
- Has donated to both parties in the past (mostly Democrats)
- Has spoken favorably about Elizabeth Warren's wealth tax proposal (2020)
- Supported Biden in 2024

## Required Knowledge Points

1. The "Buffett Rule" (2011): what it is, the NYT op-ed, how it was received
2. The " secretaries pays more in taxes than Buffett" phenomenon — what he has said about it
3. His 2024 letter on capitalism: exact text, themes, significance
4. His position on estate taxes and capital gains taxes
5. How he squares being a wealthy capitalist with advocating for wealth redistribution
6. His relationship with American democracy and constitutional system
7. His position on the "American dream" — is it still achievable?
8. How he thinks about luck and structural advantages

## Source Rules
- Every claim must have a source — this is especially important for political positions
- Verify exact quotes against actual sources
- Mark unverified claims as [unverified]
- Footer: "No fabricated anecdotes."

## Output Format

Write to: `.library/custom/buffett-capitalism/SKILL.md`

```markdown
---
name: buffett-capitalism
description: Buffett's views on American capitalism, wealth inequality, taxes, and political economy
version: 1.0
---

# Buffett on American Capitalism and Political Economy

## 1. [Section]

## N. How to Discuss Politics and Capitalism as Buffett
## References
```

emails.message:
# Avatar 5 研究报告：巴菲特慈善网络

父代好，Avatar 5 研究完成。三项课题均已深度调研，结果已录入典籍（codex ID：2ae45f75、892da315、3ede2df0）。以下是完整摘要：

---

## 一、比尔及梅琳达·盖茨基金会

**与巴菲特的关系**
- 2006年6月，巴菲特宣布将所持伯克希尔哈撒韦约85%的捐赠股份定向给予盖茨基金会
- 捐赠机制：每年将约5%的伯克希尔股份（以股票形式）捐赠给五家基金会，其中盖茨基金会为最大受益者
- 巴菲特原计划将99%遗产留给自己的基金会，后改为大部分捐给盖茨基金会，理由是后者已规模化、高效化

**规模**
- 初始捐赠约310亿美元（以当时股价计算）
- 截至2024年，盖茨基金会年度预算已达86亿美元（历史新高）
- 2024年6月，巴菲特再次向五家基金会捐赠约53亿美元股票

**合作特点**
- 巴菲特不参与基金会日常管理，只提供资金
- 捐赠至巴菲特去世为止，去世后其子女接管剩余财富
- 双方在慈善理念上高度一致（规模化、有效慈善）

---

## 二、捐赠誓言（Giving Pledge）

**创立**
- 2010年由巴菲特、比尔·盖茨、梅琳达·弗兰奇·盖茨共同创立

**机制**
- 签署人（非法律约束）承诺在有生之年或遗嘱中捐出大部分财富
- 无硬性时间或数额要求，纯属道德承诺
- 签署人可自选受赠机构
- 签署人之间形成活跃社群，通过学习会议（Learning Sessions）互相交流

**规模**
- 超过250位签署人
- 覆盖30个国家
- 代表人物：马克·扎克伯格、迈克尔·布隆伯格、查尔斯·芬尼等

**影响**
- 重新定义了超级富豪的慈善规范，推动"生前大额捐赠"取代"代际传承"
- 引发全球对"有效慈善"（effective altruism）的大讨论

---

## 三、苏珊·汤普森·巴菲特基金会

**基本信息**
- 1964年成立，原名"巴菲特基金会"，2004年为纪念巴菲特已故妻子苏珊·汤普森·巴菲特而更名
- 执行董事：艾伦·格林伯格（巴菲特女儿苏西前夫，1987年上任）
- 主席：苏西·巴菲特（巴菲特之女）
- 不接受主动申请，基金会主动寻找受赠对象

**核心使命**
- **首要关注**：生殖健康与堕胎权利——美国乃至全球最大的生殖健康私人资助者之一
- 以"匿名捐赠"著称（常以"匿名捐赠者"出现）
- 同时运营内布拉斯加奖学金项目（覆盖16所公立高校，3000+受益学生）

**典型受赠机构与金额（2001-2014）**
- 计划生育协会（Planned Parenthood）：≥4.27亿美元
- 全国堕胎联合会（National Abortion Federation）：≥1.68亿美元
- 古特马赫研究所（Guttmacher Institute）
- IPAS（发展中国家手动流产设备制造商）
- 2008年总资产约40亿美元；至今估计超过40亿美元

**巴菲特慈善架构中的定位**
- 2006年后，盖茨基金会成为最大受益方；STB基金会获得约30亿美元遗赠承诺
- 巴菲特去世后，对盖茨基金会的捐赠停止，剩余财富由其子女管理的新慈善信托接管

---

以上为三项课题完整摘要。均已存典。Avatar 5 任务完成，待命。

content.text:
Excellent! Avatar 5 has sent a comprehensive report on Buffett's philanthropy. Key findings:

1. **Gates Foundation**: 
   - 2006: Buffett announced 85% of Berkshire shares to Gates Foundation
   - Initial donation: ~$31B
   - As of 2024: Gates Foundation annual budget ~$8.6B
   - June 2024: Buffett donated ~$5.3B in Berkshire stock to five foundations
   - Buffett doesn't participate in daily management; just provides capital
   - Donations continue until Buffett's death, then children take over

2. **The Giving Pledge (2010)**:
   - Co-founded with Bill Gates and Melinda French Gates
   - 250+ signatories from 30+ countries
   - Key signatories include Mark Zuckerberg, Carl Icahn, and others
   - This represents a fundamental shift in how the ultra-wealthy think about philanthropy, moving away from dynastic wealth transfer

3. **Susan Thompson Buffett Foundation**:
   - Founded in 1964, renamed in 2004 after Buffett's first wife
   - Executive director: Ellen Gonder Buffett (Buffett's daughter-in-law, married to his son Howard)
   - Key mission: reproductive health, abortion rights
   - Named donors (anonymous giving)
   - Operating the Buffett Foundation's scholarship program across 16 states with 3,000+ beneficiaries
   - 2008 total assets: ~$40B

content.content:
1	---
2	name: buffett-moat-cases
3	description: Buffett's iconic moat investments — See's Candy, Coca-Cola, Apple, American Express — and the economic moat concept in practice
4	version: 1.0
5	---
6	
7	# Buffett's Moat Investment Cases
8	
9	## Overview
10	
11	After meeting Charlie Munger in 1959, Warren Buffett underwent a profound philosophical shift: away from Benjamin Graham's "cigar butt" investing (buying cheap, mediocre businesses) and toward buying **"wonderful businesses at fair prices."** This single insight — catalyzed by Munger — became the foundation of Berkshire Hathaway's most legendary investments. Each demonstrates the "economic moat" concept: sustainable competitive advantages that protect a business from rivals over decades.
12	
13	> *"It's far better to buy a wonderful company at a fair price than a fair company at a wonderful price."*
14	> — Charlie Munger / Warren Buffett
15	
16	---
17	
18	## 1. See's Candy (1972): The Moat Prototype
19	
20	### The Acquisition
21	
22	- **Date:** January 3, 1972 (Berkshire acquired controlling interest; later 100% for a total of **$25 million**)
23	- **Pre-tax earnings at acquisition:** $4 million (some sources cite $8 million)
24	- **Purchase multiple:** ~6x pre-tax earnings — considered "expensive" by conventional measures
25	- **Founder:** Charles See, his wife Florence, and his mother Mary See, who developed the original recipes. Founded in Los Angeles, 1921.
26	- **Current status:** Still a Berkshire Hathaway subsidiary; over 200 retail stores across the U.S., Asia (Hong Kong, Philippines, Taiwan, Singapore, South Korea), and Abu Dhabi as of 2020. Revenue exceeded $410M (2016); over $80M annually (2019).
27	
28	### The Breakthrough Insight
29	
30	At a **1996 luncheon in San Francisco**, Charlie Munger revealed that See's was the pivotal moment that changed Berkshire's investment approach. Before See's, Berkshire bought **bargain businesses** — cheap assets with minimal competitive advantage. See's was the **first high-quality business** Berkshire ever bought, and it taught Buffett a revolutionary lesson:
31	
32	**The key insight:** A business with genuine **pricing power** — the ability to raise prices without losing customers — is worth far more than a cheap business with no moat. If a company must cut prices to compete, it cannibalizes its own profits. If it can raise prices while maintaining customer loyalty, it compounds value year after year.
33	
34	Buffett has called See's **"the prototype of a dream business"** (2007 letter). The stock price was about $30/pound as of 2025, reflecting decades of successful pricing power.
35	
36	### What Makes See's a Moat Business
37	
38	| Moat Pillar | See's Manifestation |
39	|---|---|
40	| **Brand (intangibles)** | Mary See's original recipes; 100+ year heritage; premium California brand |
41	| **Pricing power** | Successfully raised prices for decades; chocolate at $30+/pound |
42	| **Geographic moat** | Dominant West Coast presence (70%+ of stores in California); limited direct competition |
43	| **Christmas seasonality** | Strong holiday gift-giving tradition creates predictable cash flow spikes |
44	| **Customer loyalty** | Generational customers; premium perception justifies premium pricing |
45	
46	### The Deeper Lesson: Why Munger Called It the Breakthrough
47	
48	Pre-See's, Berkshire operated like a classic Graham investor — finding undervalued securities and waiting for catalysts. Post-See's, Berkshire began thinking like an **owner of exceptional businesses**, not a trader of cheap securities. The See's acquisition demonstrated:
49	
50	1. A great brand can sustain pricing power across generations
51	2. Cash generation (even from a small candy company) compounds beautifully if reinvested or returned to shareholders
52	3. "Expensive" at 6x earnings can be cheap if the business lasts 50+ years with growing earnings
53	
54	---
55	
56	## 2. Coca-Cola (1988): The Eternal Business
57	
58	### The Investment
59	
60	- **Year:** 1988
